Application: Project Name: City of Shafter Unserved Broadband Access Project
Company Applicant Name: City of Shafter
Contact Position: Assistant IT Manager, GIS & Fiber Infrastructure Team Lead
Project Title: City of Shafter Unserved Broadband Access Project
Description of the major infrastructure: Throughout the City of Shafter and in small unincorporated areas surrounding the City, we will deploy approximately 21 miles of new fiber optic cable using GPON technology. GPON enables the network to scale from 1 gigabit symmetrical to 10+ gigabits as bandwidth needs increase and device technology advances. We will use aerial installation for 79% of the new fiber and the remaining 21% of new fiber will be deployed underground. The network will employ a ring topology between hubs to ensure the highest resiliency for customers. In addition, the network will utilize at least two backhaul paths for routing redundancy. We will use three existing fiber huts for this project, requiring no new remote terminals or fiber huts to be built.    As a municipality that does not offer internet service directly to our residents, we are seeking an ISP partner for our project area. As part of our Broadband Master Plan, our RFP will open 3 rd quarter 2024, and we plan for deployment in 4 th quarter 2024. The ISP partner we identify will enter into an IRU with the City to use City-owned infrastructure.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 79% of our project will be aerial deployment and 21% will be underground deployment. Of the roughly 21 total miles of fiber that will be deployed through this project, we estimate that 9.8 aerial miles and 4.4 underground miles will be within the city limits and 6.4 aerial miles will be in unincorporated Kern County.
Major Equipment Expenses: Our project will utilize existing, city-owned hubs. Through our construction contractor, we will procure aggregation routers and BNG (Calix ASM or Nokia 7750-SR7), access switches, GPON OLT's (20xE9-2 with GPON-8 cards @ 1:32 = 2048 subs), and residential (803G) and commercial (716GE-I) GPON ONTs. We have estimated the expense of all major equipment at standard market value. We anticipate our construction contractor will provide tools needed for fiber and network deployment, like splicing equipment, testers, power meters, and other diagnostic tools.

Two months is the standard time it takes to complete the RFP cycle for the City of Shafter.    Once we have a construction partner, we will begin the permitting process and expect to file permits for Phase 1 construction in September 2024, for Phase 2 construction in December 2024, and for Phase 3 construction in January 2025.    As we work through the permitting process and receive permits, we will begin construction material procurement and actual construction with the following milestones:   September 2024 ? DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The City of Shafter is requesting $6,910,394.00 from the Last Mile Federal Funding Account to extend the city-owned fiber network with an additional estimated 21 miles of ?future proof? GPON fiber optic cable to ensure last mile connectivity to an additional estimated 182 unserved locations in our project area. While we will interconnect with the California State Middle Mile Network, we will not build any middle mile infrastructure or facilities with funds for this project. Our project will leverage the existing network, including three city-owned fiber hubs to ensure great project cost efficiency. Upon deployment, this project will ensure connected locations have access to 1 gbps symmetric internet speed. The entirety of our project area is previously disturbed and meets the requirements of CEQA review exemption as a linear broadband deployment under SB156. Additionally, our project does not include any native and tribal lands that would supersede the exemption.    From the Broadband Master Planning effort, a high-level network design was developed (see Figure 5 in uploaded business plan file). The plan was designed to be constructed in three phases. The phasing approach maximizes efforts to capture the highest numbers of unserved customers and potential revenues early on and helps to reduce impact on city staff and the network builder so that the network build can be completed in manageable sized pieces. Concentrating on its city core region first, Phase 1 captures most of the downtown core of Shafter which is where the highest concentration of businesses are located. Including some City of Shafter areas, Phase 2 predominantly expands into the unincorporated community areas to the south of Shafter that has some of the greatest need for improved broadband services. Phase 3 expands into areas of the city that are currently more rural in nature, but where future growth is anticipated, including commercial, industrial, and residential development.      As a municipality that does not offer internet service directly to our residents, we are seeking an ISP partner for our project area. As part of our Broadband Master Plan, our RFP will open 3 rd quarter 2024, and we plan for deployment in 4 th quarter 2024. We will work with our ISP partner to identify where we could leverage their existing infrastructure for this project, further reducing project costs and time for deployment.

Company Applicant Name: Siskiyou Telephone Company
Contact Position: CEO
Description of the major infrastructure:
Siskiyou Telephone Company (STC) will be deploying This a fully fiber to the home project. As the middle mile portion of the project area will be built in collaboration with the California Department of Technology Middle Mile Network, this project will only involve final mile connections and is only possible due to the middle mile project.  
 We have chosen to do a fully underground project. Given the history of wildfires in Siskiyou County, and the high likelihood of these continuing, the best way to protect this infrastructure investment is to bury all the fiber-optic cable. STC is also in a strategic position to deploy underground given our existing assets. As the incumbent provider, we have in-ground conduit that delivers copper to many of the premises this project will target. This means we will easily be able to lay fiber on-top of the existing copper in the conduit which already reaches these homes.  
 STC will by burying both new conduit and new fiber for the locations that do not have existing assets in place.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: This will be a fully underground project. We will utilize 341,226 feet of fiber to connect each of the premises in the project area.
Major Equipment Expenses:
Siskiyou County is one of, if not the , most expensive places to build in the State of California. Our cost per passing is considerably higher than many other areas, even with the cost savings we have through using exisiting conduit. 
 Our engineering and construction partners have identified the major equipment expenses. Fiber: $1,192,433 Conduit: $384,735 Handholes: $130,075 Pedestals: $48,250 Customer Premise Equipment: $107,100.00 Network Equipment (in field and CO): $339,940.00
Estimated project plan with major milest:
STC has confidence in our project plan due to having (4) sections of this project with staking sheets completed and ready to submit for permitting. In total, 265,906 feet of the project already has low-level engineering complete. The remaining 75,320 feet of engineering will be completed once funding is made available. Having more than 75% of the project engineered will enable us to move forward with permitting and construction immediately once awarded money. This will allow us to focus on those (4) areas first to get material ordered and construction lined out to begin as soon as possible. On top of that, approximately 77% of the project will involve pulling new fiber through existing conduit that STC has already placed with their copper plant. This foresight will allow us to quickly and efficiently get fiber to these homes without having to deal with permits and boring new conduit for the entire project. While that initial construction work is taking place, STC will partner with their engineering firm to create staking sheets for the rest of the project. This will assure that STC can complete this project in the allotted time. 
 Low Level Engineering - Jan 2024 - April 2024 Material Procurement (ordering & management) - April 2024 - Sept 2024 Permitting (CalTrans, Yreka, Siskiyou County)- March 2024 - May 2024 Construction - June 2024 - Nov 2024 Winter months construction pause Construction - March 2025 - June 2025
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Siskiyou Telephone Company has been faithfully serving residents in Siskiyou County since 1896. As the ILEC, STC has provided phone service, DSL service, and is in the process of expanding fiber to every household we serve. This commitment requires state & federal funding, along with private capital. This project will continue STC down the path of a fully fiber territory.  
 On July 28, 2023, CDT finalized an agreement with STC to construct 163 miles of high-speed, 288-fiber-count middle mile network and install the necessary infrastructure as part of the Middle-Mile Broadband Initiative. As this work will be completed with CDT, STC is not including any middle mile in this application, though we acknowledge the State?s open access requirements. 
 This project was created to serve the most amount of homes (304) within the given construction timeframe and line up with STC?s construction of the state Middle Mile Network (MMN). As the incumbent and only terrestrial provider, we have thorough documentation of each premises in this area that we serve with copper & have identified 305 premises in the project area that are currently fed with only copper - thus unserved. This is a difference of 79 locations from what the CPUC map has identified. We have calculated this project based on the 304 number to ensure we serve each eligible location.  
 Targeted service locations begin along Route 3 northeast of Fort Jones (between Soap Creek Road & McAdams Creek Rd) & then north up McAdams Creek Rd. The project will pick up another 4 eligible locations on the northern outskirts of Fort Jones. It then travels south over Fort Jones. The project picks up again at Eastside Rd & continues down Route 3 until reaching Quartz Valley Rd, at the interstate exit for Greenview. STC will continue using the MMN path south down Route 3 until Eller Ln, where we will construct laterals to eligible homes. The last leg of the project will go through Greenview & up along Oro Fino to Lighthill Rd that loops back to Route 3 between Fort Jones and Greenview.  
 We do not anticipate any CEQA review of the project as all work is in previously disturbed ground. Indeed, much of the project will involve putting fiber into existing underground conduit. In addition, we believe this project will fall under the CEQA exemption of Section 15301/Class 1.  
 This project is a step towards digital inclusivity which is essential to foster social and economic growth in underserved communities like ours.

Application: Project Name: Digital Equity for Avalon, Catalina Island, and Surrounding Waterways
Company Applicant Name: AVX Networks
Contact Position: CEO
Project Title: Digital Equity for Avalon, Catalina Island, and Surrounding Waterways
Description of the major infrastructure:
Major infrastructure includes a fiber optic exchange/colocation location, fiber to the home and fiber to the premises, diverse fiber optic middle mile routes. 
 The distribution outside plant (OSP) will be entirely fiber optic with 48+ fiber count fiber from the beach landing site to the Avalon Central office. Additionally, a 24 strand system will be deployed throughout Avalon to support both passive XGS-PON services, as well as lit ethernet services including dark fiber, wavelength services, and carrier grade (MEF compliant) active ethernet. 
 A fiber backbone will be deployed across the spine of the island with branches to the populated areas at Middle Ranch, Two Harbors, as well as spurs to the coves which house year round residents.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
Approximately 30 miles of buried, armored subsea cable from each landing station on the island to diverse beach landing station on the mainland. Primary and backup locations have been identified and studied on both ends. 
 At AVX and DEFs suggestion, the city of Avalon has passed a resolution to accelerate permitting on the island side, and AVX has identified multiple existing, unused bores that may substantially expedite the installation on the mainland. 
 The majority of the network on the island will be aerial fiber on existing utility poles. The total of this fiber build is approximately 75 miles, including Avalon, the inhabited areas of the interior, existing tower locations, and potential sites for mobile network deployments to enhance emergency coverage. 
 On the mainland the majority of the network will be underground using existing ducting whever possible.
Major Equipment Expenses:
The major capital equipment expenses are as follows: 
 Routing and Switching Hardware UPS Equipment Generation equipment (outside of CO) Microwave during project turn up and to provided connectivity to 45-55 workers housed on offshore oil platforms year round SIP Switching Equipment to support VoIP and E911 intra island and off island
Estimated project plan with major milest: A fully vetted project plan with major milestones is included in the file attachments.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The primary technologies to be used are: 
 
 Passive/un-repeated subsea fiber optic cable from the mainland to the island. The fiber will be terminated at diverse landing station at each end, through industry standard directional bores, terminating in an on-shore vault or ?beach manhole?. The mainland side of the network will connect to via diverse fiber routes to one or more internet data centers in a highly redundant fashion. Excess fiber stands will be available as spares or future use. The AVX link from the mainland will be lit at 400Gbps per fiber pair (800 Gbps total) to begin with. With DWDM and other technologies this bandwidth can be readily increased to multiple terabits per pair. AVX is ready to accept and provide open-access on the middle mile portions of this network and looks forward to increasing overall connectivity to the island and enabling broader consumer choices, combined with truly available bandwidth at the speeds published. AVX will offer a variety of dedicated internet, Ethernet services, as well as wavelength and dark fiber upon bonafide request.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The Yurok Telecommunications Corporation (100% wholly-owned Yurok Tribe corporation) is proposing to build a 116-mile XGSPON FTTH project in Del Norte and Humboldt County. The proposed project area is all within the Yurok tribal territory. The project includes aerial on existing poles and underground within existing rights of way. Description of the major infrastructure:
Ranch WIFI, LLC. has partnered with California State Association of Counties Financial Corporation (CSACFC) to build a fiber-to-the-premise (FTTP) infrastructure to provide Internet and voice services to unserved areas in Northwest San Luis Opisbo county. The project area includes 2 noncontiguous service areas designated as Oak Shores and Heritage Ranch. Ranch WIFI presently serves 100% of the households via fixed wireless in the 2 areas identified by this grant. Given broadband and subscribers/sector limitations, FTTP is needed to meet current and future broadband demands. The FTTP infrastructure will offer a low-cost plan and receive redundant 10-40 GB of Direct Internet Access (DIA) connections from Cogent and Hurricane Electric ISPs. Ranch WIFI is committed to supporting Telehealth, remote learning, California/Federal Lifeline services from a highspeed reliable FTTP infrastructure. 
 This project will bury 37 miles (28 middle-mile, 9 last-mile) of fiber cable to provide minimum of 100 Mbps symmetrical to 725 unserved locations. The 28 middle-mile fiber route (no IRU used) will provide interconnections for nondiscriminatory open access and meet the phase 1 statewide open-access middle-mile network. Each area will require a climate-controlled shelter with DC power plant, FDUs, and Optical Line Terminals (OLT). Power sources will be centered upon clean/green-energy (solar) with power inverters and backup systems utilizing a three-tier design.  
 Given rocky terrain, fiber construction will consist of plowing, boring, and rock boring. Each route has been evaluated to determine what percent will be bored or require rock boring. Palmetto Engineering, local contractors and other contractors experienced with constructing fiber in California were consulted to calculate price/ft construction costs. Three optical line terminals (OLT) will be equipped with Calix E-7 XG801 XGS-PON cards connecting 725 optical network terminals (ONT).    Palmetto Engineering will provide engineering services for this project that includes grant preparation, FTTP infrastructure design, budgeting, Outside Plant (OSP), Inside Plant (ISP), inspection, and PE design certification. DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Ranch WIFI, LLC. (FRN: 0021286844) proposes to build a Fiber-To-The-Premise (FTTP) network to extend broadband services to 725 locations. The FTTP network infrastructure will deploy XGS-PON/G-PON technology to deliver broadband speeds ranging from 100/100 Mbps to 10/10 GB for unserved communities of North San Luis Obispo County. GPON/XGS-PON was designed as a ?future-proof? modular solution that is scalable and protects carrier investments by preventing equipment obsolescence. This platform supports triple-play services flexible traffic management; Ethernet flow mapping, prioritization, tagging; and versatile management options which are required to respond to the demand for premium services. 
 The project proposes to bury 9 miles of last mile fiber and 28 miles of middle-mile fiber that will connect to California's statewide open-access phase 1 middle-mile route. The "proposed middle-mile infrastructure is needed to achieve the last-mile connections" (page A-9). For post trenching restoration, backfilling is the beginning process of restoring an open trench for utility installation. Backfilling is the process of putting the soil back into a trench or foundation once excavation and related work is complete. Crews must be mindful of impact loading of any other buried utilities in the vicinity when placing backfill. Compaction is the completing step and is completed using mechanical means, such as a compactor, an excavator or a jumping jack style compactor. Soils should be compacted to at least the minimum percentages of dry density as determined by ASTM D698 Method A. No environmentally sensitive areas were found after searching through CalEnviroScreen and EPAs EnviroAtlas. 
 Ranch WIFI has reviewed and will comply with rules and program guideline set forth by SB 156 as they related to eligible areas, funding criteria, eligible entities, middle mile funding, and open access. If needed, Ranch WIFI will utilize existing fixed wireless backhauls capable of 10/10GB to provide broadband to new FTTP locations until interconnections to middle mile are complete. The project area includes 2 noncontiguous service areas designated as Oak Shores (589 locations) and Heritage Ranch (136 locations). The Last-Mile FTTP & Middle-Mile project has an estimated total project cost of $16,970,263. Because of boring and rock boring distances and costs in California, the maximum cost per location exceeds $9,300 and therefore does not meet all criteria under Ministerial Process.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Ranch WIFI, LLC. (FRN: 0021286844) proposes to build a Fiber-To-The-Premise (FTTP) network to extend broadband services to 1,420 locations. The FTTP network infrastructure will deploy XGS-PON & G-PON technology to deliver broadband speeds ranging from 100 Mbps symmetrical up to 10/10 GB to unserved communities of Fresno County in central California. GPON/XGS-PON was designed as a ?future-proof? solution that supports advances of end user technology without upgrading the entire network infrastructure. Its modular architecture is scalable and protects carrier investments by preventing equipment obsolescence. This platform supports triple-play services flexible traffic management; Ethernet flow mapping, prioritization, tagging; and versatile management options which are required to respond to the demand for premium services. 
 The project proposes to bury 40 miles of last mile fiber (Coalinga 27, Huron 13) that will connect to California's statewide open-access phase 1 middle mile fiber routes. This route runs through both service areas and will meet new last mile infrastructure from a new shelter in each area. For post trenching restoration, backfilling is the beginning process of restoring an open trench for utility installation. Backfilling is the process of putting the soil back into a trench or foundation once excavation and related work is complete. Crews must be mindful of impact loading of any other buried utilities in the vicinity when placing backfill. Compaction is the completing step and is completed using mechanical means, such as a compactor, an excavator or a jumping jack style compactor. Soils should be compacted to at least the minimum percentages of dry density as determined by ASTM D698 Method A. No environmentally sensitive areas were found after searching through CalEnviroScreen and EPAs EnviroAtlas. 
 Ranch WIFI has reviewed and will comply with rules and program guideline set forth by SB 156 as they relate to eligible areas, funding criteria, eligible entities, middle mile funding, and open access. If needed, Ranch WIFI will utilize existing fixed wireless backhauls capable of 10/10GB to provide broadband to new FTTP locations until interconnections to middle mile are complete. The project area includes 2 noncontiguous service areas designated as Coalinga (468 locations) and Huron (952 locations). The Last-Mile FTTP project has an estimated total cost of $13,306,767 and meets all criteria listed under Ministerial Process.

Company Applicant Name: unWired Broadband, LLC
Contact Position: CEO
Description of the major infrastructure: The proposed project is an FTTP XGS-PON network that would reach all serviceable locations in Gustine, CA. The major infrastructure that will be deployed includes: 175,179FT of fiber optic cable (sizes will vary from 48 count to 288 count); 1.25" and 4" SDR13.5 HDPE conduit; 21"x16"x12" medium handholes; 24"x36"x24" large handholes; 9"x12" round handholes; and 35.11"x18.5"x16.98" pedestal mounted fiber optic splitters.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 100% of the proposed project?s 175,179FT of fiber optic cable will be underground installation.
Major Equipment Expenses: As further detailed in the project cost estimates, the major equipment expenses include fiber optic cable, conduit, aerial strand, splitters, splice closures, handholes, optical line terminals (OLT) and switching equipment, points of presence (PoP), drop fiber, optical network terminals (ONT), and outdoor NIDs.
Estimated project plan with major milest: unWired Broadband, UWB, has already commenced preliminary work on the project, including extensive consultations with the City of Gustine and other project stakeholders, completion of preliminary engineering and design work, and commencement of permit applications. We anticipate the final engineering and design work will be completed and all permits required to start construction will be approved within 2-3 months of the FFA grant award. The construction timeline is estimated to be 12 months, including construction start-up (material purchases, scheduling, etc.) for a total project schedule of 14 months. Major project milestones include grant award / project kickoff; engineering and permitting completion / construction start; first progress report / 10% complete; second progress report / 35%; third progress report / 60% complete; fourth progress report / 85% complete; and construction complete / completion report approved.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The proposed project for which FFA funding is being requested is an FTTP XGS-PON network that would reach all serviceable locations in Gustine, CA. UWB has been working with the City of Gustine to develop the preliminary plans for the project, including the proposed use of Congressionally Designated USDA ReConnect funds to cover $1.3M in project costs for network operations equipment, project materials, and professional services. 
 The project will place fiber optic cables throughout every right-of-way in the city to service every anchor institution, single family home, and commercial address, and will leave a fiber coil to service every multi-dwelling unit. We will offer plans with 250/250 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, and 1,000/1,000 Mbps symmetrical broadband service. Plans will also include 99.99% network reliability and no data caps. 
 The project construction plan includes 175,179FT of underground fiber optic cable and 1.25" conduit to service all addresses in the city. We will be placing our buried broadband infrastructure in the right-of-way with other existing utilities (water, sewer, gas, power, ILEC telephone, and CATV) throughout the city limits. All project infrastructure will be built under/along city streets, and there will be no placement in new right-of-way or previous undisturbed ground. The proposed project may include some minor trenching. At locations where ground is disturbed by trenching or other construction activities, site restoration will include replacing and compacting soil in the disturbed area. The proposed project area does not contain any environmentally sensitive areas, and our preliminary environmental consultations have indicated that a case can be made for a Categorical Exemption (CE) from further CEQA review per 2023 CEQA guidelines sections 15302 and 15303. 
 The proposed project would leverage UWB?s existing core, middle mile, and backhaul networks. The project would also leverage the statewide Middle Mile Network, which will pass through our proposed project area. The proposed project does not include any middle mile infrastructure, as our existing upstream connection and anticipated connection to the statewide Middle Mile Network are more than sufficient to meet the capacity requirements of the proposed project, with room for future expansions and upgrades. UWB accepts the State's SB 156 open access requirements for any Middle Mile facilities in the proposed project area.

Company Applicant Name: Hankins Information Technology Inc.
Contact Position: CEO
Description of the major infrastructure: The proposed project is an extension of HIT?s current FTTP XGS-PON build in San Benito County, in and around Hollister, San Juan, and Aromas, CA. The major infrastructure to be deployed includes mostly underground fiber optic cable to unserved, low-income communities primarily in rural environments. Additional project infrastructure includes conduit, handholes, fiber marker posts, splice enclosures, and fiber huts (PoPs).
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The proposed project includes 649,206 ft of fiber installation, approximately 90% (or 585k ft) of which will be underground installation and 10% (or 65k ft) of which will be aerial installation.
Major Equipment Expenses:
As further detailed in the project cost estimates, our major equipment expenses include: conduit; high count fiber; tracer wire; handholes; splice enclosures; fiber marker posts; optical line terminals (OLT) and switching equipment; points of presence (PoP); small count (drop) fiber; optical network terminals (ONT) and outdoor NIDs; drilling equipment; and support vehicles.  
 As also shown in the project cost estimates, HIT is proposing to provide $678,610.60 (6%) in matching funds for the project since the total estimated costs ($11,114,635.44) exceed the FFA allocation for San Benito County ($10,436,024.84) by this amount.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
Upon notification of grant award, we will immediately begin project engineering and applying for permits. We estimate it will take approximately 3-6 months to complete all permitting requirements, as well as the detailed engineering and fiber design work. We expect to be ready to start construction immediately upon final grant award, with an 18-month project construction schedule. The total build is 649,206 ft. Based on our recent and ongoing experience installing fiber in nearby areas, we estimate an overall construction rate of 36,067 ft/month. While this rate refers to total fiber installed, it encompasses all aspects of project construction and grant administration, including ordering/staging materials, scheduling direct and contract labor, coordinating with Caltrans and other relevant authorities, drilling, setting handholes, installing conduit and fiber, splicing, installing splitters, OLTs and PoPs, and installing drop fiber, ONTs and NIDs on customer premises, as well as project monitoring and reporting and other grant compliance activities. 
 Our major project milestones include: (1) grant award / construction start; (3) 10% construction complete; (4) 35% construction complete; (5) 60% construction complete; (6) 85% construction complete; and (7)100% construction complete / completion report approved.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
HIT is currently deploying an FTTP XGS-PON network near the project area, with more than 140k ft of fiber already installed and providing service to about 100 customers. The proposed project for which FFA funding is being requested is an extension of this FTTP network to include as many adjacent and nearby unserved locations as possible, especially in low-income and disadvantaged communities in and around Hollister, San Juan, and Aromas, CA. The proposed project will include mostly (~90%) underground fiber, as well as ~10% aerial fiber due to some challenging terrain in the project area. Throughout the project area, we will deliver standard single mode fiber to each subscriber from an XGS-PON terminated at a PoP location. This fiber will be run to the customer location and connected to an outdoor NID, delivering upload and download speeds up to 10 Gbps, with standard plans offered at speeds of 100/100 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, and 1,000/1,000 Mbps, and no data or usage caps. 
 The proposed project area does not contain any environmentally sensitive areas and would not involve trenching. At locations where ground is disturbed to place handholes, site restoration will include placing and compacting native soil in the disturbed area. Outside plant construction will be small in scale and will occur in existing roadways or other previously developed and disturbed rights-of-way. Thus, HIT expects the project will fall within one or more categorical CEQA exemptions (e.g., Decision 13-07-032). As a CPCN holder, HIT is eligible for full facilities-based construction authority following an expedited 21-day environmental review process. If the project is determined not to be CEQA exempt, HIT would either re-design the project and reapply for CEQA exemption or file an application seeking Commission approval and full CEQA review before commencing any construction activities. 
 The project would leverage HIT?s existing core, middle mile, and backhaul networks, as well as the statewide Middle Mile Network. We may also leverage one or more PoPs from our existing FTTP network. The proposed project does not include any middle mile infrastructure, as our existing upstream connections and planned connection to the statewide Middle Mile Network can manage anticipated network traffic requirements with excess capacity for growth. HIT accepts the State's SB 156 open access requirements for any Middle Mile facilities in the proposed project area.

Company Applicant Name: VARCOMM Broadband, Inc.
Contact Position: Chief Executive Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: VARCOMM Broadband Inc. will install a combination of aerial and buried fiber optic loops and distribution plant throughout the town of Terra Bella, California.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Aerial Fiber 84% Buried Fiber 16%
Major Equipment Expenses: VARCOMM Broadband, Inc.'s major expense will be a bucket truck for installation, maintenance, and repair of aerial fiber optic. The expense for the truck will be $100,000.00
Estimated project plan with major milest: Design & Permit Submission 1/8/24 (4 weeks) Permit Processing and Approval 7/29/24 Town - Mainline Construction 8/5/24 (7 weeks) Town - Drop Construction 9/2/24 (14 weeks) South - Mainline Construction 9/23/24 (28 weeks) Access Equipment Installation 10/21/24 (1 week) South - Drop Construction 1/24/25 (12 weeks) North - Mainline Construction 4/7/25 (17 weeks) North - Drop Construction 6/30/25 (7 weeks) East - Mainline Construction 8/4/25 (14 weeks) East - Drop Construction 10/27/25 (5 weeks) All Mainline Construction Complete 11/24/25 All Drop Construction Complete 12/8/25 (Final Project Closeout)
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: VARCOMM Broadband Inc. ("VBI") proposes to deploy Fiber To The Home( FTTH) throughout the project area of Terra Bella, CA. Within the town boundaries, aerial distribution and local loop will be deployed . VBI has already secured a pole attachment with Southern California Edison to expedite this project. Company Applicant Name: County of Ventura
Contact Position: Chief Information Officer
Description of the major infrastructure:
This 100% publicly-owned project will target the communities of Oxnard, Saticoy, City of Ventura, Port Hueneme, Santa Paula, Somis, Piru, El Rio, Nyeland Acres, Fillmore, Buckhorn, Bardsdale, Rio Lindo, and other outlying unincorporated areas, and has been developed in collaboration with numerous regional stakeholders that have been leaders in broadband for decades. The County has partnered with both the California Department of Technology (CDT) and Astound Broadband for project implementation. There are three critical components of this project? the joint construction of last mile distribution and MMBN routes along Highway 126, construction of a last mile distribution route that extends from the MMBN routes to interconnect on Highway 101, and construction of a last mile distribution route along Hwy 118.  
 Last Mile & Statewide middle mile joint build: The County, along with Astound Broadband, is entering into a joint build agreement with the CDT to concurrently construct a segment of the statewide MMBN along Hwy 126 from Santa Clarita/I-5 to Santa Paula/Hwy 150. This route is also an essential last mile distribution route for the County to provide connectivity to unserved locations within Ventura County, reaching many unincorporated communities which lie outside of heavily populated areas.  
 Highway 118: The County and Astound will extend a last mile distribution fiber route along Hwy 118 west to the community of Somis that interconnects with the statewide MM Network along Hwy 118 at Lagol.  
 Highway 126: Lastly, the County and Astound will extend a last mile distribution route along Highway 126 from the MMBN interconnection at Hwy 150/Santa Paula to the MMBN along U.S. Hwy 101 while also allowing for an interconnection with both the City of San Buenaventura?s and the City of Oxnard?s networks.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: This project will construct 234,862 feet (95.5%) of underground fiber and 10,986 feet of new aerial fiber (4.5%), for a total of 245,848 feet. 163,590 feet of underground fiber will be cable plough along Ventura County Transportation railway (69.7% of all underground construction).   All routes will be publicly-owned - either by Ventura County or the State of California - and completed in partnership with Astound Broadband. Routes between I-5 and Santa Paula will also be in partnership with the California Department of Technology. A detailed breakdown of all routes is as follows:   Ventura County Routes (in partnership with Astound and CDT ? 6 conduits, 3- 288-count cables) - Santa Paula to Rancho Carlos Museum: 103,362 feet of cable plough fiber (along Ventura County Transportation railway) - Rancho Carlos Museum to LA County: 21,416 feet of underground fiber   Los Angeles County Routes (in partnership with Astound and CDT? 6 conduits, 3- 288-count cables) - Ventura County line to I-5 connecting to City of Santa Clarita network: 34,481 feet of underground fiber (through directional boring and/or open trench/excavation).   Hwy 126 to Hwy 101 (in partnership with Astound ? 4 conduits, 2- 288-count cables) - Santa Paula to Saticoy: 42,051 feet of underground fiber - Saticoy Connection to City of San Buenaventura Network: 1,768 feet of underground fiber   Ventura to Oxnard (in partnership with Astound ? 4 conduits, 2- 288-count cables) - Saticoy to Hwy 101: 18,177 feet of cable plough fiber (along Ventura County Transportation railway) - Hwy 101 to Oxnard: 13,607 feet of underground fiber   Somis (in partnership with Astound 2- 288-count cables) - Hwy 118 MMBI @ Lagol to Somis: 10,986 feet of aerial fiber
Major Equipment Expenses: In total, major networking equipment expenses will account for $1,793,160 of the project budget, all of which is requested through this grant application. These assumptions have been calculated based upon known network elements of broadband infrastructure and through Magellan's extensive experience in telecommunications.    These costs are broken down as follows: Edge Routing: $160,000 Core Routing: $250,000 Switching: $22,500 Access Network termination: $360,000 Network Software: $10,000 Network Security and Firewalls: $60,000 Network Management : $150,000 IP Services: $30,000 Spares (including switches and routers): $25,000 ONU/CPE: $257,880 NID enclosure: $36,840 Residential Gateway (RG): $128,940 RG Management: $6,000 Installation Professional Services: $213,500 Customer Billing Platform: $50,000 Subscriber Provisioning: $25,000 Fiber Management and Documentation: $7,500   All final equipment costs and breakdowns are subject to change based on final design engineering, which will be completed by the end of 2023.
Estimated project plan with major milest: FFA grant application: Quarter 4 of 2023 Design engineering: Quarter 4 of 2023 Hight level design: Quarter 4 of 2023 Low level design: Quarter 4 of 2023 Final design: Quarter 4 of 2023 CEQA Review (exemption): Quarter 1 of 2024 Permitting: Quarters 2-3 of 2024 Construction bid: Quarter 2 of 2024 Bid award: Quarter 3 of 2024 Staking/preconstruction: Quarter 3 of 2024 Backbone construction: Quarter 4 of 2024-Quarter 2 of 2025 Splicing and network connection: Quarter 2 of 2025 Grant-funded service drop installation: Quarter 2 of 2025 Network launch: Quarter 3 of 2025
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Ventura County and its cities share the belief that high-speed internet is critical for closing the digital divide for all Californians. Broadband improves quality of life through access to information, services, & resources, remote learning/work, telehealth. As such, Ventura County has developed a formal plan to build the Ventura County Fiber Network, an open-access last mile distribution network to connect unserved locations in the cities, towns, and unincorporated areas of the County. The project will also joint build the State?s planned middle-mile network on Highway 126 from Santa Clarita/I-5 to Santa Paula. 
 This publicly-owned project is a joint build between the County of Ventura and the State of California?s Department of Technology (CDT), utilizing Astound's experience constructing fiber networks, running along Highways 126 and 118 in Ventura County. Last-mile connections will be deployed by Astound, with an anticipated reach to 1,821 unserved locations. Subscribers to this network will have access to broadband symmetrical speeds ranging from 50 Mbps to 1+ Gbps to meet their unique needs. The fiber optic technology has been chosen for its ?future proof" capabilities, able to adapt to the need for capacity as innovation and technologies expand. While we anticipate this project to be categorically exempt from CEQA review, a thorough review will be completed by the County?s Planning Department (local CEQA authority) once design engineering is completed. This project will be constructed within existing rights of way, avoiding environmentally sensitive areas. The exact date of this review will be determined post award once design engineering is complete. 
 This project will also serve as a cornerstone of the Statewide Middle Mile Backbone Network (MMBN). The County of Ventura and Astound have entered into a joint build agreement with CDT to complete sections of the planned MMBN along Highway 126, saving the State significant costs, with the State CDT contributing $4,779,129 in match funding to the project. Funding through this FFA grant will continue the State?s open access fiber routes, establishing last mile connections across the entirety of Ventura County and building redundancy within the statewide network. Without this open access project, the State of California will miss out on the significant advancements in connectivity this project will bring to disadvantaged communities within Ventura County and surrounding areas.

Application: Project Name: Foothills - Broadband Expansion in Placer County
Company Applicant Name: County of Placer
Contact Position: Chief Information Officer
Project Title: Foothills - Broadband Expansion in Placer County
Description of the major infrastructure: This project is an Aerial Fiber Passive Optical Network (PON) covering unserved and underserved areas of the Alta, Applegate., Cape Horn / Magra communities in Placer County. The major infrastructure to be deployed will be approximately 184 miles of aerial strand, approximately 10 - 15 miles of underground conduit and/or microtrenching and approximately 210 miles of fiber cables. The project includes distribution equipment, splice equipment, fiber and premise equipment needed to deliver a range of service options, from a basic 50 Mbps symmetrical service and with overall capacity of up to 2 Gbps symmetrical broadband service for each home passed.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: It is estimated that approximately 90% (184 miles) of the proposed infrastructure will be aerial fiber on leased utility poles and less than 10% (10 - 15 miles) will be newly constructed underground conduits and/or microtrenching.
Major Equipment Expenses:
Where possible, Astound Broadband leverages exiting infrastructure to serve the proposed project locations (including existing buildings, backhaul, power plant, racks and networking equipment, etc.). New equipment and the installation labor required to serve the project locations will include distribution PON chassis, fiber, splicing components, and on-premise optical terminals and network interface devices. Major installation and equipment costs are summarized below for the entire project as follows: 
 Equipment: $4,090,347 (Aerial Strand Material, Aerial Fiber, Anchor Material, Pole Replacements, Pole Installation Materials, Underground 2" conduit, Underground Fiber Materials, FTTH install Kits, Modems, Outdoor Enclosures, Splice Enclosures, Distribution Cards) 
 Installations: $21,530,373 (Aerial Strand Labor, Aerial Fiber Placement Labor, Anchor Labor, Make Ready, Pole Installations Labor, Traffic Control Overhead Placement, Underground 2" Conduit Labor, Place Fiber in 2" Conduits, Traffic Control Underground Placement)
Estimated project plan with major milest: The Project timeline is 18 months from start to finish, with major milestones as follows. Please note that without a definitive start date, the timeline and milestones are defined in relative terms. Major milestones include: Astound Announcement: Month 1 Engineering and Design: Month 2 through Month 8 Purchasing: Month 2 through Month 8 Permitting: Month 6 through Month 10 Construction: Month 11 through Month 18 Marketing: as connections come on line thru Month 18 Address to Billing: as connections come on line thru Month 18 Release to Sales: as connections come on line through Month 18
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The Project is a passive optical fiber network primarily using leased utility poles to reach the project area. The project will deliver high speed Internet services up to 2 Gbps download and upload with unlimited data. 
 The project is categorically exempt from CEQA pursuant to CEQA Guidelines section 15301, 15303(d), and 15304 (f). The project is statutorily exempt under Section 15303 (d) because it involves the extension of broadband infrastructure, which is a limited number of new, small facilities. The project may include work which is exempt under 15301 as the minor alterations of existing facilities. The project may include minor trenching and backfilling with surface restoration, which is exempt under Section 15304 (f). 
 Where possible, Astound Broadband leverages existing infrastructure to serve the proposed project locations (including existing buildings, backhaul, power plant, racks and networking equipment, etc.). No middle mile facilities are being proposed as part of this project. 
 This is a proven model for effective fiber broadband service delivery. Placer County has been engaged in promoting and expanding broadband access for many years (see https://www.placer.gov/7757/broadband). 
 ?The County used an RFP / RFQ process to solicit qualified providers that could build and serve broadband to various communities throughout the County. From the qualified providers list, partners provide informal bids on potential projects to determine which partner to utilize on a project. Astound Broadband has consistently provided the lowest cost per home build option for the County and the service delivery has been exceptional. To date, Astound Broadband has partnered with the County on 11 projects (8 are under construction and 3 are completed). As noted above, over $19 million in projects have been awarded to date. This Last Mile Federal Funding Account grant will allow the County to continue to expand aerial fiber PON (up to 2 Gbps symmetrical) broadband to unserved and underserved areas in the Alta, Cape Horn / Magra, and Applegate communities. 
 Using the application tool, we have identified that this project area contains a combined median income of $74,750 which is well below the 80% threshold ($79,787) of the total Placer County median income which was $99,734 in 2021 dollars (source: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/placercountycalifornia/PST045222).

Company Applicant Name: Karuk Tribe
Contact Position: Chief Information Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: The Karuk Tribe plans to deploy a wholly Tribally owned and operated fiber company along with their current WISP.  Technology used will be 144 strand, mostly underground fiber, with a network capable of 10Gbps and is a redundant network that interconnects with the SB156 Statewide Middle Mile initiative with Goldenstate Net in Orleans for route diversity.  The goal of the project is to make it as future proof as possible by installing a high strand count and an additional conduit; one for an upgrade path or for diversity.  Additionally, future proofing will be considered for the networking components by ensuring more than one location can route and switch internet traffic.  The project will provide, at minimum, 100Mbps/100Mbps service to end users and a maximum of 1GBps.  Roadside cabinets will be strategically placed to support redundant networking of internet traffic.   The Karuk Tribe has decades of experience operating an ISP and the contractors chosen, (EnerTribe) have an accumulated experience of 50+ years in tribally chartered broadband infrastructure projects.  EnerTribe has worked for hundreds of Tribes and helped manage over a half billion in infrastructure projects over the course of the last 15 years.     The Tribe makes the following commitments:  Interconnect with the state-wide middle-mile fiber project by GoldenState Net Offer a low-cost plan at 50/20+ for $40 a month + Provide matching funds totaling $25,570,084.00 Identify additional gap funding to ensure alleviate risk of detrimental impact on tribal membership, it has requested for a waiver Reach all unserved homes in the census block
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: It is expected that roughly 90% will be underground and 10% aerial.
Estimated project plan with major milest: This is a 24-month project anticipated to commence in March/April 2024 upon being awarded. The initial phase involves project planning and preparation, including project kickoff meetings with the tribal council and relevant tribal departments. A legal review of contractor contracts will also be conducted during this phase. EnerTribe will establish the project timeline using project management tools, compile a project contact list and arrange regular project management calls. The planning phase is projected to conclude within two months. The subsequent significant task is outreach to tribal governments, agencies, and providers. Collaboration with the Tribal Council and relevant tribal departments, such as THPO, Land Management, and Realty, as needed, is crucial. Outreach efforts will commence at the project's outset and continue throughout, concluding before or during the construction phase.  Following this is the permitting process, which initiates with a thorough assessment and early, ongoing engagement. Securing federal, state, and county support and approval is vital. The primary focus of the permitting endeavor will be to complete the project description for NEPA and CEQA review processes. Permitting may span up to 18 months.  Fiber engineering activities will commence early in the project's lifecycle, encompassing site surveys and engineering, a process lasting approximately 6 months. Equipment procurement will follow upon the completion of fiber engineering. This stage involves creating a Bill of Materials (BOM), obtaining quotes, obtaining tribal council approval for purchases, placing orders, and considering lead times for delivery, estimated at 8 months in the project schedule. Fiber construction is allocated a 5-month timeline, hopefully to be finalized before winter. Post-fiber construction activities involve testing electronics and conducting speed tests to ensure operational efficiency, a phase scheduled for 2 months. Finally, the creation of an as-built record of the entire FTTH project will be established for future reference. The compilation of the as-built record occurs at different stages of the project to ensure comprehensive data capture.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The project will make use of the latest technology for FTTH deployments and in an effort to meet the needs of tomorrow, will deploy an additional conduit as an upgrade path. While the current plan is to build a 10GB network, it is clear the project will need to account for 100GB in the near future.   The project will deploy 144 count fiber or greater and will provide customers broadband speeds of as much as 100Mbps upload and 100Mbps download. GPON technology and optical splitting will ensure fiber management is primary and all of the residences are served. This project proposes to install 90% of the fiber under ground and the remaining fiber on existing poles, mainly across the Klamath River. Trenching and directional drilling are the proposed underground methods. Trenching can be conducted with a specialized trenching machine that can cut a trench 6 to 12 inches in width and 36 inches deep. Trenching occurs in ditches and existing rights of way to avoid sensitive areas. These ditches may be full of vegetation and rocks and must first be cleaned out. This is the only vegetation removal needed for underground installation.  No mature trees will be removed, though seedlings established in and blocking the drainage ditch may be removed. Aggregate base and/or native soil will be backfilled to restore the disturbance. Compaction rates must be met in order to avoid shoulder or base failure. Roadside ditches are intended to function as drainage, and vegetation is routinely removed to facilitate drainage. Therefore, no vegetation will be re-introduced. 
 Directional drilling is used to cross underneath a road at a shallow angle to avoid cutting the pavement. It is also used to avoid impacts on infrastructure such as culverts, water lines, and sensitive resources such as old-growth tree roots and archaeological sites.  Aerial Fiber will be installed after poles have been inspeceted and engineered to pass teh additional loading.  These fiber runs will be installed according to state, local and Industry best practice standards.  Any and all construction methods proposed will comply with Permitting requirements from state, local, tribal and NEPA/CEQA as needed. This project will interconnect with the State-wide SB-156 middle-mile initiative along hwy 96 and, in a similar fashion offer open-access services to local providers. There are existing building facilities and telecommunications huts in this region that will be used for housing Networking equipment.

Application: Project Name: Foothills East - Broadband Expansion for Placer County
Company Applicant Name: County of Placer
Contact Position: Chief Information Officer
Project Title: Foothills East - Broadband Expansion for Placer County
Description of the major infrastructure:
This project is an Aerial Fiber Passive Optical Network (PON) covering unserved and underserved areas of the Foothills East Project Area in the Foresthill community of Placer County. The major infrastructure to be deployed will be approximately 80 miles of aerial strand and fiber cables. The project includes distribution equipment, splice equipment, fiber and premise equipment needed to deliver a range of service options, from a basic 50 Mbps symmetrical service and with overall capacity of up to 2 Gbps symmetrical broadband service for each home passed. 
 Note: This project is based on using existing pole lines and ROW shown in the joint use program by the pole owners. If applications are denied for pole attachments, this could jepordize and stop the whole project.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: It is estimated that all of the proposed infrastructure will be aerial fiber on leased utility poles.
Major Equipment Expenses:
Where possible, Astound Broadband leverages exiting infrastructure to serve the proposed project locations (including existing buildings, backhaul, power plant, racks and networking equipment, etc.). New equipment and the installation labor required to serve the project locations will include distribution PON chassis, fiber, splicing components, and on-premise optical terminals and network interface devices. Major installation and equipment costs are summarized below for the entire project as follows: 
 Equipment: $1,448,298 (Aerial Strand Material, Aerial Fiber, Anchor Material, Pole Replacements, Pole Installation Materials, Underground 2" conduit, Underground Fiber Materials, FTTH install Kits, Modems, Outdoor Enclosures, Splice Enclosures, Distribution Cards) 
 Installations: $6,115,618 (Aerial Strand Labor, Aerial Fiber Placement Labor, Anchor Labor, Make Ready, Pole Installations Labor, Traffic Control Overhead Placement, Underground 2" Conduit Labor, Place Fiber in 2" Conduits, Traffic Control Underground Placement)
Estimated project plan with major milest: The Project timeline is 18 months from start to finish, with major milestones as follows. Please note that without a definitive start date, the timeline and milestones are defined in relative terms. Major milestones include: Astound Announcement: Month 1 Engineering and Design: Month 2 through Month 8 Purchasing: Month 2 through Month 8 Permitting: Month 6 through Month 10 Construction: Month 11 through Month 18 Marketing: as connections come on line thru Month 18 Address to Billing: as connections come on line thru Month 18 Release to Sales: as connections come on line through Month 18
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The Project is a passive optical fiber network primarily using leased utility poles to reach the project area. The project will deliver high speed Internet services up to 2 Gbps download and upload with unlimited data. 
 Where possible, Astound Broadband leverages existing infrastructure to serve the proposed project locations (including existing buildings, backhaul, power plant, racks and networking equipment, etc.). No middle mile facilities are being proposed as part of this project. 
 This is a proven model for effective fiber broadband service delivery. Placer County has been engaged in promoting and expanding broadband access for many years (see  https://www.placer.gov/7757/broadband ). 
 ?The County used an RFP / RFQ process to solicit qualified providers that could build and serve broadband to various communities throughout the County. From the qualified providers list, partners provide informal bids on potential projects to determine which partner to utilize on a project. Astound Broadband has consistently provided the lowest cost per home build option for the County and the service delivery has been exceptional. To date, Astound Broadband has partnered with the County on 11 projects (8 are under construction and 3 are completed). As noted above, over $19 million in projects have been awarded to date. This Last Mile Federal Funding Account grant will allow the County to continue to expand aerial fiber PON (up to 2 Gbps symmetrical) broadband to unserved and underserved areas Foothills East Project (Foresthill community). 
 Using the application tool, we have identified that the median income for this project area is $81,719. The median income for the County of Placer is $99,734 in 2021 dollars, and the 80% threshold is $79,787. While the projectwide median income for this project is $1,932 higher than the 80% threshold we believe that there are many households in the project area that are below the threshold and it seems clear that at least 50% of the units in this project area fall under the 80% threshold ($79,787). 
 ?As we looked at the source data from the CPUC https://broadbandmap.ca.gov/ and https://fibermap.vetro.io/, we discovered that each census block in this project area had an repetitive identical median income values (i.e., $128,667.00, $81,719.00) which seemed unusual and could be an inaccuracy in the base data. (source:  https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/placercountycalifornia/PST045222 ).

Application: Project Name: Oakland Connect - Last Mile Connectivity for Oaklanders
Company Applicant Name: The City of Oakland
Contact Position: Chief Information Officer/Director of Information Technology
Project Title: Oakland Connect - Last Mile Connectivity for Oaklanders
Description of the major infrastructure: The City of Oakland will construct a City-owned hybrid middle mile/last mile network, consisting of 12.85 miles of new middle mile fiber (144-count SMFO cable), 11.75 miles of existing City municipal fiber (144-count) for middle mile, and 9.28 miles of new last mile fiber connections (12-strand SMFO cable) in disadvantaged and unserved locations.  The City?s middle mile ring will include connections to the State?s middle mile network.     The proposed network infrastructure aligns with the CPUC Last Mile program goal to close the digital divide by investing in last mile connections for residents of affordable housing in disadvantaged neighborhoods where the lack of reliable, highspeed broadband access impedes economic mobility, compounding the generational impacts of historical redlining and divestment. The fiber will connect 2,544 un/underserved households located within 27 of Oakland Housing Authority?s (OHA) affordable multi-dwelling units (MDUs) in West Oakland, Downtown, Fruitvale, and East Oakland. This work will include in-building wiring improvements for 1,277 OHA units. The proposed fiber will also provide last-mile connections to 14 community anchor institutions (CAIs), nine public safety buildings, and 302 mass market unserved locations. In addition to the 27 MDUs and 2,544 households served by the project, the route will facilitate future last-mile connections to an additional 12 MDUs operated by non-profit organizations, 4,578 businesses, and could support fixed wireless networks that could serve tens of thousands of additional unserved residents. From the east, the proposed route connects to the City?s existing network at 98th Ave and International Blvd in East Oakland, heading north to Foothill Blvd and west along Foothill Blvd to 23rd Ave in Fruitvale, with a spur to the State middle mile network at Fruitvale Ave. The route serves OHA MDUs and CAIs in East, Fruitvale, and West Oakland.     When the network has been constructed, the City will formally solicit proposals from qualified Internet Service Providers (ISPs) to enter into agreements to lease the City?s network to deliver Internet service to customers in accordance with CPUC requirements for bandwidth speeds, latency, and pricing. The ISP agreements will include ongoing network operations and management.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The proposed fiber build will be 100% underground in previously disturbed rights-of-way throughout the city and 0% aerial.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses for the Oakland Connect fiber build include:   67,700 LF of 144-count SMFO cable in new conduit, 339 fiber access pull boxes, 3 connections to state middle mile network6 community anchor network terminations, 8,000 LF of 12-count SMFO cable in new conduit, requiring building switches (Cisco MS120-8) for connections to:  Two fire stations: Fire Station 18; Fire Station 03  Eastmont Mall Head Start Center  San Antonio Park Head Start Center  Cesar Chavez Branch Library  Melrose Branch Library  8x City Hub/community anchor network termination and switches (Cisco 9300/24)   Five fire stations: Fire Station 20, 1401 98th Ave; Fire Station 23, 7100 Foothill Blvd; Fire Station 13, 1225 Derby St; Fire Station 04, 1235 East 14th St  Eastmont Police Department Sub Station at 2701 73 rd Ave  City of Oakland Emergency Operations Center at 1605 MLK, Jr Way  City of Oakland Data Center at Lionel J. Wilson Building, 150 Frank Ogawa Plaza (Central office) - connected by leased fiber to Digital Realty Data Center (720 2 nd Street, Oakland)  27 Oakland Housing Authority (OHA) multi-dwelling unit (MDU) building fiber branch cable connections/terminations (4 strands used for each building with redundant connections to City hubs), 41,000 LF of 12 SMFO branch cable in new conduit  1,277 OHA unit in-building wiring improvements  OHA MDU Building Switches and in-unit routers will be provided by 3 rd party ISP through a separate RFP (not included in grant application).
Estimated project plan with major milest:
Project Initiation 	 (January 2024 - April 2024 )  Establish Oakland Connect Governance Task Force with representatives from City of Oakland; #OaklandUndivided, Oakland Housing Authority, and relevant community-based groups;  Execute agreement between OHA and City of Oakland to facilitate project;  Project kickoff and coordination with CPUC and external partners.    Network Design and Engineering 	 (February 2024 - May 2024)  Review & document requirements to be satisfied by the materials, designs, and services requisite for completing the fiber buildout (City of Oakland; Kimley-Horn);  Finalize routes and network design (Kimley-Horn);  Prepare Construction Documents (Kimley-Horn);  Confirm governance, ownership, and management strategy (addressing zoning and ROW ordinances, legal ramifications, and structure of leasing and asset sharing agreements) (City of Oakland);  Finalize environmental documents. Confirm CEQA exemption & NEPA Categorical Exclusion (City of Oakland).    Construction 	 (June 2024 ? July 2025)   Publish construction bids pursuant to the City?s public works standard competitive contracting process;  Materials Ordering and Acquisition;  Coordinate construction activities with external partners (including OHA, Caltrans & AC Transit, to the extent necessary);  Make-Ready Engineering/Duct Work;  Fiber Placement & Street/Sidewalk Restoration;  Fiber Splicing and Termination;  Network Equipment Installation and Configuration;  System Testing & Acceptance.  
 Permit Acquisition 	 (June 2024 ? Nov 2024)  Obtain PX permits (city-issued permit) for work in Oakland streets and sidewalks;   Obtain building permits for fiber-cable branch cable terminations and equipment and  in-building wiring improvements;  Obtain Caltrans Encroachment Permit (State ROW locations).     Request for Proposal & Agreements: Internet Service Provider 	 (March 2025 ? July 2025)  Release RFP to solicit proposals from eligible ISP partner to provide last mile connections to 27 un/underserved MDUs with 2,544 units of housing, 9 public safety buildings, and 14 CAI?s throughout Oakland. The RFP will include requirements for speeds, latency, and pricing, in accordance with CPUC Federal Funding Account guidance. When selected, the City will negotiate and enter into formal agreements with one or more service providers to lease the network to deliver Internet service to customers.    Last Mile Connectivity & Maintenance 	 	 August 2025 - Ongoing
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The City of Oakland will construct a city-owned hybrid middle mile/last mile network through West Oakland, Downtown, Fruitvale, and East Oakland, providing fiber connections to:  2,544 unserved households located within 27 of Oakland Housing Authority?s (OHA) affordable housing multi-dwelling units (MDUs);  14 community anchor institutions (CAIs); and  9 public safety buildings.  This network will consist of 12.85 miles of new middle mile fiber (144-count SMFO cable), 11.75 miles of existing City municipal fiber (144-count) for middle mile, and 9.28 miles of new last mile fiber connections (12-strand SMFO cable).    (1) Technology: The network will utilize fiber-optic cables and communications equipment, including multiple SFP optical ports and network ports to allow for future expansion (e.g. connections to locations, community wireless systems, etc.).    (2) Speeds: The City anticipates maximum network speeds of 1 Gbps symmetrical upload/download. The network will initially provide backbone 10G connections to the City?s existing Data Center (150 Frank H. Ogawa Plaza) and Digital Realty Data Center (720 2 nd St) and could be upgraded to higher speeds in the future.    (3) Construction Details for CEQA Review: Fiber will be installed through directional underground boring and short trench sections in existing City and State rights-of-way, with curb-to-curb street restoration performed as required for areas of excavation on affected roadways. The project will be CEQA exempt - see Section 19.  
 (4) Leveraging of Existing Facilities: The route connects into both the city?s existing fiber network and the proposed state middle mile network. The network utilizes the City?s Data Center and Digital Realty Data Center as Network Central Offices and City-owned fire stations for placement of network hubs.    (5) Middle Mile Justification: The underground fiber trunk installation in new conduit within City ROW is necessary to facilitate the construction of 9.28 miles of new last miles fiber laterals to the OHA MDUs and CAIs along the conduit route. In addition to the 2,544 households served by the project, the route will facilitate future last-mile connections to an additional 12 MDUs operated by non-profits, 4,578 businesses passed, and could support fixed wireless networks that could serve thousands more unserved residents.    (6) Open Access: The City of Oakland accepts the open access requirements for the middle mile facilities in the proposed project specified by State's SB 156.

Estimated project plan with major milest: The project is slated to run from Contract award through to final Network turn up within a window of 263 days. There are no required permits expected to hold up the initial start of construction. Company Applicant Name: Surfnet Communications, Inc.
Contact Position: Chief Operations Officer
Description of the major infrastructure:
This project is entirely fiber based. All cables will be placed onto existing poles. 
 Prior to attaching to these poles, we will enter into joint use agreements with either PG&E or the appropriate incumbent LEC for that area. As a licensed CLEC we have active agreements in place with those companies for joint occupancy. 
 We will perform load calculations and make applications for joint occupancy according to CPUC requirements and guidelines. Each pole will be field surveyed and tested for structural integrity. Due to aging, some poles will need to be replaced or augmented with temporary poles to meet loading requirements as determined in G.O. 95. In some cases, it may become necessary to install short lengths of conduit to mitigate G.O. 95 deficiencies or to conform Rule 20A undergrounding specifications.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 25 total miles of aerial fiber to be installed on the utility poles for last-mile provisioning.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major expenses will include fiberoptic cable, routers, and hubs. We anticipate an 18-month construction timeline, starting from the date of the grant award.
Estimated project plan with major milest: Dates are estimated based on expedited approval of grant and permitting where applicable. Project milestones as follows:  10% Milestone ? 9/15/2024 Construction drawings ready for bidding  Vendors for all project areas identified and engaged.  Initial orders for materials placed.  35% Milestone ? 12/15/2024 Material ordering completed and deliveries scheduled.  Permits for Project Areas Submitted  Some contractors are working on projects.  60% Milestone ? 3/15/2025 All permits received.  Contractors identified and bids awarded for all projects.  All contractors actively working on projects.  85% Milestone ? 9/15/2025 50% of projects completed.  The project areas that have been acceptance tested are ready for sales and marking efforts.  100% Milestone ? 12/15/2025 All construction is completed and service available to all areas within the scope of the grant funding.  Final Report written and submitted. Project timelines and projections of completion outside of the proposed Gantt chart will be communicated with the CASF team immediately, if identified.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
We are applying for 100% funding through the Last-Mile FFA Grant for our FTTP project. Our budget includes no cash match.  
 Though not a Lifeline provider, we offer the ACP program and will provide a low-cost 50/20 Mbps plan for $40/month, committing to this pricing for 10 years. 
 Our FTTP construction plans have been preliminary designed, focusing on viability, cost, timeline, and execution. The technical design employs 100Gbps-capable equipment, offering 1Gbps service now and room for future enhancements.  
 With 20 years in business and 70 years of leadership experience, Surfnet is well-positioned for success. Project costs have been precisely estimated, including subcontracted construction and pole engineering by qualified companies. A $2.5 million line of credit is in place to ensure completion without affecting operational reserves, backed by our shareholder support for all FFA grants. Preliminary designs are fiber to the home or premise with less than 5% of the locations a possibility of alternative technology.  
 The project will interconnect with middle-mile infrastructure. We affirm that we will align with SB 156 for open access and public ownership. We've partnered with public agencies and non-profits, ensuring 100% public ownership of the resulting infrastructure, which Surfnet will lease and maintain. 
 Internet backhaul will be achieved through our existing network or new carrier connections, using an "active" headend for service distribution. Cabinets, either existing or new, will house backhaul routers. 
 The project utilizes Passive Optical Networking (PON), a power-efficient technology with minimal visual impact due to its small 1/2" cables. PON can deliver up to 10Gbps speeds. 
 All new cabling will be on existing poles, primarily owned by PG&E, eliminating CEQA review needs. We will conduct pole inspections and apply for joint occupancy using PG&E-approved methods. 
 The cost of each project meets the ministerial review criteria, and we request that it be funded as budgeted in our plan. We are local and familiar with the project area and are confident in the cost estimates we have done. Instances where there is a discrepancy between the estimated subsidy needed, we would demonstrate the low populations and development costs in this high-cost region.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Internet backhaul for this project will be accomplished through a fiber connection from points in our current network, new points of connectivity within our network or by leveraging existing or new connections to carriers. We will then create an "active" headend that will house the electronics that will be used to distribute services to the proposed area. In some cases, we will use existing cabinets that we have in place today. Where these are not available, we will install a PGE approved equipment enclosure at or on the base of the pole. These cabinets will contain the routers needed to complete the backhaul. 
 The technology we propose for the distribution of services is called Passive Optical Networking or (PON) technology. PON is a very power efficient technology that uses "point to multipoint architecture" that uses optical splitters to feed light into the service locations. It is widely used by legacy carriers. We are very experienced with its deployment characteristics and unique management requirements. It is our preferred deployment architecture. 
 PON is a highly power efficient, point to multi-point technology. Because of this, the cable placed on the poles is extremely small, being about 1/2" in diameter which results in minimal visual impacts and does not overload old poles. PON allows us to provide speeds of up to 10Gbs symmetrically to the end user. 
 We expect that all new cable will be installed on existing poles, thus eliminating any need for CEQA review. In most cases these poles are owned by PG&E. We will do pole inspections and load calculations of the poles using PG&E approved techniques and software. When completed we will make application for joint occupancy. 
 The current reach of our legacy network eliminates the need for any additional middle-mile construction. Furthermore, we are committed to SB156 and we will make any of our middle-mile facilities available as needed.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
We are applying for 100% funding through the Last-Mile FFA Grant for our FTTP project. Our budget includes no cash match.  
 Though not a Lifeline provider, we offer the ACP program and will provide a low-cost 50/20 Mbps plan for $40/month, committing to this pricing for 10 years. 
 Our FTTP construction plans have been preliminary designed, focusing on viability, cost, timeline, and execution. The technical design employs 100Gbps-capable equipment, offering 1Gbps service now and room for future enhancements.  
 With 20 years in business and 70 years of leadership experience, Surfnet is well-positioned for success. Project costs have been precisely estimated, including subcontracted construction and pole engineering by qualified companies. A $2.5 million line of credit is in place to ensure completion without affecting operational reserves, backed by our shareholder support for all FFA grants. Preliminary designs are fiber to the home or premise with less than 5% of the locations a possibility of alternative technology.  
 The project will interconnect with middle-mile infrastructure. We affirm that we will align with SB 156 for open access and public ownership. We've partnered with public agencies and non-profits, ensuring 100% public ownership of the resulting infrastructure, which Surfnet will lease and maintain. 
 We've selected low-income areas based on state and county income guidelines. 
 Internet backhaul will be achieved through our existing network or new carrier connections, using an "active" headend for service distribution. Cabinets, either existing or new, will house backhaul routers. 
 The project utilizes Passive Optical Networking (PON), a power-efficient technology with minimal visual impact due to its small 1/2" cables. PON can deliver up to 10Gbps speeds. 
 All new cabling will be on existing poles, primarily owned by PG&E, eliminating CEQA review needs. We will conduct pole inspections and apply for joint occupancy using PG&E-approved methods. 
 The cost of each project meets the ministerial review criteria, and we request that it be funded as budgeted in our plan. We are local and familiar with the project area and are confident in the cost estimates we have done. Instances where there is a discrepancy between the estimated subsidy needed, we would demonstrate the low populations and development costs in this high-cost region.

This project is entirely fiber based. All cables will be placed onto existing poles.

Contact Position: Chief Strategic Initiative Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Kern County (applicant), in partnership with Cal.net, will use only fiber optic systems to bridge the digital divide in this community. Cal.net?s high-capacity high- availability network will be able to serve every resident who wants 100/100 Mbps. This same equipment will be able to serve anchor institutions and any business with custom service requirements as well. The network will be connected with the California Middle Mile Broadband network and another existing fiber network, each terminating at a separate data center, to ensure redundancy.  There are 13.25 miles of fiber planned to be built, and 4 cabinets constructed within the network.  Indefeasible Rights of Use are not used in this project.
