Estimated project plan with major milest: Upon receipt of permits from the relevant jurisdictions, the entire project is expected to take a total of eighteen (18) months to complete and test in preparation for the go live launch date.  Summary of major milestones: Q1: Feasibility study, Preliminary OSP engineering, Verify Existing Infrastructure Q2: Verify Existing Infrastructure, OSP Construction-mainline, OSP construction-splicing Q3: OSP construction-mainline, OSP construction-lateral, OSP construction-splicing Q4: OSP construction-mainline, OSP construction-lateral, OSP construction-drops, OSP construction-splicing Q5: OSP construction-lateral, OSP construction-drops, OSP construction-splicing, Tie-in to the statewide MMBI network ,OLT/head-end turn-up , Begin customer provisioning  Q6: OSP construction-drops, OSP construction-splicing, Tie-in to the statewide MMBI network ,OLT/head-end turn-up , Begin customer provisioning
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Service is based on the FFA 100 Mbps download requirement but has the ability to be customized to match the consumers? specific requirements. However, we can deliver packages with download speeds up to 1 Gbps.  Service is based on the FFA 100 Mbps upload requirement but has the ability to be  customized to match the consumers? specific requirements. However, we can deliver packages with upload speeds up to 500 Mbps.  
 This project will utilize two basic trenching types ? microtrenching and plowing.  All work will be performed in existing road rights of way and will therefore be categorically exempt from CEQA review.  Where microtrenching affects pavement, appropriate restoration materials and methods will be applied depending on the type of pavement affected.  Plowing will occur off-pavement, and material displaced will be replaced in a manner consistent with the existing ground and surface material.   
 The design leverages the existing infrastructure adjacent to or near the candidate sites.  The California Middle Mile Broadband network will be utilized at one or more connection points.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Cal.Net offers fiber delivered internet using Passive Optical Network (PON) technology comprising one or more Optical Line Termination (OLT) chassis which connects a PON to customer homes using Gigabit Ethernet PON (GPON [1] ) and/or 10 Gigabit Ethernet Symmetrical PON (XGS-PON [2] ). The initial deployment would utilize the GPON standard, allowing for 2.5/1.24G Down/Up speeds aggregate per PON port. This could be upgraded in the future to XGS-PON allowing for 10/10G Down/Up speeds aggregate per PON port. The OLT chassis has uplinks to Cal.net geographically redundant datacenters with multiple internet peers, using dedicated fiber transport or licensed microwave wireless transport. The nodes would be deployed in cabinets where the middle-mile backhaul interconnects. From these nodes, Cal.net would construct predominately underground fiber plant. 1:32 splitters collocated with the OLT each serve a cluster of served locations, what we term Fiber Service Areas (?FSAs?). Remote splitters can be placed at a later date to increase the number of homes by up to 32x the initial build. Both GPON and XGS-PON may coexist on the same fiber using course wave division multiplexing (CWDM). Cal.net uses CWDM muxes, demuxes, filters, and passive splitters and combiners to implement a fiber network designed to serve current customers, with the ability to scale the network and grow to serve future customers as well. This architecture enables the lowest possible latency on round trips to the Internet. Each GPON optical transmitter in the OLT feeds a 32x1 optical splitter which can serve up to 32 homes (expandable to 128 homes per transmitter), at an aggregate download throughput of 2.4Gbps, and upload throughput of 1.2Gbps. Each XGS-optical transmitter is similar but supports up to 10Gbps symmetrical aggregate throughput. Homes may be moved from one FSA splitter to another to ensure sufficient capacity is available for all subscribers.   Distances within the proposed network should require no greater than Class C+ optics, allowing for distances up to 60 km between the OLT and any subscriber.

Company Applicant Name: City of San Buenaventura
Contact Position: Chief Technology Officer
Description of the major infrastructure:
Funding through this grant application will establish a city-wide last-mile Fiber to the Home (FTTH) network targeted at reaching the most critically unserved locations in Ventura, CA. This 100% publicly-owned fiber optic network will leverage the city?s Traffic Signal Project (TSP) routes that are currently under bid, maximizing funding while enabling a further reach to more locations. Our proposed $11.2 million project will bring scalable broadband speeds to 901 households, all of which are confirmed as unserved by CPUC data.      There will be three interconnection points with the statewide Middle Mile Network? one along Hwy 33, one along Hwy 101, and a third at the eastern end of the City, where the City?s network will interconnect with the County?s planned Highway 126 route to Santa Clarita and the State?s Middle-Mile Network on Interstate 5.    This project contains roughly 71,791 feet of new fiber routes, broken down between 36,289 feet of underground fiber and 35,502 feet of aerial fiber that will create fiber rings around the city off of TSP infrastructure . All underground routes will be laid within existing rights of way while all aerial routes will be attached to pre-existing poles in compliance with the pole loading requirements of General Order 95. These routes have been intentionally established to ensure that the city?s network has the redundancy and capacity for servicing any and all future broadband needs. There are no middle-mile routes proposed within this application. While the final engineering details are subject to the final design (to be completed in Q1 of 2024), these routes will include 2? conduit with a 288-strand fiber cable.  
 Astound Broadband, our partnered Internet Service Provider (ISP), will oversee all end-user connections of this project. Astound has over decades of experience with broadband infrastructure and deployment throughout its history and acquisition, and will bring broadband symmetrical speeds ranging from 50 Mbps (low-cost option) to 2 Gigs (top tier) that meet the unique needs and financial positions of subscribers. A Letter of Intent detailing project responsibilities and expectations has been initiated with Astound. Upon project completion, other ISPs will have the ability to lease portions of the City?s network, using the open-access fiber routes to reach customers. This will promote competition, thus driving down broadband costs and creating affordable options currently not available within the city.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
The proposed fiber routes for this project will consist of 35,502 feet of aerial fiber attached on existing poles and 36.289 feet of underground fiber for a total of 71,792 feet of new fiber routes.    The aerial portion of this build comes to roughly 49.5% of the project total. These will be attached on existing poles owned by Southern California Edison. Necessary permitting will be obtained once grant funding is made available, prior to construction. There will also be additional aerial overlash fiber, determined based on post-award field surveys and final design engineering.  
 The underground portion of this build comes to roughly 50.5% of the project total. These will be installed though excavation and/or directional boring, with some laterals and service drops utilizing micro trenching. Underground portions of the build will be laid in existing rights of way within the city away from environmentally sensitive locations. As a result, we anticipate a categorical exemption under CEQA, but will undertake a full review once the design engineering is completed.
Major Equipment Expenses:
In total, major installation and equipment expenses will account for $1,254,922 of the project budget, all of which is requested through this grant application. These assumptions have been calculated based upon known network elements of broadband infrastructure and through Magellan's extensive experience in telecommunications.   
 These costs are broken down as follows:  Edge Routing: $160,000 Core Routing: $250,000 Switching: $22,500 Access Network termination: $135,000 Network Software: $10,000 Network Security and Firewalls: $60,000 Network Management	: $150,000	 IP Services: $30,000 Spares (including switches and routers): $25,000 ONU/CPE: $94,605 NID enclosure: $13,515 Residential Gateway (RG): $47,302 RG Management: $6,000 Installation Professional Services: $168,500 Customer Billing Platform: $50,000 Subscriber Provisioning: $25,000 Fiber Management and Documentation: $7,500 
 All final equipment costs and breakdowns are subject to change based on final design engineering, which will be completed by the first quarter of 2024.
Estimated project plan with major milest: FFA grant application: Quarter 4 of 2023 Design engineering: Quarters 1-2 of 2024 High level design: Quarter 1 of 2024 Low level design: Quarter 2 of 2024 Final design: Quarter 2 of 2024 CEQA Review (exemption): Quarter 2 of 2024 Permitting: Quarters 3-4 of 2024 Construction bid:  Quarter 3 of 2024 Bid award: Quarter 4 of 2024 Staking/preconstruction: Quarter 4 of 2024 Backbone construction: Quarter 4 of 2024-Quarter 2 of 2025 Splicing and network connection: Quarter 2 of 2025 Grant-funded service drop installation: Quarter 3 of 2025
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The City of Ventura recognizes that connectivity across the city is far from ideal. While infrastructure within the city has expanded over the past couple of decades, a substantial number of unserved pockets are left disconnected, creating obstacles to digital equity. Critical public infrastructure is without decent connectivity, some without any redundancy or backup connections. We are seeking to address these growing issues by establishing a city-wide ring-based network that will bring last mile connections to these critically disadvantaged locations.   In total, we anticipate 901 new passings to underserved households in addition to the 20 anchor institutions and public safety facilities that will receive new and improved services. Subscribers to this new network will have access to affordable broadband tiers ranging from 50 Mbps to 2 Gigs through Astound Broadband.   While we anticipate this project to be categorically exempt from CEQA review, a thorough review will be completed by the County?s Planning Department (local CEQA authority) once design engineering is completed. This project will be constructed within existing rights of way, avoiding environmentally sensitive areas. The exact date of this review will be determined post award and prior to construction.    Our network will be open access and connect directly to the statewide Middle Mile network along Highways 33 and 101, as well as a third connection on Interstate 5 utilizing the County?s network along Highway 126. While there are no middle mile portions within this project, the City of Ventura recognized the open-access requirements outlined within SB 156 and within the FFA Program Rules and Guidelines. In addition to the statewide network, this project will interconnect with the County?s Public Broadband Project that is still in development. This project will be a cornerstone of the County?s broadband plan, connecting County offices and building redundancy into their network through interconnection.  
 We recognize that this project is much larger than just the City? the City of Ventura is the county seat, and its network will act as a pivotal interconnection point of a regional network that reaches all disconnected and disadvantaged locations. Without project completion, it will be challenging for our neighboring cities and public institutions, including the Cities of Santa Paula, Moorpark, Port Hueneme, Oxnard and County of Ventura, to interconnect and communicate with their peer public agencies.

Application: Project Name: City of Fremont Last Mile Broadband Project
Company Applicant Name: City of Fremont
Contact Position: City of Fremont Information Technology Services
Project Title: City of Fremont Last Mile Broadband Project
Description of the major infrastructure: The City of Fremont is seeking FFA Last-Mile grant funding to construct Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network to connect unserved households, businesses, and anchor institutions. The grant will leverage 30.5 miles of existing underground City conduit & fiber backbone, construct approximately 6.4 miles of new aerial backbone, construct 10.4 miles of new underground conduit/fiber backbone, and connect 514 unserved locations all of which are confirmed unserved by CPUC. The project will utilize a Gigabit Passive Optical Network (GPON) to deliver reliable, scalable broadband speeds.     The project will interconnect with 160,600 feet of existing city-owned fiber at multiple locations throughout the City to ensure redundancy and a looped architecture. It will interconnect with sections of the Statewide Middle Mile Backbone Network (MMBN) at two points: 1) East Warren Avenue & I-680, and 2) Mowry Avenue & Vancouver Common.     This project contains roughly 40,691 feet of new fiber routes, broken down between 13,096 feet of underground fiber and 27,595 feet of aerial fiber. All underground routes will be laid within existing rights of way while all aerial routes will be attached to pre-existing poles. These routes have been intentionally specified to ensure that the city?s network has the redundancy and capacity for servicing any and all future connection. Any middle-mile segments within this project are critical for being able to service last-mile connections to unserved households and businesses. While the final engineering details are subject to the final design (to be completed in Q2 of 2024), these routes will include two (2) 2-inch conduits, one with a with a 288-strand fiber cable and one with a mule-tape.    Our partnered Internet Service Provider (ISP), Sonic, will oversee all end-user connections of this project. Sonic has a distinguished track record in deploying both aerial and underground fiber infrastructure, which presently interconnects over 150,000 residents and empowers many businesses and enterprises with a comprehensive suite of lit and dark services. A Letter of Intent detailing project responsibilities and expectations has been initiated with Sonic. Sonic will allow other providers access to the backbone at reasonable rates, to ensure fair and equitable access to available backbone fiber resources.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Total fiber footage: 132,445   Aerial: 45.5%, total of 60,345   Underground: 54.5%, total of 72,100
Major Equipment Expenses: In total, major equipment expenses will account for $552,481.52 of the project budget. All of this will be covered by a match from our partner Sonic and funds are not requested in the grant for these expenses.    These costs include the following list of equipment:     Edge Router - $35,000  Access Network - $64,792  Spares - $10,000  Drops - $155,125  ONT and Residential Gateway Equipment - $287,564.52    The largest expenses for the project come from the underground fiber ($5,956,825) and aerial fiber ($1,002,317). Labor costs have been incorporated on a per foot basis, as is standard in telecom. Funds for these expenses are requested in the grant.
Estimated project plan with major milest: Construction for this project will be completed within the 18 month timeframe. All design engineering will be completed Q1-Q2 of 2024. At this time, we plan to also be CEQA exempt and will begin the permitting project for the rest of the project?this will take place in Q3 and Q4 of 2024. During Q3, we will also put out the construction bid for the project, to be awarded in Q4 when staking will also begin. Actual construction will begin in Q4 of 2024 and continue through Q2 of 2025. Q2 of 2025 will also see the networking and splicing and service drops. The network will be lit in Q3 of 2025.    Design Engineering ? Q1-Q2 2024  HLD ? Q1 2024  LLD ? Q2 2024  Final Design ? Q2 2024  CEQA Review (exempt) - Q2 2024  Permitting ? Q3 & Q4 2024  Construction bid ? Q3 2024  Staking & Preconstruction ? Q4 2024  Backbone construction ? Q4 2024, Q1 & Q2 2025  Splicing and network connections ? Q2 2025  Grant funded service drop installation ? Q2 2025  Network Launch ? Q3 2025
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The City of Fremont is seeking FFA grant funding to construct Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network to connect unserved households, businesses, and anchor institutions. In its project design, the City prioritizes unserved and underserved locations to provide affordable, reliable broadband to the most vulnerable groups within the community, small businesses, and those most impacted by the digital divide.     In total, we anticipate 514 new passings to unserved locations. Subscribers to this new network will have access to affordable broadband tiers ranging from 50 Mbps to 10 Gbps through Sonic, our private internet service provider (ISP) partner. Moreover, these speeds can be further enhanced in the future through upgrades to Sonic's electronics infrastructure. Beyond the unserved locations, there will be 2,313 passings that Sonic will be able to connect to (outside of FFA funds).    While this project will not include any middle mile builds, we understand and support the state?s open access requirements. Our project will connect with the State Middle Mile Network at 1) East Warren Avenue & I-680, and 2) Mowry Avenue & Vancouver Common. In addition, the project has been designed to tie into the city?s current fiber network to utilize existing facilities.      While we anticipate this project to be categorically exempt from CEQA review, a thorough review will be completed by the City?s Planning Department (local CEQA authority) once design engineering is completed. This project will be constructed within existing rights of way, avoiding environmentally sensitive areas. The exact date of this review will be determined post award and prior to construction.    We recognize that within such a diverse and densely populated region, the network developed as part of this project is one step among many to close the digital divide and continue our transformation into a Smart City. By focusing on unserved and underserved locations in emerging communities and small businesses, we seek to prioritize those in greatest need; however, the intent of our broader Broadband Master Plan is to provide robust broadband connectivity across the region. Infrastructure delivered through this portion of the plan will enable future projects, such as Traffic Signal Modernization and further business service opportunities.

Company Applicant Name: Tolowa Dee-ni' Nation
Contact Position: Community Development Director
Description of the major infrastructure: The Tolowa Dee-ni? Nation has a well-documented history of establishing Tribal owned corporations to provide infrastructure services to tribal members and the surrounding communities. The Nation owns and operates 4 businesses and 2 utilities (water & wastewater). In 2023 the Tribe received $500,000 in funding from the NTIA Tribal Broadband Connectivity Program and initiated the creation of the Tolowa Broadband Corporation (TBC) to initiate planning & design of the local fiber & wireless network required to meet the broadband needs of the community. This includes the planning & design to create a carrier-neutral Middle Mile Fiber Backbone to serve the Tribal Community, Anchor Institutions, and Unserved Customers in the Smith River area. These dollars will help supplement the requested CPUC Last Mile funding. The fiber to the home infrastructure will deliver XGSPON FTTH technology to all 1,174 service locations. All customer locations will connect with an indoor optical network terminal, (ONT) with battery backup. Inside gateways are proposed with dual band wireless frequency options and LAN ports. The technology can deliver up to 10Gbps symmetrically to each location. One communications shelter is required at the tribal administration office where the XGSPON OLT, DDI, layer 3 switch, and firewall will be placed with generator backup power. All underground fiber cable will be placed in conduit and 22 new poles will need placed. Pre-connectorized terminals and drops will used.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The proposed project includes 7,891LFT of aerial fiber attached to 32 utility poles and 60,008LFT of underground conduit and 1100LFT of bridge attached conduit in Del Norte County. 90% of the project is underground while 10% of the project is aerial. Of that underground conduit and fiber , 43,741LFT is trench, 1100LFT is bridge attachment, and 16,267LFT is HDD. In addition, the project includes 16,435LFT of buried drop fiber and 9,394LFT of aerial drop fiber.
Major Equipment Expenses: Pull fiber through conduit - $108,734 Lash fiber to strand - $14,677 Communications Shelter w/install- $117,000 XGSGPON OLT/ONT?s /equipment and software + installation- $214,532 Ped w/terminal + installation - $7,500 Splice Cases + installation- $5,180 Layer 2 Switch, DDI, Firewall + installation - $35,000 Fiber cable - $194,750 Splicing and Testing w/material - $115,000.00 Aerial terminal + installation - $26,500

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The Tolowa Broadband Corporation (TBC) is proposing to build a 13-mile FTTH project in Del Norte County. The proposed project area is all within the Tolowa tribal reservation and ancestral territory. The project will require aerial construction on pre-existing poles and new poles within pre-disturbed private property areas. New underground is required by way of trench and directional drill in pre-disturbed existing rights of way. Trenching will be restored using native soil where compaction requirements can be established and select fill when needed. No sensitive areas have been identified. The Tolowa Dee-ni? Nation plans to create the Tolowa Broadband Corporation to build, maintain, manage, and operate a carrier neutral fiber network. The business plan to create and operate this corporation outlined in the associated documents in this grant application and was created with the input from Tribal business leaders and experienced consultants with knowledge of creating and operating fiber and ISP businesses. The Tolowa NTIA project is providing much of the funding needed for expanded staffing, training, and equipment that have been modeled after other successful Tribally owned and operated telecommunications companies.   While the NTIA funds were a good start to establish the Tolowa Broadband Corporation, due to the challenging terrain and lack of fiber in this remote rural area, more infrastructure (fiber and towers) are needed to provide quality high speed ISP services to all locations on the reservation and in the ancestral territory in an equitable and affordable manner.   These CPUC grant funds are a critical source of funding to ensure equitable connectivity for all unserved locations on the reservation and in the surrounding community. The proposed project will include up to 1Gbps symmetrical services to all unserved locations. All locations within the proposed project area are unserved. The TBC middle mile project will establish and offer (in acceptance of the state SB 156 requirement) open access leased fiber on its middle mile fiber.

Company Applicant Name: AT&T Services, Inc. on behalf of its affiliate Pacific Bell Telephone Company d/b/a AT&T California
Contact Position: Contact Person	Ross Johnson rj2397@att.com Director ? Regulatory External and Legislative Affairs 430 Bush Street  San Francisco, CA  94108 ? 3735 4154175028
Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 3 miles of fiber, in 1 central office (s). Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 0 miles aerial and 3 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Description of the major infrastructure:

 Prior to attaching to these poles, we will enter into joint use agreements with either PG&E or the appropriate incumbent LEC for that area. As a licensed CLEC we have active agreements in place with those companies for joint occupancy.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
We are applying for 100% funding through the Last-Mile FFA Grant for our FTTP project. Our budget includes no cash match.  
 Though not a Lifeline provider, we offer the ACP program and will provide a low-cost 50/20 Mbps plan for $40/month, committing to this pricing for 10 years. 
 Our FTTP construction plans have been preliminary designed, focusing on viability, cost, timeline, and execution. The technical design employs 100Gbps-capable equipment, offering 1Gbps service now and room for future enhancements.  
 With 20 years in business and 70 years of leadership experience, Surfnet is well-positioned for success. Project costs have been precisely estimated, including subcontracted construction and pole engineering by qualified companies. A $2.5 million line of credit is in place to ensure completion without affecting operational reserves, backed by our shareholder support for all FFA grants. Preliminary designs are fiber to the home or premise with less than 5% of the locations a possibility of alternative technology.  
 The project will interconnect with middle-mile infrastructure. We affirm that we will align with SB 156 for open access and public ownership. We've partnered with public agencies and non-profits, ensuring 100% public ownership of the resulting infrastructure, which Surfnet will lease and maintain. 
 Internet backhaul will be achieved through our existing network or new carrier connections, using an "active" headend for service distribution. Cabinets, either existing or new, will house backhaul routers. 
 The project utilizes Passive Optical Networking (PON), a power-efficient technology with minimal visual impact due to its small 1/2" cables. PON can deliver up to 10Gbps speeds. 
 All new cabling will be on existing poles, primarily owned by PG&E, eliminating CEQA review needs. We will conduct pole inspections and apply for joint occupancy using PG&E-approved methods. 
 The cost of each project meets the ministerial review criteria, and we request that it be funded as budgeted in our plan. We are local and familiar with the project area and are confident in the cost estimates we have done. Instances where there is a discrepancy between the estimated subsidy needed, we would demonstrate the low populations and development costs in this high-cost region.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Subject to the City of San Bruno's municipal code, governance of the San Bruno City Council and exercised through the Office of the City Manager, City of San Bruno, the proposed broadband project plan will be funded through the City's General Fund which will be the recipient of FFA funding on behalf of San Bruno CityNet Services. The project will be subject to the City of San Bruno's competitive bidding process to separately source project materials and as award labor contracts to vendors to complete implementation. The proposed broadband project will be operated as an adjunct to San Bruno CityNet's operational remit which comprises operation and maintenance of the San Bruno CityNet Services business operations, cable/fiber plant and data centers as well as a Cable Television headend and VoIP telephone service provisioning in the City of San Bruno, CA. The objective of the proposed broadband project is to upgrade the existing Hybrid Fiber Coaxial network and add to the Fiber to the Home (FTTH) footprint which currently addresses 21.2% of the service locations in the City of San Bruno. The current FTTH technology comprises public Internet Protocol (IP) circuits received through Internet Routers and distributed to service locations via GPON units (Gigabit Passive Optical Networks) on fiber optic cables to end user ONTs (Optical Network Terminals). The proposed broadband project will expand the capacity of internet routers operated by San Bruno CityNet Services from 30 Gigabits to 120 Gigabits. An additional six GPON units will be added to increase internet bandwidth available to service locations through new fiber optic cable to cover the 78.2% of the footprint currently served through HFC (Hybrid Fiber Coaxial Cable). The current target for internet speed to be achieved through the proposed broadband project is 1000 Mbps (Megabits per second) on the downstream as well as upstream paths.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
This is a fiber-to-the-home project providing 1,000 Mbps/1,000 Mbps with path to 10G service to unserved premises within the City of Indio. This FFA project is targeting some of the highest need areas of our City where the provision of reliable and affordable high speed broadband will create greater digital equity and provide opportunities for remote work, education, and telehealth for disadvantaged Indio residents. 
 The proposed FFA project will leverage conduit and innerduct from the CV Sync partnership with Coachella Valley Association of Governments (CVAG). The CV Sync project consists of upgrading the local agencies? legacy traffic signal controllers, traffic management systems, and communication systems in order to coordinate traffic on corridors in the Coachella Valley. At the same time, it will deploy 24 miles of 3 inch ITS conduit in the City of Indio. The CV Sync project has begun installation of conduit in the greater Coachella Valley. Construction in the City of Indio will begin in the 4th quarter of 2023. This will enable the City to begin construction of FFA-proposed broadband facilities by the end of 2024, leveraging the conduit for the installation of FFA-funded fiber optic cable. 
 Implementation of this project will be through a public-private partnership. However, 100% of the fiber network assets funded by the FFA program will be owned by the City of Indio. 
 The proposed project includes 34,158 feet of fiber in new conduit and 66,552 feet of fiber in existing conduit. All construction will take place within existing rights of way along city streets. Where fiber installation intersects with the state highway, fiber will be installed in existing conduit. Where minor trenching will take place, the site will be restored by back fill.

After Phase 4 is completed, the redundant chassis will be deployed at a colocation center in Los Angeles, CA utilizing the California Statewide Middle Mile project. Estimated project plan with major milest: The City of Carson is proposing the construction of Phase 1 of the fiber optic network in a phased approach, listed in subphases below. Primary focus will be on the backbone installation along Avalon Blvd from E Alondra Blvd to E Sepulveda Blvd. The following subphases are to focus on anchor institutions followed by the residential parcels in each of the City?s four districts.   The main backbone of City of Carson?s fiber optic network will run down the east side of Avalon Blvd from E Alondra Blvd to E Sepulveda Blvd. This route is to consist of 2 (two) 4? HDPE conduits tied into 24?x36?x24? pull boxes. Pull box placement is expected to be underground, flush to grade, underneath sidewalks. Road crossings require pull boxes on either side. 1 (one) 864-count single mode fiber optic cable is proposed along this route with splice enclosures capable of housing the cable placed at major road crossings. Part of this phase is to include a connection inside City Hall that will serve as the city?s main server room (exact location within to be determined). Timeline of Avalon Blvd route is expected at approximately three months, with an additional month to construct the server room.   The anchor institutions (City Hall, Civic Center, Cal State Dominguez Hills) are to be connected to the proposed fiber optic network by intercepting these institutions communications ducts at the right-of-way property line, locations shown on plans are approximate. 24?x36?x24? pull boxes and ?y? intercepts are to be placed over the existing communication ducts. 1 (one) 96-count single mode fiber optic cable is to run into each institution, except for City Hall which will serve as the main hub for the City of Carson and have the north and south legs of the 864-count cable terminating within.   Phase 1 proposes connecting six residential neighborhoods within the City of Carson, all with varying amounts of households. Routes along these neighborhoods are to have of 1 (one) 4? HDPE conduit tied into 24?x36?x24? pull boxes. 1 (one) 288-count single mode fiber optic cable is proposed within these neighborhoods with splice enclosures placed at intersections and end of roadways for interconnectivity with drop cables. Drop cables for each household are to consist of 1 (one) 6-count single mode fiber optic cable with a ˝? to 1? conduit. Fiber connections are to terminate within a fiber termination panel inside each household. Timeline to connect unserved residents is expected at approximately 18 months.

Company Applicant Name: AT&T Services, Inc. on behalf of its affiliate Pacific Bell Telephone Company d/b/a AT&T California
Contact Position: Director ? Regulatory External and Legislative Affairs
Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 78 miles of fiber, in 1 central office (s). Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 69 miles aerial and 9 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 274 miles of fiber, in 1 central office (s). Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 231 miles aerial and 43 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 94 miles of fiber, in 1 central office (s). Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 45 miles aerial and 50 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 210 miles aerial and 58 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 92 miles of fiber, in 11 central office (s). Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: AT&T estimates that it will deploy 40 miles aerial and 52 miles of underground (buried) fiber. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
1)      Type of technologies to be provided in the proposed project area 
 El Dorado County (applicant), in partnership with Cal.net, will use only fiber optic systems to bridge the digital divide in this community. Cal.Net  offers fiber delivered internet using Passive Optical Network (PON) technology comprising one or more Optical Line Termination (OLT) chassis which connects a PON to customer homes using Gigabit Ethernet PON (GPON) and/or 10 Gigabit Ethernet Symmetrical PON (XGS-PON). The initial deployment would utilize the GPON standard, allowing for 2.5/1.24G Down/Up speeds aggregate per PON port. This could be upgraded in the future to XGS-PON allowing for 10/10G Down/Up speeds aggregate per PON port.    2)      Download and upload speed capabilities of proposed facilities: Service is based on the FFA 100 Mbps download and upload requirements with options for up to 1 Gbps download and 500 Mbps upload.   3)      Sufficient construction detail to enable a preliminary indication of the need for a California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) review and if proposed project areas contain any environmentally sensitive areas: This project will utilize two basic trenching types ? microtrenching and plowing. All work will be performed in existing road rights of way and will therefore be categorically exempt from CEQA review. Where microtrenching affects pavement, appropriate restoration materials and methods will be applied depending on the type of pavement affected. Plowing will occur off-pavement, and material displaced will be replaced in a manner consistent with the existing ground and surface material.   4)      Identification of the leveraging of existing available facilities: The design leverages the existing infrastructure adjacent to or near the candidate sites. The California Middle Mile Broadband network will be utilized at one or more connection points.   5)      An explanation of why any Middle Mile facilities in the proposed project are necessary for accessing the proposed last-mile infrastructure.: No new middle mile facilities are part of the funding request or in the proposed project.   6)      A statement accepting the open access requirements for any Middle Mile facilities in the proposed project:  Not applicable - no middle mile facilities are in the proposed project.

Company Applicant Name: Santa Ynez Band of Chumash Indians
Contact Position: Grants Coordinator
Description of the major infrastructure: The proposed infrastructure consists of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network to provide high speed broadband services to 303 locations within Chumash Lands, including the original Reservation and the Trust Land known as Camp 4.  13 miles of fiber will be deployed via underground methods. 3 miles of distribution fiber will be deployed at the Chumash Original Reservation. A 2.2-mile fiber route will connect the Camp 4 fiber distribution with the Chumahs Casino. Finally, 7.8 miles of fiber will be deployed at Camp 4 utilizing a joint utility trench.  The data center at the Chumash Casino (at the Original Reservation) will host all the active network equipment. Therefore, no remote terminals are considered.   Construction of middle mile infrastructure is not required as the plan of the Chumash is to leverage the California Middle Mile Broadband Initiative (CMMBI) network for backhaul service. While the MMBI is built, existing backhaul at the data center will be upgraded to support the traffic from the broadband network.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The proposed fiber network considers 100% of underground fiber installation.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major installation expenses are as follows:  Underground fiber installation, which includes conduit boring  Conduit installation in Camp 4?s joint utility trench (JUT)  Fiber pulling  Microtrenching and microduct installation for drops  Major equipment costs consist of:  4-way HDPE innerduct  Fiber optic cables  Underground vaults  HDPE microduct  Optical Line Terminal (OLT) cards and optical modules  IP Multimedia Subsystem and Voice Platform  Routing and switching equipment  Vaults, splicing closures, splitters, and distribution boxes
Estimated project plan with major milest: The project plan for network buildout targets a 12-month timeline for completion after Grant award. This project plan considers that 1) the proposed project is subject to CEQA categorical exemption within the Santa Ynez Reservation and all the necessary permits for Camp 4 construction have been obtained, 2) the Tribe will grant all the necessary permits within their lands, and 3) industry wide delays in the supply chain must be considered.  Based on the above, the estimated timeline and milestones are described below:  Milestone 1: Permitting [Months 1 to 6]  Milestone 2: Materials Procurement & Delivery [Months 1 to 6]  Milestone 3: Joint Utility Trenching [Months 1 to 6]  Milestone 4: Conduit boring and vault installation [Months 7 to 8]  Milestone 5: Fiber Cable installation [Month 8]  Milestone 6: Fiber Splicing [Month 9]  Milestone 7: Service Drops and Network Equipment Provisioning [Months 10 to 12]   Please note that the timeline above is subject to change based on factors that are outside of Chumash?s control such as unexpected delays in permitting, supply chain disruptions, extreme weather conditions, and other force majeure considerations.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The Chumash Fiber Network project seeks to provide FTTP services to 153 locations in the Chumash Original Reservation and 150 locations under construction at Camp 4, a 1,411-acre area parcel of trust land with an ongoing housing development. Among these locations, 195 are considered unserved.  The proposed technology for this deployment is XGS-PON, based on ITU-T Recommendation G.9807.1. XGS-PON offers a symmetric line rate of ~10 Gbit/s, shared efficiently among multiple users through optical splitters. This configuration ensures symmetrical download/upload speeds of at least 1 Gbps per subscriber.  13 miles of fiber will be undergrounded. 3 miles of distribution fiber will be deployed at the Chumash Original Reservation. A 2.2-mile fiber route will connect the Camp 4 fiber distribution with the Chumahs Casino. Finally, 7.8 miles of fiber will be be deployed at Camp 4 utilizing a joint utility trench.  7.8 miles of fiber will be deployed at Camp 4 utilizing a joint utility trench for which all the permits and CEQA requirements have been fulfilled. The remaining 5.2 miles will be deployed through underground methods, including 3 miles within the Reservation. For these 5.2 miles, the Tribe anticipates categorical exemption from CEQA based on the following:  Class 4 (14 C.C.R. § 15304): This category covers minor alterations to land, such as the 5 miles of underground installation, mainly employing direct boring in previously disturbed land.  Exceptional Cases: We do not anticipate exceptions to this categorical exemption (14 C.C.R. § 15300.2) since we have not identified environmentally sensitive areas, scenic highways, and the Tribe will conduct the necessary cultural reviews to avoid impact to any historical resources within the area. Final CEQA determinations will follow outside plant engineering and final network design.  The project leverages existing facilities:  1,140 ft of existing conduit between the Chumash Casino and the Museum alongside CA-246  The data center at the Chumash Casino where network equipment will be stored.  Existing backhaul connectivity at the data center, and once built, CMMBI facilities for backhaul services.  The Tribe is requesting to revise the unserved locations in the proposed area as locations in Camp 4 are under construction. Adequate evidence is provided in Section 8 Project Eligibility. The Tribe is not requesting funding for middle mile facilities as we are planning to leverage the CMMBI network once it is built.

Company Applicant Name: Zito Southern California, LLC
Contact Position: Grants Project Manager
Description of the major infrastructure:
Zito Southern California, LLC ("Zito") is proposing to deploy three broadband projects in San Diego County: Julian/Ramona FTTH: Build 212 miles of new GPON fiber to the home/premise (FTTH) to serve 2,324 unserved locations. Borrego Springs FTTH Upgrade: Upgrade an existing Hybrid Fiber Coaxial (HFC) system to FTTH to serve 995 low-income locations. Borrego Springs Middle Mile Expansion: Build 55.9 miles of new middle mile fiber to create two completely diverse paths out of Borrego Springs. Zito will offer several tiers of FTTH symmetrical (same speed up and down) broadband service. The top tier will be 1000 Mbps (1 Gbps) symmetrical, at very affordable rates. This Zito Fiber To The Home/Premise (FTTH) architecture will utilize ITU-T G.984 Gigabit Passive Optical Network (GPON) with a typical ratio of 32 subscribers per PON. Zito already operates a DOCSIS based fiber/coax network in San Diego County as well as GPON networks a number of other states. The GPON network will operate out of the same main hub or in a cabinet that is in closer approximation to the existing Zito fiber system in San Diego County. Optical Line Terminals (OLT?s) are anticipated to be DZS. Optical Network Units (ONU?s) are anticipated to be Halny. These ONU's will allow Zito to monitor the performance of the GPON network, as well as manage network faults, configurations, and security. 
 Benefits These projects would provide state-of-the-art FTTH broadband, voice, and IPTV video services to 3,319 locations in San Diego County. 1,826 (55%) of these locations lie in areas designated as Low Income Areas. The middle mile expansion would significantly improve network reliability and avoid outages in Borrego Springs. 
 Implementation Zito would begin with a formal project site survey and fiber design. Once the design is complete, Zito would seek all necessary permits and begin construction. The majority of the new fiber lines would be aerial, attaching to existing utility poles. 
 Conclusion Zito's broadband deployment proposal would provide significant benefits to San Diego County, including improved broadband access, reliability, and affordability.   NOTE: Due to text size limitation in the FFA portal, this is a shortened version of a more complete version, available upon request.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Julian/Ramona: Zito proposes to build 212 miles of all-new GPON fiber to the home/premise (FTTH) to serve 2,324 underserved locations in Julian and Ramona. The FTTH architecture will utilize ITU-T G.984 Gigabit Passive Optical Network (GPON) with a typical ratio of 32 subscribers per PON. Similar architecture will be deployed for the Borrego Springs project below. Borrego Springs: Zito proposes to upgrade its existing Hybrid Fiber Coaxial (HFC) system to all-new FTTH to serve 995 low-income locations in Borrego Springs. The architecture would be the same as that described above. Approximately 58 miles of fiber would be built over existing pole attachments. These services would be 1000 Mbps (1 Gbps) symmetrical (same speed up and down) Borrego Springs to Salton City/Calipatria to Kane Middle Mile Expansion: Zito proposes to build 55.9 miles of new middle mile fiber to create two completely diverse paths out of Borrego Springs. Only at this point can we firmly determine what network components will be needed where, as well as assess all of the poles for attachment, as well as any underground. Zito will comply with all Open Access requirements for this middle mile fiber. Zito is not revising the area for which it is requesting funding. ?
