Company Applicant Name: Cold Springs Rancheria of Mono Indians of California
Contact Position: Grantwriting consultant
Description of the major infrastructure: Optical Fiber cable in a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) XGS-PON architecture, from an existing fiber termination point in the Rancheria. All cables are planned to be placed underground mainly in existing disturbed Rights-of-Way. ONTs (Optical NetworkTerminals) will be placed at each premise, with the hosting OLT (Optical Line Terminal) at the centralized location. The distances to the most distant subscribers will be well within the service limits of the industry-standard equipment.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 100% underground, due to fire danger
Major Equipment Expenses: ONTs at each premise will cost roughly $150 on average for the optical interface and built-in WiFi access points. An OLT at the existing optical fiber aggregation point is estimated to cost $30,000, including three optical fiber splitters and fiber terminations. Interfaces to upstream transport equipment is estimated to cost $5,000 for the base optical transponders in the OLT chassis. A cabinet with power interfaces and batteries is expected to cost $25,000.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
Assuming the funds are available at the end of 2023 or early in 2024: 
 2 months for detailed engineering and assembly of the specifications for an OSP contract, and ISP contract 2 months for bidding and selection of contractors, Environmental evaluations and Permitting 4 months for logistics and delivery of materials 2 months for cable installation 1 month for CPE installation, turn-up, and cutover, and the coordination with the upstream ISP 1 month for final testing, assembly of As-Built documents, and contract close-outs 12 months total project
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Our proposed FTTP XGS-PON network will provide standard Ethernet and WiFi interfaces, with service speeds up to 1 Gigabit, and higher-speed offerings with special adaptations. Voice service comes from existing telephone network infrastructure, and can be provided via cloud-based VoIP over the FTTP network. The existing optical fiber feed into the Rancheria is owned by Ponderosa telephone. Our proposed network would initially utilize Ponderosa Tel's upstream ISP services. Their backbone capacity will make it possible for us to access the Internet backbone and many cloud-based services, including a path towards competitive service offerings.

Application: Project Name: City of Lathrop Broadband Infrastructure Project
Company Applicant Name: City of Lathrop
Contact Position: Information Systems Director
Project Title: City of Lathrop Broadband Infrastructure Project
Description of the major infrastructure: This is a fiber-to-the-home project that will include 26.2 miles of last mile fiber installations. Approximately 57% of installations will be aerial and 43% will be underground. There will be two routers in the city limits with direct backhaul to Los Angeles and San Jose. Key network equipment is currently planned to be located at City Hall, pending final design. There will be no assets included under an Indefeasible Right of Use.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
Total Aerial Installation: 79,392 ft - 57% Total Underground Installation: 58,919 ft - 43% 
 The following is the breakdown across both segments of the project: 
 City of Lathrop Ring: New Underground Fiber & Conduit: 23,500 ft New Fiber (existing conduit): 33,202 ft New Aerial Fiber Routes: 24,425 ft 
 French Camp Loop: New Underground Fiber & Conduit: 2,217 ft New Aerial Fiber Routes: 54,967 ft
Major Equipment Expenses: $3,357,028 - new public network backbone conduit & fiber $5,271,659 - new public network aerial fiber $1,219,500 - network equipment, to include the following: broadband switching equipment, including edge routing, core routing, switching, and access network termination
Estimated project plan with major milest: The proposed project is on an 18-month construction timeline beginning in the 4th quarter of 2023 when it is anticipated the FFA grant will be awarded. Design engineering will last from Q1- Q2 2024. Final design and CEQA review should be completed by the end of Q2 2024. It is anticipated that permitting will last from Q3-Q4 2024. The initial construction bid will be released in Q3 and the bid award finalized in the 4th Quarter of 2024. Staking will take place in Q4 and backbone construction is estimated to last from the 4th Quarter of 2024 through the 2nd Quarter of 2025. Grant funded service drop installations will take place in Q2 2025, followed by the network launch.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The proposed fiber-to-the-home project will reach 711 unserved locations in the City of Lathrop and the unincorporated community of French Camp. The provision of FFA funds for last mile broadband infrastructure will facilitate digital equity and inclusion, support economic development, and improve quality of life, particularly among our more disadvantaged and vulnerable communities. The City of Lathrop began conceptualizing this project when it applied for a Local Agency Technical Assistance grant from CPUC in 2022. The City was awarded that grant in June 2023 and contracted with an engineering consultancy firm, Magellan Advisors, to help develop a broadband master plan for the City. The proposed project is a high level design aimed to reach the areas of greatest need in Lathrop and the adjoining community of French Camp. 
 This project will leverage 73,455 feet of existing City fiber and add 33,202 feet of new fiber in existing City conduit to form the network backbone that will drive the proposed FFA project. In total, the project includes 25,717 feet of fiber in new conduit, 33,202 feet of fiber in existing conduit, and 79,392 feet of aerial installation on existing poles. All construction will take place within existing rights of way along streets and rail lines. This project will require minor trenching where the surface is restored by backfill. No proposed activities will take place in environmentally sensitive areas.   Both segments of this project (City of Lathrop Ring and French Camp Ring) will interconnect with the state Middle Mile. There will be no construction of middle mile facilities in the proposed project. 
 Implementation of this project will be through a public-private partnership between the City of Lathrop and OnWard, a private internet service provider based in CA. As a qualified and certified ISP, OnWard will lead the network operations, maintenance, management, marketing, sales, customer service and technical support necessary to ensure superior data connectivity across the City's network. However, 100% of the network backbone (excluding service drops which are on private property) will remain publicly owned by the City.

Company Applicant Name: City of Sacramento
Contact Position: IT Operations Manager
Description of the major infrastructure: We will deploy an estimated 68.25 miles of fiber using XGSPON technology across Sacramento and in a few areas just outside the city limits. We will place the fiber in new and existing, City-approved telecommunications conduit and innerducts as needed. In the past, the City has installed infrastructure leveraging multiple techniques including, Rock Wheel, Direct Burial, T-Cut Trench, and a host of other methods to effectively install PVC, & HDPE conduits. Where such infrastructure is needed the city will install all infrastructure as duct banks, bundling multiple conduits during installation and leverage innerducts to further subdivide conduits.      Additional major components that will be deployed include handholes, utility vaults, and six above ground aggregation hubs with one spare. This infrastructure is common throughout the industry and has been deployed in previous city projects. Once high-level design is completed, we will know the locations and quantities this project will require.    The City is seeking an ISP partner to provide service for this project. That RFP was open July 27 ? August 18, 2023. We are currently negotiating with a likely partner and anticipate the project starting in January 2024. The ISP partner we identify will enter into an IRU with the City to use City-owned infrastructure.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 100% of our project will be built with buried fiber.
Major Equipment Expenses: Our major equipment expenses include fiber optic cable, pull boxes, splices, hubs, and ONTs at customer premises. We have estimated the expense of all major equipment at standard market value. We anticipate our construction contractor will provide tools needed for fiber and network deployment, like splicing equipment, testers, power meters, and other diagnostic tools. We anticipate that our ISP partner will cover the costs of the ONTs and cost of materials and construction for the new hubs.
Estimated project plan with major milest: The City is seeking an ISP partner to provide service for this project. That RFP was open July 27 ? August 18, 2023. We are currently negotiating with a likely partner and anticipate the project starting in January 2024.    Once we have established an official partnership, we anticipate we will complete high-level network designing and low-level engineering in the first two quarters of 2024 with our ISP partner to ensure we fully leverage both the City-owned infrastructure and the ISP?s infrastructure to support the new construction needed to serve the project area.    If we receive FFA funds by the end of the first quarter of 2024 and HDL and LDL completed by the end of the second quarter of 2024, we will put out a construction bid for the project in the third quarter of 2024 and will have the construction contractor established by the end of 2024. This will allow us to begin staking and permitting in the first quarter of 2025.    As permits are received, our contractor would begin construction of our network building connections to the curb throughout the project area. Following our phased approach, during the second and third quarters of 2025, initial Phase 1 construction will prioritize building to unserved locations in our project area that require the least amount of construction or the shortest runs. During the third and fourth quarters of 2025, we would connect our network to the statewide middle mile network, if available, and complete the construction to the curb for the remaining unserved households. During the fourth quarter of 2025 and the first quarter of 2026, we would build out Phase 2, connecting city facilities and public safety locations to the network.    Service drops to the premises would begin as soon as feasible once construction to the curb has begun and splicing has begun in the third quarter of 2025 we anticipate that service drops would continue to occur through the remainder of the construction period and beyond as new subscribers sign up for service.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The City of Sacramento is requesting $38,696,843.00 from the Last Mile Federal Funding Account to extend the city-owned fiber network with an additional estimated 68.25 miles of XGSPON fiber, capable of delivering 10/10 Gbps speeds to ensure last mile connectivity to an additional estimated 4620 unserved households. Our conceptual network design can be implemented in multi-phased approach to provide flexibility relative to grant availability, budgets, and resource constraints and to maximize the network reach into new markets to attract a private partner. The two phases outlined below can be further broken down into sub-phases or built incrementally as needed.    Phase 1 would modify the City's fiber network through a combination of new construction, upgrading existing infrastructure, and interconnecting with the statewide middle-mile network. The construction is necessary to build a robust fiber network that will support residential services to unserved locations and provide route diversity to key communities to maximize network uptime. Phase 1 would interconnect the City's network ring with the statewide middle-mile network at three points along Interstates 5 and 80 and California State Highway 99.    Phase 2 connects city facilities, including 16 fire stations, to the City-owned fiber to create a significant annual cost savings, ensure uninterrupted service delivery, and significantly increase the broadband speeds at these sites from 20 Mbps to 10/10 Gbps.    The entirety of our project area is considered last mile and is previously disturbed and meets the requirements of CEQA review exemption as a linear broadband deployment under SB156. Additionally, our project does not include any native and tribal lands that would supersede the exemption.    The City is seeking an ISP partner to provide service for this project. That RFP was open July 27 ? August 18, 2023. We are currently negotiating with a likely partner and anticipate the project starting in January 2024. We will have additional details about that provider?s existing infrastructure and service offerings after the application has closed.

Application: Project Name: Providing Broadband Service to Butte County Locations in Need of Investment
Company Applicant Name: City of Chico
Contact Position: Management Analyst
Project Title: Providing Broadband Service to Butte County Locations in Need of Investment
Description of the major infrastructure:
Through this project, the City of Chico (the City) will deploy fiber optic broadband infrastructure through an active Ethernet broadband connection and fiber optic huts constructed throughout the proposed service area. This major infrastructure will be installed strategically in the City of Chico city limits and one adjacent unincorporated area to optimize connectivity and redundancies throughout the project area. This includes dedicated fiber which will be installed to each household and business in the service area. Project designs, completed by an award-winning consultant firm Biarri Networks, are already in place; this project is shovel-ready.   Though Section 2. Plan Details reflects information generated by the project?s map tool, it is only considering 4,725 locations, while this project will actually be serving 9,040 locations. This is demonstrated by the Project Location Data provided in the application.  
 ?
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
This project will consist of 100 percent underground installation. 
 ?
Major Equipment Expenses:
Major equipment expenses include: 
 ·       3 fiber utility huts/structures; ·       175,168 linear feet of fiber optic cables; ·       1,219 multiport service terminal; ·       105,818 linear feet of duct; ·        252 hand holes/vaults/pits; ·       80 marker posts; ·       711 splices; and ·       24,060 groundings. 
 Individual costs are detailed in the project?s Proposed Project Expenditures in Section 10 of this application.
Estimated project plan with major milest: The estimate project plan is provided below including major milestones and a construction timeline.   Pre-Construction Activities - Major milestones include: ·        Order and receive broadband infrastructure equipment from October 15, 2023 to December 15, 2023. ·        Complete construction bidding process by November 30, 2023 when contract is signed. ·        Obtain construction permits and CEQA review timeline as needed (likely a CEQA-exempt project) from October 1, 2023 to January 15, 2024.   Construction Activities: Per program guidelines, construction activities will commence upon receipt of permits and will be completed within an 18-month period, as required for CEQA-exempt projects. Major milestones include:   ·        Start installation of broadband infrastructure on January 15, 2024. ·        Broadband infrastructure installed along City streets by January 15, 2025. ·        Broadband service, on-property infrastructure, and support available to customers by March 1, 2025.   See this project?s Deployment Schedule attached in Section 9 for further detail on this project?s plan.   Marketing and Outreach Activities : Per program guidelines, this project?s Marketing and Outreach Plan will continue throughout the 10-year pricing commitment.   Major milestones and activities include a public outreach meeting before project breaks ground; engaging a marketing/PR firm TBD to drive marketing efforts and achieve broadband subscription targets; promotional signage in the service area; publishing educational information on the City website; monthly progress updates sent through the City and supporters? outreach channels; press releases; utility bill inserts; a door-to-door campaign; and a ribbon cutting ceremony.   A key highlight of this project is providing call center services once broadband service is available, among other efforts. Marketing and outreach material will be available in multiple languages to meet the needs of the community.   Efforts will also include engaging one or more Internet Service Providers (ISP) to provide ISP service on the city-owned and operated fiber optic infrastructure.    These efforts are detailed in the project?s Marketing and Outreach Plan attached in Section 13. of this application.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The proposed project plan is to install fiber optic infrastructure to serve 9,040 households and businesses ? including a portion of a Tribal Designated Statistical Area ? within the Chico city limits. This project will also serve an adjacent unincorporated area with an identified need for broadband investment (see Project Map and Project Location Data). 
 The goal of this infrastructure is to provide a fast, reliable, and low-cost broadband connection to customers in predominantly disadvantaged communities who could not afford an available wireline connection otherwise. This project will offer a low cost plan at 100 (download speed) /20 (upload speed) Mbps for $40/month with a pricing commitment of 10 years to ensure benefits reach low-income residents without data caps. The City's ultimate goal is to offer a lower cost plan at 1,000 (download speed) /1,000 (upload speed) Mbps for $30 a month if costs allow. The City will also provide California LifeLine through a third party phone operator.    These critically important efforts will help bridge the digital divide and build equitable access to broadband. Letters from Butte County; a tribal government; local non-profits; and other stakeholders (see Section 14. Government and Community Support) demonstrate the recognized service need and tremendous benefits of this project.   The project plan includes a reasonable and achievable Deployment Schedule and construction timeline (completed in 18 months) including a schedule for permits and CEQA review, as applicable. It also includes robust marketing and outreach efforts to support broadband subscription targets. This timeline assumes the high likelihood that this project will be CEQA-exempt (see Section 19. CEQA). This schedule is supported by a reasonable budget that shows it will deliver speeds and services and be sufficiently robust to meet increasing demand for bandwidth. A 58 percent match of total FFA funds requested ? provided by various funding sources ? will further enhance project?s efforts.   Existing City-owned facilities and property will be leveraged to house active broadband equipment and the City will provide conduit for fiber optic broadband installation. The City will also purchase refurbished communication huts for this project. 
 No grant funding will be requested for Middle Mile facilities for this project. The City is in negotiations with the State of California to install one of its Middle Mile communication huts on City property.

The project may include deployment or leasing of fiber-optic infrastructure and equipment or other wired technologies and/or the deployment of fixed wireless small cells, mesh nodes, or other radio-based transmission equipment to deliver broadband service to end users. The County is requiring its private partners to design the infrastructure to ensure the minimum 100/100 symmetrical speeds to end users regardless of the last mile technology used.   The County is soliciting the specific services to execute this Project under the County?s Community Broadband Network Services Master Agreement and through three subordinate Work Order Solicitations for the scope of this grant. The proposal submission periods for all three Work Order Solicitations have closed. All three WOS have multiple proposals that are pending the County?s evaluation or successful contract negotiations with the high-ranked proposers. The WOS required detailed designs and bills of material. However, since these are active services procurements, the County is not able to disclose the specific details on the major infrastructure to be deployed until the Work Orders are executed.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The County is not able to provide an estimated breakdown of aerial and underground installation at this time. The County is conducting active Work Order Solicitations for the services related to the Community Broadband Networks and has not awarded any Work Orders at the time of this application. The County is not able to disclose the specific details on the aerial and underground infrastructure to be deployed until the Work Orders are executed.
Major Equipment Expenses: The County is not able to provide a summary of major equipment expenses at this time. The County is conducting active Work Order Solicitations for the services related to the Community Broadband Networks and has not awarded any Work Orders at the time of this application. The County is not able to disclose the specific details on the major infrastructure to be deployed until the Work Orders are executed.
Estimated project plan with major milest: Network construction for all Community Broadband Networks is currently planned to be completed by December 31, 2025. The County intends to utilize American Rescue Plan Act State and Local Fiscal Recovery Funds, and the federal funding requirements related to ARPA SLFRF require all project construction to be completed by the end of 2026. Section 4.2.5 of the County?s Work Order Solicitations for the Service Areas in this project have the following requirements:   ?Federal funding requirements related to the American Rescue Plan Act require all project construction to be completed by December 31, 2026. Thus, to allow for unforeseen delays, Proposers must plan to complete network construction by December 31, 2025. If a 2025 deadline is not able to be met, the Proposer must detail how it will guarantee completion prior to the federal deadline.?   The County is not able to provide any further information regarding an estimated project plan with milestones and construction timeline at this time. The County is conducting active Work Order Solicitations for the services related to the Community Broadband Networks and has not awarded any  Work Orders at the time of this application. Although the County is not able to provide a full project plan until all Work Orders are awarded, the County does not intend to proceed with Work Orders that is not able to show viable plans to complete in advance of the ARPA expenditure deadline of December 31, 2026, per 31 CFR 35.5(a).
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Using a public-private partnership approach approved the County Board of Supervisors, the County has solicited proposals from MSPs to deploy and operate last-mile broadband network infrastructure and to provide retail internet service in four designated Service Areas within Los Angeles County: East Los Angeles, South Los Angeles, the San Fernando Valley, and Southeast Los Angeles. The solicitations are currently awaiting evaluation or contract negotiation at the time of this application.   Due to the separate solicitations and large geographical area of the project areas, the County will be making multiple awards to one or more proposers. Thus, the type of service to be provided in the project area may be either wireline or wireless, or a combination of both.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: LCB Communications LLC (LCB)is proposing to build a hybrid network consisting of Middle-Mile and Last Mile fiber and fixed Wireless.  To complete this project, it is essential that LCB build middle mile infrastructure. LCB is looking to serve 580 households with Fiber to the Home (FTTH). LCB will also run Fiber to the Premise (FTTP) to 2 Anchor institutions and 3 businesses. LCB Communications has chosen a hybrid solution to maximize coverage while minimizing costs. The proposed Aromas San Juan Project has diverse terrain ranging from open valley to narrow steep canyons that are heavily wooded and are Tier 2 Fire Threat Districts.  LCB?s hybrid network will be capable of providing up to 5Gbps symmetrical service to fiber subscribers. The fiber deployed will be underground in the public right of way, there should not be environmental concerns as the equipment will be bored and placed in previously disturbed soil.  Need to complete. The network will potentially bring service to 890 Low-Incom Unserved and unserved Households and Anchor Institutions located within the unincorporated communities of San Juan Bautista, Aromas, Royal Oaks and Las Lomas. An outdoor rated telco cabinet will be placed in key locations along the path within the right of way (RoW) and will connect to an existing backhaul circuit. The outdoor rated telco cabinet will be connected to commercial power and have battery backup power. From the outdoor rated telco cabinet LCB will place conduit and fiber within the right of way using a horizontal directional drill. Hand holes will provide access for pulling and splicing fiber and will be placed every few hundred yards along the right of way in previously disturbed soil. At the end-user location, we will deploy fiber connected from the distribution terminal to a specific point at the location be it a home or a wireless access point. Homes will be connected to the fiber network via a pedestal which will be connected to the distribution terminal. We will perform in-house fiber installation in a neutral location at the home. LCB will place an integrated optical network (ONT) termination device with backup power. LCB would like to build a network that will stand the test of time. As such families along the fiber route will have a choice of 3 options, a 1Gbps, 2.5Gbps or 5Gbps. symmetrical solution.

 Applicant Entity Information

Company Applicant Name: County of Marin
Contact Position: Marin County Chief Information Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: The project will deploy some 18 miles of outside plant, including 17.8 miles of underground mainline fiber cable with strand counts between 12 and 864 fibers inside 19.7 miles of 1.25? and 2? SDR-11 HDPE fiber duct installed inside 16.4 miles of the ROW. A total of 509 underground vaults varying in size will be installed to provide access, slack storage, and to house the splice enclosures and the multiport service terminals. A total of 29.3 miles of pre-connectorized single fiber drop cable will be installed from the service terminal ports to the side of the serviceable locations and terminated in a slack storage box on the side of the structure. The project will deploy two serving locations to support Active Ethernet and Automated Open Access inside plant needs. The first serving node will serve as a new central office consisting of a 10? x 20? aggregate Telcom shelter with appropriate environmental controls set on a foundation with a diesel backup generator. This facility will terminate the first 864 fiber strands to be installed, with the capacity to terminate and serve over 3,000 additional terminations as needed in the future. This location will also house a ?provider edge? where service providers will ?plug into? the infrastructure to offer their services and packages. The automated open-access business model will reduce operational overhead by allowing self-provisioning and supporting auto payment / billing options. These services will be provided by a cloudlet environment installed inside this central office location. The second serving location will be a micro shelter or cabinet. This location will also be provided with back power and environmental controls. Initially a redundant symmetrical 10 Gbps connection will be used to connect these two serving locations. The Top of Rack (ToR) equipment used to link the two facilities are equipped with port speeds of up to 100 Gbps, enabling simple upgrades to 40 or 100 Gbps. A 1 Gbps customer access port and 1 Gbps CPE equipment will be installed at every unserved address inside the project area. No IRUs are required or relied upon for this project. The project relies on the Statewide open access middle mile network, Golden State Net for service provider middle mile connectivity. Due to uncertainty around when Golden State will complete Marin County Hwy 1 segment construction, some reimbursable expenses for Internet backhaul services for a period of 3 years are included in the total requested.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: This project will deploy a last mile Active Ethernet FTTP infrastructure that will make no less than 1 Gbps and up to 10 Gbps symmetrical services available to every unserved address within the project boundaries. The business model will be an ?automated? open access model. Subscribers will have multiple service provider options and packages presented to them in an online marketplace with unbundled pricing and real-time, self-service provisioning. This new fiber plant, including all ducts, cables, and vaults, will be installed underground, along local, county, and state roadways within the Dillon Beach, Tomales, and Muir Beach communities. All infrastructure will be installed within existing right-of-way and utility easements in developed areas where previous ground-disturbance activities have occurred. Underground conduit would generally be installed at a depth of 30 to 40 inches by using primarily boring, plowing, and occasionally trenching, depending on the location and local, state, and federal requirements. In some cases, to avoid existing infrastructure, new underground conduits may be required to be installed significantly deeper. This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the new underground facilities to be deployed are categorically exempt per CEQA Guidelines Section 15303, Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment, including utility extensions. All the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304, as the expected installation methods will consist of boring and plowing requiring little surface restoration. The project is expected to include only minor trenching and backfilling. All Filling of earth into such minor excavations will be performed with the materials previously removed, and in a manner compatible with the natural features of the site. Marin County has no existing digital infrastructure in the area but will utilize existing County properties and facilities to support the two new serving locations. Marin County has utilized the FFA unserved information, and is not requesting any change unless new information becomes available. Marin County is not seeking Ministerial Review for this application. Marin County is not seeking funding for any middle mile facilities with this project but intends to cross connect with Golden State Net as soon as it is available.

Contact Position: Parks, Recreation & Libraries Superintendent
Description of the major infrastructure:
Located at the base of the Sierra Foothills mountain range, Roseville is an incorporated small urbanized city with a population of approximately 153,000 residents. Fiber optic network cable will be extended from the City's existing fiber optic network to 22 school-aged childcare facilities, and to 5 major city recreational facilities. The new additions will be used to establish free internet service for students attending Adventure Club school-aged childcare programs, and for the general public who visit the major recreational facilities. The project will also include networking equipment for each of the 27 facilities that will facilitate the distribution of free Internet within the school-aged childcare facilities and in the outside areas of the recreational facilities. 
 Enrollment for the City?s 22 Adventure Club school-aged childcare facilities regularly exceeds 2000 students per year. Over the 25-year life of the fiber optic network, the project will help 50,000 students advance their K-5 education with free Internet. Also, regional parks and facilities including Mahany Park, Maidu Park, Pistachio Park and Johnson Pool see regular attendance of 120,000 visitors annually which includes vulnerable populations like the homeless (estimated city population of 240). Finally, the new Roseville Soccer Complex is a regional sports facility with an anticipated attendance of over 600,000 visitors annually. Providing free Internet service to all of these facilities helps all members of the public, young and old, and housed and unsheltered to learn and thrive in the new digital age.

upload/download service levels for modern 802.11 protocols, and within range of the WiFi access points both inside and outside school and recreation facilities. 3.       Construction details for preliminary CEQA information: a.       The purpose of this project is to provide free public Internet service to 22 school-aged childcare facilities, and 5 major recreation areas using the City?s existing fiber optic network ring which connects all traffic signals and most public administration buildings within the city. For each of the 27 proposed connections, construction will be performed between a nearby traffic signal light, and the target facility (childcare, recreation) being served. The path of construction will stay within city roadways, city driveways, city hardscapes, and city easements. To minimize construction impacts to city property, the preferred method of construction will be boring. Trenching will only be used when it is deemed necessary, and post-trenching roadway and driveway restorations will be subject to requirements prescribed by the City of Roseville Public Works department. 4.       Existing facilities that will be leveraged: a.       Twenty-two Contact Position: Policy Director
Description of the major infrastructure: We plan to deploy managed ethernet fiber to the premises network.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The construction will be 80% underground and 20% aerial.
Major Equipment Expenses: The major equipment and material expenses will be for electronics and outside plant. Electronics includes customer premises equipment (CPE), distribution network equipment, the core network and the installation of these components. We estimate the cost of these items will be $586 thousand for CPE, $52,000 for distribution equipment, $79 thousand for core equipment and $164 thousand for installation. The bulk of the cost of the network will be for the installation of outside plant, with $9.3 million for distribution plant and $2.0 million for drops.
Estimated project plan with major milest: By October 2023, CCSF?s Fiber-to-Housing (FTH) Program will provide free broadband Internet service to more than 100 locations with over 15,000 residential units throughout the county. Our experienced project team will leverage the FFA Grant Funding to extend this service to the unserved locations in Chinatown, Bayview, and Tenderloin neighborhoods over an 18-month period. These are the major activities involved in delivering broadband Internet service via the CCSF fiber network. 1.   Property Owner Outreach ? contact the property owners to inform them about the City?s Internet service program and seek their acceptance to provide right of entry for our teams to perform the needed construction work. San Francisco enjoys a great cultural diversity of owners in the target areas. We have multilingual outreach team members to engage with the communities we serve. Our experience with our current program is that not all property owners agree to have the service connected to their building. Property Owner Outreach to be completed 3 months from award. 2.   Site Assessment ? perform a technical survey of the site to determine the construction requirements to bring City fiber into the building, the state of the current communications wiring and infrastructure, and determine the best LAN solution to deliver Internet service to the residents. Site assessment to be completed 4 months from award. 3.   Schedule and Sequence ? add the assessed site to a phase of the project to optimize the use of construction resources and funding. Schedule and Sequence to be completed 5 months from award. 4.   Construction & Service Launch ? initiate and launch the execution of project phases per the developed schedule. The following key workstreams occur during construction and service launch: a.   Fiber construction ? plan and install the fiber cable required to connect the building to the City?s fiber network. b.  LAN installation ? plan and install the LAN equipment and wiring to deliver broadband Internet service to the building?s residents and tenants. c.   Service testing ? perform network quality and performance testing. d.  Resident outreach and service connection ? coordinate with the property managers to inform and connect the residents and tenants to the service. e.   Transfer to Support ? hand off the site and service for the building to the Dept. of Technology service team for on-going monitoring, service, and maintenance. The Construction and Service Launch will be completed 18 months after award.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The City & County of San Francisco?s (CCSF) Fair-Shake Internet service will deliver equitable, free internet service to affordable housing and unserved locations in Bayview, Chinatown and Tenderloin neighborhoods. These neighborhoods were chosen due to high concentrations of unserved locations and high socioeconomic need. The residents of these neighborhoods are primarily people of color and have been historically under invested. Fair Shake will build on the successful model of our Fiber to Housing (FTH) program which is currently offering free internet service to 15,000 units as the program seeks to reach all 43,000 affordable housing units.   In addition to residents, Fair-Shake will also serve small, disadvantaged businesses. COVID-19 has created particular hardships for small businesses with a disproportionate impact in low-income communities. Changing patterns of shopping have made necessary for businesses to have an online presence. The Fair-Shake Internet program will serve as a key driver of economic growth for small, disadvantaged businesses in our targeted areas. This initiative not only fosters entrepreneurship but also bolsters economic self-sufficiency in disadvantaged neighborhoods.   Fair-Shake will leverage CCSF?s existing 391 miles of fiber to provide internet service to each location in a ring configuration, so that each location will have a redundant connection with a diverse physical path beyond the drop. The network will provide an ethernet connection to each unit through access switches in each building and will supply customer premises equipment. The Fair-Shake fiber internet service will deliver fast and reliable service at 1000 mbps (upload and download speed). CCSF will serve as the ISP to Fair-Shake as well as FTH users. This initiative will not install middle mile fiber.   CCSF will utilize existing city-owned conduit and existing jointly owned poles whenever possible, and will rely on excavation only when necessary. There will be no cabinets or huts in the sidewalk or public right-of-way. Fair-Shake facilities will be underground, on utility poles or in existing buildings.  We anticipate that this project will be categorically exempt under CEQA.   The CCSF fiber team has the local knowledge and experience to best deliver internet service and support to disadvantaged and unserved communities. The program will rely on third party providers to offer voice telephone service. CCSF will contribute $2 million to deliver this vital service.

Application: Project Name: City of Fresno Last Mile Grant
Company Applicant Name: City of Fresno
Contact Position: Project Liaison/Program Administrator
Project Title: City of Fresno Last Mile Grant
Description of the major infrastructure:
This project will deploy a fully fiber network to the unserved and underserved locations within the City of Fresno. Fiber is the ideal technology for this projects. Its immense bandwidth capacity ensures future-proof connectivity, capable of handling the increasing demands of emerging technologies like 4K/8K video streaming, virtual reality, and IoT. Additionally, fiber is less susceptible to signal interference and provides lower latency, enhancing user experiences and facilitating critical applications such as telemedicine and remote work. Fiber technology will allow residents in Fresno to engage in our digital world.  
 The City has a substantial existing fiber network totaling more than 93 route miles of underground conduit and fiber cable that will provide the backbone and foundation for this project. New proposed fiber deployment will include both aerial and underground. All aerial fiber will be deployed on existing poles and will follow General Order 95. All buried fiber will be placed in existing ROWs and will not be constructed in any environmentally sensitive areas.  
 In addition, this network will leverage extensive existing fiber strands from CVIN/Vast Networks as part of the public private partnership. By combining 21 miles of CVIN/Vast fiber with the existing network, we dramatically increase the reach to unserved neighborhoods while minimizing new build and associated costs. This partnership will result in savings for the State. Leasing fiber follows the direction of the State Middle Mile Network?maximizing the benefits of a robust network while reducing capital expenditure. Almost the entire build will connect back to the City?s existing fiber assets.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: We anticipate this project will utilize 42,799 feet of new aerial fiber (22%), and 37,741 feet of new underground fiber (19.5%) to create a GPON fiber-to-the-premises network. The project will also utilize 112,992 linear feet of 12 fiber strands from CVIN/Vast Networks, secured through a 20 year IRU (58.5% of total).
Major Equipment Expenses:
This project has been designed for growth and efficiency?this is reflected in our selected equipment. While final engineering has not yet taken place, we do know our major expenses (physical equipment over $100k) will include new edge routers and core routers and the customer premises equipment.   
 Edge Routing: $160,000  
 Core Routing: $250,000  
 Access Network termination: $135,000  
 ONU/CPE: $191,520  
 Switches and spares will account for another $47,500. Construction costs incorporate both labor and materials and are calculated based on linear foot, as is standard in the telecom industry. These costs amount to $9,409,399. The remainder of our costs are for non-tangible items such as engineering, permitting, billing, firewalls, network software, etc.  
 All major equipment costs are subject to minor changes after final design engineering has been completed with FFA funds.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
We have developed a timeline that will allow our project to be completed within the required 18 month timeframe for CEQA exempt projects. Engineering work will take two business quarters, beginning Q1 2024 and ending Q2. This will be followed by permitting completed in in Q3 and Q4. The construction bid will take one quarter ? Q3 of 2024.   
 Construction work will take three business quarters, starting in Q4 2024 and ending in Q2 2025. Splicing will take place in Q2 of 2025.Service drops will follow and be completed within Q2. The final milestone is turn-up, which will happen in Q3 of 2025.  
 Broken down:  
 Design Engineering ? Q1-Q2 2024  
 HLD ? Q1 2024  
 LLD ? Q2 2024  
 Final Design ? Q2 2024  
 CEQA Review (exempt) - Q2 2024  
 Permitting ? Q3 & Q4 2024  
 Construction bid ? Q3 2024  
 Staking & Preconstruction ? Q4 2024  
 Backbone construction ? Q4 2024, Q1 & Q2 2025  
 Splicing and network connections ? Q2 2025  
 Grant funded service drop installation ? Q2 2025  
 Network Launch ? Q3 2025
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The City of Fresno funded the creation of a Broadband Strategic Plan through the City's general funds. In 2021, the City Council passed a resolution in support of broadband to all our residents. Subsequent to this, the City used LATA funds for design engineering to create a robust network to reach the unserved and underserved residents in our City. FFA funds are the next step in this important endeavor.   
 The City of Fresno is proposing to build a fiber-to-the-premises network to unserved areas within the city, which will bring internet speeds of 1 Gbps symmetrical. We will focus this project around the neighborhoods of Roosevelt, Mclane, Hoover, & Bullard. These neighborhoods have high concentrations of unserved and underserved households, MDUs, and anchor institutions. In total, we have identified 1,824 unserved locations that will be connected with FFA funding.  
 The project will deploy 80,540 feet of new fiber in a redundant network that connects and utilizes the existing 93-mile city owned fiber backbone. This project will also utilize 112,992 feet of fiber with a IRU from CVIN/Vast Networks. Our hybrid lease/build approach follows the economical direction taken by the state with their Middle Mile Network.  
 This project will connect to the state Middle Mile Network along Highway 41 in the northern part of the City. There are no middle mile builds in this project, though the City does recognize the importance of the open-access requirements for state-funded middle mile.   
 We will be seeking private partners for this project. A solicitation will be released seeking a network operator and a last mile service provider. The service provider will be required to follow specific guidelines on affordability. Cost is one of the greatest barriers to entry in our city. While many neighborhoods are deemed served, adoption rates are abysmally low. Fresno?s Broadband Strategic Plan identified high prices as a primary reason for this lack of engagement. To avoid this scenario, our ISP partner will be required to have a low-cost, high-speed plan that is available to qualifying households.   
 We do not foresee any CEQA issues in this build as it will be minor alterations in previously disturbed areas. The Fresno Planning Department has determined that the project will be CEQA exempt. Previous fiber deployments in the city have been exempt under Section 15301/Class 1 and Section 15303/Class 3. These same sections of the guidelines will be utilized for exemptions of this project.

Contact Position: Senior Vice President, Project Structuring & Investments
Description of the major infrastructure:
To minimize cost and to maximize the affordability of the network with careful consideration to the security, functionality, and aesthetic characteristics of the network, the infrastructure proposed for the broadband utility is a combination of an underground and above ground, all fiber network built for multi-tenancy and open access that will connect all of the residential and commercial units. 
 The network can also have Non-Building Access Points (NBAP) capacities at various locations to connect to IoT gateways and strategies supporting Electric- Vehicle charging station connectivity requirements, smart shelters and any additional smart community devices, if desired by Destination Crenshaw, City of Los Angeles or LA Metro. It will also consist of Wi-Fi wireless coverage extensions via light poles infrastructure to provide coverage for public Wi-Fi and future IoT services using a combination of LoRa, WIFI-6e and 5G protocols for multicarrier and slicing capabilities, as well are LoRa and NB-IoT sensor support for the requested public Wi-Fi along the Crenshaw Boulevard commercial corridor. 
 The broadband utility provides dark fiber infrastructure with excess capacity for future proofing of special needs that require dedicated fiber while minimizing the amount of electronics required to offer a data utility service, which is in essence, those active electronics that support an isolated and secure set of pipes that are meant to support and enable retail service providers as well as emerging smart city services and applications.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The breakdown of aerial vs. Underground is estimated to be 90% underground and 10% aerial.  Much of the Crenshaw district is not serviced with existing pole lines, but where they do exist for last-mile access, it is intended to utilize them to keep costs down and accelerate penetration rates.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses can be separated into two major categories, passive equipment generally referred to as outside plant (OSP) materials, and active equipment, also referred to as network electronics.   The total for OSP materials is $52,223,337, and the total cost for active equipment is $6,382,282.  A breakdown of these costs can be found on the estimated direct costs FFA template as submitted.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
The following are the assumptions built into our projected project timeline.   The timeline will commence at the end of the Feasibility Level Design (FLD) stage. The FLD phase is expected to begin in October and end by the end of December 2023. The FLD stage will include the identification of an engineering firm and the signing of a contract. Thus, starting the Low-Level Design (LLD) and permit application process on January 1, 2024. The LLD and permit application process is expected to take six months, from January 1 to mid-June, 2024. A year is allowed for the permitting process, involving four entities: the Bureau of Engineering (BoE), the LA Department of Transportation (Dot), the Department of Water and Electricity and the TTCTMC. It is assumed that there will be 3 rounds of evaluations of the permits between the engineering company and the Bureau of Engineering (BoE), which BOE predicts will take 30 days per round. There is an assumption that expedited fees will be paid for the permitting process with BoE to hasten the process. Construction is divided into four phases (A, B, C, D), each expected to last four and a half months. The timeline aligns with feedback from construction companies, stating that the construction process would take about a minimum of 1 year once permitting is in place. 
 In summary, the project start date is January 1st 2024, commencing LLD and permitting preparation, this will take 6 months. Permits will be submitted on June 17th 2024 with an expected 12-month processing time. Phase 1 construction will begin May 19, 2025 and each of the four phases will take 4.5 months, resulting in a project end date of October 2, 2026. Please see the GANTT chart submitted in support of our application.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Destination Crenshaw is a community-based nonprofit, working in close partnership with Plenary (the Applicant) to execute a community-owned fiber network. Since its incorporation as a nonprofit in 2017, Destination Crenshaw has been leading development of a 1.3 mile stretch of parks and open space, investing in local businesses, creating local jobs, and funding commissions for Black artists. Destination Crenshaw recognizes the importance of pairing these infrastructure investments with digital infrastructure, and it used a competitive RFP to solicit Plenary to execute this transformational digital infrastructure investment to build fiber. 
 Destination Crenshaw established the Digital Equity Zone to identify and serve areas in the Crenshaw community lacking sufficient access to reliable, affordable internet. In partnership with City of Los Angeles Council District 8 and the Bureau of Street Lighting, Destination Crenshaw has developed a plan to establish a Digital Equity Zone that will provide high-speed broadband connections to the Crenshaw District?s visitors, businesses, and residents. The plan aims to connect all unserved locations and the Greater Crenshaw neighborhood to a Fiber-to-the Premise network, leveraging existing infrastructure and deploying new assets where needed.  
 The project area encompasses 396 census blocks and is home to over 15,000 households?the majority of which are Black. Of the estimated 41,600 residents in the project area, 50% identify as Black, 42% identify as Hispanic or Latino, 3% are White, and just under 1% identify as Asian. Centered around LA?s most iconic Black commercial corridor, the Digital Equity Zone aims to connect residents and business owners alike to affordable, high-quality and reliable internet service.  
 In response to Destination Crenshaw?s RFP, Plenary is proposing a public-private partnership model with the community because of the critical importance of the asset in the context of Destination Crenshaw?s long-term vision. Plenary will build an open-access fiber network, to be 100% owned by the community, and Plenary will recover its investment through taking a percentage of the revenue from the network in its first twenty years of operations.. Because of the critical importance of the asset in the context of Destination Crenshaw's long-term vision to spur economic development for the residents and businesses of Crenshaw, enabling them to stay in the community and thrive as it grows.

Company Applicant Name: City of Fort Bragg
Contact Position: Special Projects Manager
Description of the major infrastructure: Digital network architecture is designed to bring between 1-10 Gbps Internet connectivity to all homes, businesses, public safety locations, and anchor institutions via a robust underground last-mile fiber distribution system. The project has three main infrastructure components: 1) Last Mile Distribution Systems; 2) Optical Electronics; and 3) Fiber to the Premise drops. The City will design, engineer, build and maintain the entire network of infrastructure by providing all the necessary equipment to light the last mile network.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
No ariel is proposed. One hundred percent (100%) of households and businesses (4017 locations) in the City of Fort Bragg will be passed via underground conduit and fiber. The network will have a centralized data center hut to host Internet electronics equipment that connects to 15 Distribution Areas (DA's). The data center hut will be supplied with Core Routing, Firewall, Maintenance Switches, and Optical Line Terminal equipment (OLT). The data center hut will have four-hours back-up power via batteries and will be further supported by a 35-kw standby emergency generator in the event of prolonged power outage. 
 The 15 DA's are equipped with XGS-PON splitter vaults located throughout the city. These DA locations will serve as the aggregated fiber locations where conduit, fiber cable, and fiber distribution terminals (splitters) serve each residence/business location. At each DA location, construction crews will place underground conduit and fiber wire connected from the DA vault to a fiber service terminal (flowerpot) connection point or near current premise utilities entrance point. Drops will be placed underground and installed service request. Extra conduit has been designed to the north, south, east, and west to facilitate future growth opportunities.
Major Equipment Expenses:
The major electronic expenses include the Core Router Network, Data Center Prep, and OLT XGS-PON Ports and Lasers. The electronics design incorporates two functions: 1) Core Routing; and 2) Optical Line Termination (OLT). Each of these provide the lighted path from the end-user to the Internet. We have solicited pricing from Juniper and Cisco for the core routers, and from Calix and ADRAN TA-500 for Optical Line Termination (OLT). 
 The project includes 254,890 linear feet of conduit. The design calls for HDPE SDR11 type conduit with each conduit at a diameter of 1.25-inch, 2-inch, or 4-inch for base infrastructure, and a third 2.5-inch conduit for future unknown needs.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
The build out schedule is dependent on a few factors including funding, material availability, construction crew availability, permits and weather. We anticipate 20 weeks using a two-crew approach to complete infrastructure. The timeline is based on the number of available workdays in a week, number of crews, and the production footage per day in terms of feet of conduit/cable placed (800 ft). Construction work will follow an "inside out" strategy, working from data center hut toward outlying areas. The design team has identified necessary construction permits and prepared traffic control plans to facilitate efficient construction timeline once a contractor has been identified. 
 Drops will be an ongoing process until targeted premises are connected. A separate "drop crew" will follow "construction crew", installing drops and ONT/RG (Optical Network Terminal with Residential Gateway for Wi-Fi) from distribution cables and MST. A technician will complete required in-premise wiring and place ONT/RG required for service activation. 
 Currently, the overall deployment schedule for this project has an assumed construction start date of June 2024 with a final completion date of June 2026, or a total of 36 months to reach targeted take rate.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The proposed project includes city-wide underground Fiber to the Premise broadband infrastructure. The network is designed to pass every residence, business, public safety location and anchor institution, offering 1-10 Gbps to 100% of premises. Conduit and fiber will be placed in existing right of way utilizing horizontal Directional Drilling. If trenching is required, work will be completed within existing right of way, back filled and sealed to match surrounding surface. The City has a large street resurfacing project taking place that will be coordinated to follow behind construction crew. 
 The network is designed to leverage existing AT&T backhaul, and California Middle Mile Infrastructure; both located along CA Route 1, which travels from the southern to northern boundaries of city limits.

Company Applicant Name: MCC Telephony of the West LLC
Contact Position: Sr. Director, Government Partnership Opportunities
Description of the major infrastructure:
For this project, Mediacom will deploy a Fiber to the Premises (FTTP) system. This system will initially be deployed as a 10G XGS-PON solution (the ?X? represents the number 10, the ?G? stands for gigabit, the ?S? stands for symmetrical, and the ?PON? stands for Passive Optical Networks). This system will be capable of delivering multi-Gigabit download and upload speeds. The optical line terminal (OLT) hardware Mediacom deploys is pre-built to support 25G PON, which allows for cost effective upgrades when there is a system need. When building a new FTTP network, we place an OLT facility in or near the primary service market, equipped with backup power and transfer switch capable of connecting a portable generator in extreme cases such as natural disasters. Each OLT will have fiber optic connectivity to clusters of homes and businesses, each with a fiber access terminal at the right of way of the connecting property. For homes or businesses that choose to become customers, Mediacom will build from that right of way point to the home or business and provide them with a fiber optic network terminal (ONT) at their premises. This style of connection will allow us to offer several different speed tiers, with our top tier being 2000 Mbps download and 1000 Mbps upload (2 Gbps Internet) with unlimited data. This network is equipped to handle multi-gigabit speeds that can be supported on an individual case basis up to and included a dedicated XGS-PON 8.5 Gbps service. At the customer premises, the customer will have access to managed Wi-Fi services to ensure whole-home coverage. Business customers will have access to our full suite of business products including private network connectivity both locally and nationally. This means that the network we are launching in this area will be something that can last many years into the future without major infrastructure changes.   This project will interconnect with our existing fiber system that runs throughout California and our other twenty-one served states.   This new infrastructure in this project will bring 2,000 Mbps download and 1,000 Mbps upload fiber to the targeted service area. This FTTP system will reliability deliver well over the required minimum of 100 Mbps symmetrical speeds to every home, business, or community anchor in this grant application. 
 We are confident in this because of the style of network being delivered and the engineering on the backend.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: For this project, we estimate that 86% of this build will be aerial and 14% of the build will be underground.
Major Equipment Expenses: For this project, the primary equipment expenses area in the fiber, conduit, switch & routing, and headend. This includes the fiber that will be run, conduit for the locations that must be underground, the XGS-PON equipment, fiber cabinets, switching equipment, power supplies, ONT equipment, and gateways.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
For this project, all dates will be contingent upon approval of the grant and execution of the contract. Following that, we expect the timeline to be: 
 Phase 1 - Months 1-5 ? Walkout, Design, and Engineering ? Verify the design and walkout, make any necessary engineering changes. 
 Phase 2 - Months 3-5 ? Permitting & Preconstruction ? We will start applying for permits as applicable, review all necessary make ready, prepare materials and bid for contracting crews. 
 Phase 3 ? Months 3-24 ? State Regulations ? We will complete all necessary requirements for state regulations required for the build. 
 Phase 4 - Months 5-42 ? Construction ? Physical construction of the plant. This will be done in cabinet phases so only segments of the project will be under construction at a time. All underground and aerial construction will take place, including all required pole work. We will only work in areas where all permitting and state regulations are clear for us to preform work. 
 Phase 5 - Months 12-42 ? Cabinet Placement & Activations ? As cabinet areas are completed, we will activate cabinets and customers in order to fully serve the area segment by segment. This will run concurrent with construction in order for customers to be connected while construction in other areas continue. 
 Phase 6 - Months 42-48 ? Grant Closeout ? We will submit all required paperwork, invoices, material logs, and all other details to the state in order to close out the project and seek reimbursement.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: For this project, we are proposing an XGS-PON Fiber the Home (FTTH) technology that will provide end-users speeds of up to 2,000 Mbps download and 1,000 upload. This plant will be 86% aerial and 14% underground, attaching to existing pole infrastructure in the area to minimize disruption of land and natural resources in the area. The 14% underground area is only for areas where we power and other utilities are underground and there are no aerial poles to attach to. For all underground areas, we will use directional boring in order to be as accurate and less disruptive to the area as possible. The areas that are disturbed by the directional bore will be fully restored following the completion of the undergrounding work.

Company Applicant Name: Placerville, City of
Contact Position: Sr. Management Analyst
Description of the major infrastructure: The project will deploy some 32 miles of outside plant, including 27 miles of underground mainline fiber cable with strand counts between 12 and 864 fibers inside 40 miles of 1.25? and 2? SDR-11 HDPE fiber duct installed inside 22 miles of the ROW. A total of 572 underground vaults varying in size will be installed to provide access, slack storage, and to house the splice enclosures and the multiport service terminals. A total of 19 miles of pre-connectorized single fiber drop cable will be installed from the service terminal ports to the side of the serviceable locations and terminated in a slack storage box on the side of the structure. The project will deploy two serving locations to support Active Ethernet and Automated Open Access inside plant needs. These serving nodes will consist of 10? x 20? aggregate Telcom shelters with appropriate environmental controls set on foundations with diesel backup generators. These facilities will terminate the first 864 fiber strands to be installed, with the capacity to terminate and serve more than 2,400 additional terminations as needed in the future. These locations will also house a ?provider edge? where service providers will ?plug into? the infrastructure to offer their services and packages. The automated open access business model will reduce operational overhead by allowing self-provisioning and supporting auto payment / billing options. These services will be provided by a cloudlet environment installed inside these locations. Initially a redundant symmetrical 10 Gbps connection will be used to connect these two serving locations. The Top of Rack (ToR) equipment used to link the two facilities is equipped with port speeds of up to 100 Gbps, enabling simple upgrades to 40 or 100 Gbps. A 1 Gbps customer access port and 1 Gbps CPE equipment will be installed at every unserved address inside the project area. No IRUs are required or relied upon for this project. The project relies on the Statewide open access middle mile network, Golden State Net for service provider middle mile connectivity. Due to uncertainty around when Golden State will complete Placerville City Hwy 50 segment construction, some reimbursable expenses for Internet backhaul services for a period of 5 years are included in the total requested.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The project is 100% or completely installed underground. This supports the State?s undergrounding priorities for essential infrastructure and is important due to PG&E?s plan to underground their infrastructure and remove the poles in Placerville. Underground construction costs are high in Placerville due to the bedrock conditions.
Major Equipment Expenses: Project major equipment expenses include console servers (x2) for out of band remote management), routing equipment (x1), provider edge switching (x1) and redundant (x2) 100 Gbps core switch equipment compatible with both in band and out of band management and Software Defined Networking (SDN) functions. This core network equipment comprises approximately $80K of the equipment budget and will be housed in the central office facility. A small, compute and storage, half rack, cloudlet will also be installed to support automated open access functions and network performance monitoring and alarming. The cost of the hardware required to support this environment comprises approximately $40K of the equipment budget. Distribution and access (x9) switching make up another $16K in equipment costs with the CPE devices adding $165K to equipment costs.
Estimated project plan with major milest: This project leverages the architecture, design, cost estimates, and project plan developed using Placerville?s LATA funding. Placerville?s plan is to contract with a third party to assist with project management, quality assurance inspections, and plant ?as built? documentation. Project start: 11/01/2023 ? DescProposedBroadbandPlan: This project will deploy a last mile Active Ethernet FTTP infrastructure that will make no less than 1 Gbps and up to 10 Gbps symmetrical services available to every unserved address within the project boundaries. The business model will be an ?automated? open access model. Subscribers will have multiple service provider options and packages presented to them in an online marketplace with unbundled pricing and real-time, self-service provisioning. This new fiber plant, including all ducts, cables, and vaults, will be installed underground, along local, City, and state roadways within the City of Placerville?s boundaries. All infrastructure will be installed within existing right-of-way and utility easements in developed areas where previous ground-disturbance activities have occurred. Underground conduit would generally be installed at a depth of 30 to 40 inches by using primarily boring, plowing, and occasionally trenching, depending on the location and local, state, and federal requirements. In some cases, to avoid existing infrastructure, new underground conduits may be required to be installed significantly deeper. This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the new underground facilities to be deployed are categorically exempt per CEQA Guidelines Section 15303, Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment, including utility extensions. All the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304, as the expected installation methods will consist of boring and plowing requiring little surface restoration. The project is expected to include only minor trenching and backfilling. All Filling of earth into such minor excavations will be performed with the materials previously removed, and in a manner compatible with the natural features of the site. Placerville City has no existing digital infrastructure in the area but will utilize existing City ROW, properties and facilities to support the two new serving locations. Placerville City has utilized the FFA unserved information and is not requesting any change unless new information becomes available. Placerville City is not seeking Ministerial Review for this application. Placerville City is not seeking funding for any middle mile facilities with this project but intends to cross connect with Golden State Net as soon as it is available.

Description of the major infrastructure:
CRIT is committed to ensuring that 100% of the unserved households in the proposed service area receive access to high quality broadband. CRIT estimates that 99% of the service area is currently unserved: 
 ·        The Riverland Route:  The Riverland Route is a critical component of this broadband project, featuring a combination of underground and aerial installations. Serving as a vital link connecting the Big River segment and extending northward to the intersection of Parker Dam Rd and Hwy 62, this route is poised to bring enhanced connectivity to the forefront. With the potential to serve 314 residences, 7 businesses, and 1 anchor institution, it holds the promise of optimizing data flow. This optimization lays a foundation for technological advancements, offering the potential for telecommuting, online learning, and broader access to essential resources. As a result, this initiative is expected to significantly improve the overall quality of life for residents in the area.  ·        The Parker Dam Route:  The Parker Dam Route represents another pivotal element of the proposed broadband deployment. Distinguished by its utilization of both aerial and underground installations, this route is designed to support backbone fiber, distribution fiber, and FTTP infrastructure. With the potential to serve 744 residences, 15 businesses, and 4 anchor institutions, it signifies a substantial leap forward in terms of connectivity. Moreover, this route has the unique potential to provide high-speed internet access directly to the dam itself, thereby offering critical infrastructure support. Additionally, the incorporation of redundancy in future projects will ensure sustained and reliable connectivity for the entire area.  ·        The Blythe Route :   The Blythe Route is engineered as a redundancy ring with a focus on minimizing impact on residential areas. This route commences from Palo Verde Dam, extending southwest to Defrain Blvd before proceeding southward and intersecting with 6th Avenue. From this point, it turns eastward, traversing 6th Avenue and leading to Lovekin Blvd. Continuing southward, it eventually reaches Hobsonway. In the denser urban sections of Blythe, underground installation is deemed necessary, while the remaining sections are set to be entirely aerial. Notably, this route incorporates four anchor locations, including Palo Verde College and local high schools, positioning itself as a vital part of the region's digital infrastructure.
