DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Key technical details of the proposed plan include: Download Speed Capabilities : The XGS-PON technology employed in the project enables remarkable download speeds, with a maximum line rate of 9.95328 Gbit/s. This ensures that end-users will have access to lightning-fast internet connectivity, capable of supporting bandwidth-intensive activities such as streaming, online gaming, and high-definition video conferencing. CRIT will provide up to 1GB download speeds to unserved households. Upload Speed Capabilities : The XGS-PON technology also provides outstanding upload speeds, matching the download speed capabilities with a maximum line rate of 9.95328 Gbit/s. This symmetrical speed ensures that users can seamlessly upload large files, engage in video conferencing, and participate in other real-time online activities with minimal latency. CRIT will provide up to 1GB upload speeds to unserved households. Construction Detail and Environmental Considerations : The project description includes comprehensive construction details, including the methods and techniques to be employed for infrastructure deployment. In cases where trenching is required, the application outlines the restoration procedures post-trenching to mitigate any environmental impact. It underscores the commitment to environmental stewardship and compliance with relevant regulations, thereby ensuring the responsible execution of the project. Leveraging Existing Facilities : The project strategically leverages existing facilities wherever possible to optimize efficiency and minimize infrastructure duplication. This approach not only reduces deployment costs but also aligns with sustainability principles by making the most of available resources. Middle Mile Facilities Necessity : The inclusion of Middle Mile facilities in the proposed project is essential for accessing the last-mile infrastructure within the reservation. These Middle Mile components serve as the backbone of the network, facilitating the efficient aggregation and transport of data to and from customer premises. Without these facilities, the deployment of reliable last-mile connectivity would be challenging. Acceptance of Open Access Requirements : The project embraces the open access requirements for any Middle Mile facilities. This commitment to open access ensures that multiple service providers can utilize the Middle Mile infrastructure, promoting competition and choice for end-users.

Company Applicant Name: Round Valley Indian Tribes
Contact Position: Tribal Administrator
Description of the major infrastructure:
RVIT plans to overbuild the existing Frontier owned middle-mile fiber line from Laytonville to Covelo and then to complement the existing NTIA project which consists of some fiber direct to tower sites and mostly wireless systems by deploying a fiber-to-the-home system. RVIT has made every effort to establish a viable partnership with Frontier to no avail. RVIT has requested Frontier follow the appropriate tribal authorization and permitting process to build on tribal territory/ancestral lands, though they have not followed through. Frontier has not been granted authorization to build in the Covelo Valley and Frontier has stated they will not be building on tribal lands. RVIT requires reliable partners who have the best interest of the tribe and its citizens in mind. The fiber system will consist of 144 strand fiber along existing rights-of-way in previously disturbed areas on tribal trust land and ancestral lands. The fiber will be mostly installed underground. The major equipment to be deployed is designed for a 10GBps network offering reliable, high-speed services. This project will interconnect with the State-wide middle-mile initiative and, in a similar fashion offer open-access services to local providers.   This project will implement an optimal fiber design for a 10GB network to the home in a community prioritized high performance, reliability, scalability, and ease of maintenance while taking into account community needs and regulatory requirements; and will provide, at minimum, 100Mbps/100Mbps service to end users.  Trenching or plowing is the preferred method of underground installation. Trenching creates a 1-to2-foot wide disturbance area at the bottom of the ditch and requires careful backfill and compaction after placing the conduits. Directional drilling is used to cross underneath a road at a shallow angle to avoid cutting the pavement. It is also used to avoid impacts on infrastructure such as culverts, water lines, and specific sensitive resources such as old-growth tree roots and archaeological sites. 
 RVIT makes the following commitments: Interconnect with the state-wide middle-mile fiber project by GoldenState Net Offer a low-cost plan at 50/20+ for $40 a month + Provide matching funds totaling $14,999,363.58 Identify gap funding - the Tribe?s plan is to use tribal general funds but given the impact it could have on the tribal community, it has requested for a waiver Reach all unserved homes in the census block
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: This project will be 94% underground with 6% of the fiber run coming as a backbone into the Covelo community over existing fiber poles.  This breakdown will be verified once formal engineering has been completed.  We expect a minimum of 85% of trenching and 15% of directional drilling.  The middle-mile sections of the project will likely be overhead with the last-mile sections being underground.
Estimated project plan with major milest: This is a 24-month project anticipated to commence in March/April 2024 upon being awarded. The initial phase involves project planning and preparation, including project kickoff meetings with the tribal council and relevant tribal departments. A legal review of contractor contracts will also be conducted during this phase. EnerTribe will establish the project timeline using project management tools, compile a project contact list and arrange regular project management calls. The planning phase is projected to conclude within two months. The subsequent significant task is outreach to tribal governments, agencies, and providers. Collaboration with the Tribal Council and relevant tribal departments, such as THPO, Land Management, and Realty, as needed, is crucial. Outreach efforts will commence at the project's outset and continue throughout, concluding before or during the construction phase.  Following this is the permitting process, which initiates with a thorough assessment and early, ongoing engagement. Securing federal, state, and county support and approval is vital. The primary focus of the permitting endeavor will be to complete the project description for NEPA and CEQA review processes. Permitting may span up to 18 months.  Fiber engineering activities will commence early in the project's lifecycle, encompassing site surveys and engineering, a process lasting approximately 6 months. Equipment procurement will follow upon the completion of fiber engineering. This stage involves creating a Bill of Materials (BOM), obtaining quotes, obtaining tribal council approval for purchases, placing orders, and considering lead times for delivery, estimated at 8 months in the project schedule. Fiber construction is allocated a 5-month timeline, hopefully to be finalized before winter. Post-fiber construction activities involve testing electronics and conducting speed tests to ensure operational efficiency, a phase scheduled for 2 months. Finally, the creation of an as-built record of the entire FTTH project will be established for future reference. The compilation of the as-built record occurs at different stages of the project to ensure comprehensive data capture.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: RVIT will overbuild the existing Frontier owned middle-mile fiber line from Laytonville to Covelo and complement the NTIA project which consists of some fiber direct to tower sites and mostly wireless systems by deploying a fiber-to-the-home system. RVIT has requested Frontier follow the appropriate tribal authorization and permitting process to build on tribal territory/ancestral lands, and create viable partnership, though they have not followed through. The project will make use of the latest technology for FTTH deployments and meet the needs of tomorrow. The plan is to build a 10GB network to the home to prioritize high performance, reliability, scalability, and maintenance while considering community needs and regulatory requirements, it will account for 100GB in the near future. The project will deploy 144 count fiber or larger and tie it into the fiber backbone built in NTIA. GPON technology and optical splitting will ensure fiber management is primary and residences are served. Where underground installation is used, additional conduit will be installed as an upgrade path. Customers will be provided broadband speeds of as much as 100Mbps upload and 100Mbps download.  This project will interconnect with the State-wide SB-156 middle-mile initiative and offer open-access services to local providers. There are existing facilities and telecommunications huts in this region that will be used for housing networking equipment. Trenching or plowing is the preferred method of underground installation. Trenching creates a 1-to2-foot wide by 36? deep disturbance for fiber conduit.After trenching, aggregate base and/or native soil will be filled in. Trenching occurs in ditches and existing rights of way to avoid sensitive areas, vegetation disruption is limited and soil will be restored to its state before disruption. Directional drilling is used to cross underneath a road at a shallow angle to avoid cutting the pavement. It is also used to avoid impacts on infrastructure such as culverts, water lines, and specific sensitive resources such as old-growth tree roots and archaeological sites. The RVIT FTTH project will provide fiber to underserved/unserved homes in RVIT.  Broadband connectivity is a vital enabler of economic development in the community. It bridges the digital divide, brings educational opportunities, telehealth services, preserves cultural heritage, and enhances communication during emergencies and disasters.

Company Applicant Name: Fort Bidwell Indian Community
Contact Position: Tribal Administrator
Description of the major infrastructure: This project will be a last mile project providing high speed broadband fiber to the home (FTTH) to homes and businesses residing in the project area.  There is a middle component to interconnect each town.  The project will provide, at minimum, 100Mbps/100Mbps service to end users and a maximum of 1GB service to the communities of Fort Bidwell, Lake City, Cedarville (and Cedarville Rancheria) and Eagleville.  Fort Bidwell Indian Community (FBIC) plans to deploy a 144 strand, mostly underground, 10Gbps, redundant network that interconnects with the SB156 Statewide Middle Mile initiative with Goldenstate Net in Cedarville.  The length of the FTTH project is estimated at 1,570,800 ft. The goal of the project is to make it as future proof as possible by installing a high strand count and an additional conduit; one for an upgrade path and one for diversity.  Additionally, future proofing will be considered for the networking components by installing redundant networking communication huts in the towns of FBIC, Cedarville, and Lake City. Additionally, this last mile project will interconnect with the middle mile initiative in Cedarville, but additional middle mile fiber optics will be brought over from Highway 395 to Fort Bidwell by Modoc County.  Modoc County has agreed to do this in exchange for FBIC doing fiber to the home in Eagleville.   The Tribe has chosen, EnerTribe, a native American telecommunications infrastructure company with an accumulated experience of 50+ years in tribally chartered broadband infrastructure projects.  EnerTribe has worked for hundreds of Tribes and helped manage over a half billion in infrastructure projects over the course of the last 15 years.     The Tribe makes the following commitments: Interconnect with the state-wide middle-mile fiber project by GoldenState Net in Cedarville Offer a low-cost plan at 50/20+ for $40 a month  Provide matching funds totaling $650,000 Identify gap funding - the tribe?s plan is to use tribal general funds but given the impact it could have on the tribal community, it has requested for a waiver Reach all unserved homes in the census block

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The project will make use of the latest technology for FTTH deployments and in an effort to meet the needs of tomorrow. The current plan is to build a 10GB network to the home and will prioritize high performance, reliability, scalability, and ease of maintenance while considering community needs and regulatory requirements; it will also account for 100GB in the near future. The project will deploy 144 count fiber or greater with an additional conduit installed as an upgrade path for future use. Customers will be provided broadband speeds of as much as 100Mbps upload and 100Mbps download. GPON technology and optical splitting will ensure fiber management is primary and all of the residences are served.  The entirety of this project is proposed to be underground using trenching and Directional drilling. If any aerial fiber must be used it will be permitted across existing pole. Engineering plans will be proposed and will comply with the permitting requirements of Local, tribal and NEPA/CEQA as needed. Trenching occurs in ditches and existing rights of way to avoid sensitive areas. These ditches may be full of vegetation and rocks and must first be cleaned out. This is the only vegetation removal needed for underground installation. No mature trees will be removed, though seedlings established in and blocking the drainage ditch may be removed. A Trench, typically 6?-2? wide and down 36 to 42 inches below the finish grade of the bottom of the ditch will be dug. Aggregate base and/or native soil will be backfilled to restore the disturbance. Directional drilling is used to cross underneath a road at a shallow angle to avoid cutting the pavement. It is also used to avoid impacts on infrastructure such as culverts, water lines, and sensitive resources such as old-growth tree roots and archaeological sites.  
 This project will interconnect with the State-wide SB-156 middle-mile initiative and, in a similar fashion offer open-access services to local providers. There are existing facilities and telecommunications huts in this region that will be used for housing Networking equipment. The middle-mile section of the project is required to interconnect the communities of Fort Bidwell, Lake City, Cedarville and Eagleville. The middle-mile portion of the project is also required for the tribe to meet the State-wide middle-mile fiber initiative in Cedarville. The Tribe commits to providing an open access network for other providers to also make use of.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Spectrum Pacific West, LLC on behalf of its affiliated entities Charter Fiberlink CA-CCO, LLC (u-6878-C; Time Warner Cable Information Services (CA) LLC (U-6874-C), and Bright House Networks Information Services (California), LLC (U-6955-C) (hereinafter "Charter" or "Applicant") is applying for a grant of $6,616,050.98 from the California Advanced Services Fund (CASF) Federal Funding Account. The proposed project, as described below, will enable High Speed Internet services as well as Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) to a total of 868 CASF-eligible serviceable locations. The $6,616,050.98 represents 75% of the projected cost of delivering service to the CASF-eligible locations. The additional cost of the Project in the amount of $2,170,000.00 will be paid for by Charter. Charter requests a grant to construct a last-mile FTTP EPON network to provide service to unserved and underserved households in Lake Matthews (the ?Project? or "Project Area"). FTTP EPON is a standardized and mature model operated by Charter across the country, engineered and managed to meet 1 Gbps symmetrical speeds, and typical median latency of approximately 26 milliseconds. These high bandwidth and low latency attributes mean that Charter?s FTTP EPON deployment is suitable for, among other uses, remote learning, remote working, and telehealth. FTTP EPON can accommodate numerous members of a household or small business simultaneously using bandwidth-intensive applications, such as video conferencing, telemedicine, video streaming, gaming, virtual or augmented reality sessions, and numerous other applications. New customers in the Project Area will have access to several Internet speed options, including Charter?s ?Spectrum Internet? with a standard starting speed of up to 300/10 megabits per second (?Mbps?) and Charter?s ?Internet Gig? service at up to 1000/1000 Mbps (1000 Mbps is equivalent to 1 Gbps). Additional services that will be available to Charter Internet customers include, but are not limited to, antivirus security protection and in-home WiFi. As an added benefit of our FTTP EPON network, Charter can also offer competitive video, mobile, and voice services in the Project Area.

Company Applicant Name: Frontier of California Inc.
Contact Position: Vice President - Grant Administration
Description of the major infrastructure:
Frontier will deploy best-in-class xPON - Gigabit Passive Optical Network (?GPON?) (ITU-T G.984) and XGS-PON (ITU-T G.9807.1). Frontier currently uses xPON architecture throughout its network and has already made FTTP available to more than 5.2 million consumers across its 25-state footprint, including to homes and businesses in California. 
 The project will place 296.97 miles of planned fiber and will utilize 2 central offices to connect 2,544 locations with premier Fiber-To-The-Premises (?FTTP?) symmetrical services. This project involves 100% last mile fiber deployment. 
 The deployed network technology is 10 Gbps-capable, and currently supports symmetrical 5 Gbps residential broadband speeds. In addition, as reflected by the publicly available results of the FCC?s Measuring Broadband America Program, xPON readily meets speed and latency requirements (i.e., <100 ms latency). (See generally FCC, Measuring Broadband America (last accessed Jan. 30, 2021).   In fact, according to Ookla Speed test results for the 2nd quarter 2023, Frontier?s fiber network ranked #1 in California for Top-Rated Internet, Fastest Internet Upload Speeds, and Lowest Internet Latency. (See Frontier Wins Again for Its Speeds and Low Latency ? Frontier Newsroom .) Accordingly, the network to be deployed readily supports high bandwidth applications in use today like video conferencing, telehealth services, and gaming, as well as emerging augmented/virtual reality applications that are increasingly a part of daily living and a premium broadband service experience. 
 In short, Frontier is deploying FTTP?the most scalable, best-in-class broadband infrastructure?which is designed to accommodate ever-increasing bandwidth requirements and quality demands, and lower response/latency demands for ever-increasing usage of highly interactive applications.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: It is estimated that this project will be approximately 50% aerial and 50% underground installation.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses for this project include power to central offices, optical line terminals, fiber distribution panels, fiber cable, distribution hubs, and terminals to be placed on premises. A breakdown of estimated major equipment expenses is included in the Estimated Direct Costs attachment provided in Section 10.
Estimated project plan with major milest: The timeline for construction through project closeout is estimated to be approximately 18 months after final permit approval if the project is categorically exempt from CEQA or approximately 24 months after CEQA resolution approval. A detailed view of project milestones and construction timelines can be found in the Deployment Schedule section of this application.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Frontier proposes to deploy a last mile ?future-proof? FTTP network that will make 5 Gbps symmetrical service available to 2,544 locations in Fresno County. The project will provide 5 Gbps symmetrical speed, which is scalable to 10 Gbps or higher. Based on expansion capability of up to 10 Gbps (without the addition of system electronics), the project proposes to deploy infrastructure to meet current and future needs. The proposed xPON fiber network is capable of scaling to support 25 Gbps and 50 Gbps symmetrical speeds as capacity needs grow. Fiber installations would take place near the communities of Reedley and Sanger, with the southern extent of the project extending into Tulare County west of Dinuba. In general, proposed fiber routes are located along local roadways; however, some segments are on or adjacent to State Route 180. Refer to the attached topographic map. 
 Fiber would be installed using a combination of existing infrastructure, where available, supplemented by new underground conduit and/or aerial installations. New infrastructure would be installed within existing right-of-way and utility easements in developed areas where previous ground-disturbance activities have occurred. Underground conduit would generally be installed at a depth of 18 to 48 inches by either boring, trenching, or plowing, depending on the location and local, state, and federal requirements. In some cases, to avoid existing infrastructure, new underground conduits may be required to be installed significantly deeper.   Frontier anticipates the proposed project will either be categorically exempt from CEQA review, pursuant to CEQA guidelines 14 C.C.R. § 15301 regarding exemption for existing facilities and 14 C.C.R. § 15303 regarding minor modifications to existing structures; or will require development of a Mitigated Negative Declaration (MND). Final design and review of resources present will inform the level of CEQA review required for the project.   Frontier will leverage all existing infrastructure within the Right of Way, including existing poles, underground systems, and where Frontier currently has direct buried conduit and facilities.   Frontier is not seeking grant funding for any middle-mile facilities in this application.

New infrastructure would be installed within existing right-of-way and utility easements in developed areas where previous ground-disturbance activities have occurred. 
 Underground conduit would generally be installed at a depth of 18 to 48 inches by either boring, trenching, or plowing, depending on the location and local, state, and federal requirements.  Frontier anticipates the proposed project will either be categorically exempt from CEQA review, pursuant to CEQA guidelines 14 C.C.R. § 15301 regarding exemption for existing facilities and 14 C.C.R. § 15303 regarding minor modifications to existing structures; or will require development of a Mitigated Negative Declaration (MND). Final design and review of resources present will inform the level of CEQA review required for the project.   Frontier will leverage all existing infrastructure within the Right of Way, including existing poles, underground systems, and where Frontier currently has direct buried conduit and facilities.   Frontier is not seeking grant funding for any middle-mile facilities in this application.

Company Applicant Name: Frontier of California Inc.
Contact Position: Vice President - Grant Administration
Description of the major infrastructure:
Frontier will deploy best-in-class xPON - Gigabit Passive Optical Network (?GPON?) (ITU-T G.984) and XGS-PON (ITU-T G.9807.1). Frontier currently uses xPON architecture throughout its network and has already made FTTP available to more than 5.2 million consumers across its 25-state footprint, including to homes and businesses in California. 
 The project will place 36.33 miles of planned fiber and will utilize 1 central office to connect 304 locations with premier Fiber-To-The-Premises (?FTTP?) symmetrical services. This project involves 100% last mile fiber deployment. 
 The deployed network technology is 10 Gbps-capable, and currently supports symmetrical 5 Gbps residential broadband speeds. In addition, as reflected by the publicly available results of the FCC?s Measuring Broadband America Program, xPON readily meets speed and latency requirements (i.e., <100 ms latency). (See generally FCC, Measuring Broadband America (last accessed Jan. 30, 2021).   In fact, according to Ookla Speed test results for the 2nd quarter 2023, Frontier?s fiber network ranked #1 in California for Top-Rated Internet, Fastest Internet Upload Speeds, and Lowest Internet Latency. (See Frontier Wins Again for Its Speeds and Low Latency ? Frontier Newsroom .) Accordingly, the network to be deployed readily supports high bandwidth applications in use today like video conferencing, telehealth services, and gaming, as well as emerging augmented/virtual reality applications that are increasingly a part of daily living and a premium broadband service experience. 
 In short, Frontier is deploying FTTP?the most scalable, best-in-class broadband infrastructure?which is designed to accommodate ever-increasing bandwidth requirements and quality demands, and lower response/latency demands for ever-increasing usage of highly interactive applications.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: It is estimated that this project will be approximately 70% aerial and 30% underground installation.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses for this project include power to central offices, optical line terminals, fiber distribution panels, fiber cable, distribution hubs, and terminals to be placed on premises. A breakdown of estimated major equipment expenses is included in the Estimated Direct Costs attachment provided in the Proposed Project Expenditure section.
Estimated project plan with major milest: The timeline for construction through project closeout is estimated to be approximately 18 months after final permit approval if the project is categorically exempt from CEQA or approximately 24 months after CEQA resolution approval. A detailed view of project milestones and construction timelines can be found in the Deployment Schedule section of this application.

Company Applicant Name: Comcast Cable Communications Management, LLC
Contact Position: Vice President Regulatory Affairs - Californi
Description of the major infrastructure:
Comcast Cable Communications Management, LLC (?Comcast?) proposes to construct a fiber network capable of symmetrical service of 1 gigabit or higher speeds (the ?Project?) to the project area identified in section 3 (the ?Project Area?) which will include 36.5 miles of fiber, 0 number of hubs. This Project will not require any additional head ends. The network will be highly reliable, with low latency, and bandwidth can be scaled well beyond foreseeable needs. 
 The fiber network would be constructed to connect Comcast?s existing network to additional homes, businesses, and community anchor institutions (?CAIs?) in the Project Area. The newly constructed network will be capable of meeting or exceeding the level of service required by the California Public Utilities Commission?s (?CPUC?) Federal Funding Account (?FFA?) program, with rates and terms consistent with those offered by Comcast in California. Households, businesses, and CAIs in the Project Area would have access to Comcast?s full suite of products and services, which, in addition to broadband service, includes voice, video, and home security services.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the proposed Project. Final engineering, design, and construction for the Project will be conducted if Comcast is awarded the grant. Comcast anticipates that approximately 65% miles of the network will be installed above ground and approximately 35% miles will be underground.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses will include the following: conduit for new underground deployment; messenger strand for aerial deployment; fiber-optic cable; power supplies; vaults and pedestals; fiber-optic transmitters and receivers; Optical Line Terminal (?OLTs?); and all cabling and combiner equipment needed to connect network hubs to the new network.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the Project. Final engineering, design, and construction will commence upon CPUC approval. The initial tasks of design and construction will include project engineering and right of way preparation. This Project includes a combination of aerial and underground infrastructure as noted above. Comcast will, to the extent feasible, leverage its existing facilities near the Project Area. Based on Comcast?s initial assessment, Comcast anticipates it will be complete the Project, which is categorically exempt from CEQA, on a rolling basis within 18 months after CPUC approval is received. This means that Comcast will activate service as areas are completed so many locations will be able to access service earlier than the completion of the Project. Moreover, Comcast anticipates that it will complete 100% of the Project within 18 months after Comcast obtains all necessary permits. Comcast expects the following milestones: 10% of the Project Area to be complete in 4 months, 35% of the Project to be complete in 8 months, 85% of the Project to be complete in 16 months, with 100% of the Project completed in approximately 18 months. Comcast will use commercially reasonable efforts to complete construction within 18 months after CPUC approval. However, delays in the issuance of permits or other factors beyond Comcast?s reasonable control, such as extreme weather conditions, strikes, fires, pandemics and epidemics, embargoes, actions of civil or military law enforcement authorities, acts of God or other acts of legislative, judicial, executive, or administrative authorities may necessitate an extension of the construction completion date. See Attachment 9. Schedule for all Permit Approvals and Project Construction. 
 The chart below is for illustrative purposes only based on Comcast?s current understanding of similar proposed projects, contains assumptions based on current market dynamics, and is subject to change. 
 Date(s) Activity Month 0 Project Approved / Approval Issued Months 1-3 Finalized Design and Engineering Months 3-6 Municipal and Pole Attachment Permits Months 6-18 Construction and Make-Ready work 
 Total Estimated Project Completion = 18 Months
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
Comcast will build a fiber network capable of symmetrical service of at least 1 gigabit, serving unserved and underserved communities in the Project Area. This Project will provide service for approximately 476 locations (FFA Map) of which 476 are unserved (FFA Map), 0 are underserved (FCC Map), and 0 are served. Comcast notes there are discrepancies between the FCC Fabric Map and CPUC FFA Map, and Comcast can provide the methodology of providing these numbers upon request. Access to reliable, high-speed broadband services will enable Project Area customers to take advantage of essential online resources. See response to ?A description of the major infrastructure to be deployed? for technologies used. 
 The preliminary CEQA analysis shows all Project construction will meet one or more categorical CEQA exemptions and does not contain any environmentally sensitive areas. 
 Comcast plans to install approximately 36.5 total miles of broadband network infrastructure. For aerial installation, Comcast will follow GO 95 pole safety and loading requirements and obtain permits and traffic control for proper placement of aerial equipment. For underground installation, Comcast will follow the permitting jurisdiction?s requirements for underground, including obtaining permits and traffic control for access to manholes/handholes and operating underground equipment. Comcast will reasonably return the site to its original condition to the extent feasible following installation. 
 Comcast will leverage compatible and available facilities to support the Project, relying on existing utility poles in rights-of-way for aerial installation where attachments are permitted by the pole owner. Comcast will support make ready to condition poles, where feasible or required for attachment, and rely on its existing core network infrastructure (including hubs) to support the Project. 
 ? Open access requirements do not apply because Comcast only seeks FFA funds for last mile infrastructure and does not intend to interconnect with the open access statewide Middle Mile Network or any other provider?s infrastructure, but Comcast will keep evaluating middle-mile infrastructure opportunities available to the Project. See Application Section 5. Comcast will connect the Project to its core network in Concord using a newly constructed transport facility detailed in section 5.

This Project will not require any additional head ends. The network will be highly reliable, with low latency, and bandwidth can be scaled well beyond foreseeable needs. 
 The fiber network would be constructed to connect Comcast?s existing network to additional homes, businesses, and community anchor institutions (?CAIs?) in the Project Area. The newly constructed network will be capable of meeting or exceeding the level of service required by the California Public Utilities Commission?s (?CPUC?) Federal Funding Account (?FFA?) program, with rates and terms consistent with those offered by Comcast in California. Households, businesses, and CAIs in the Project Area would have access to Comcast?s full suite of products and services, which, in addition to broadband service, includes voice, video, and home security services.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the proposed Project. Final engineering, design, and construction for the Project will be conducted if Comcast is awarded the grant. Comcast anticipates that approximately 47% miles of the network will be installed above ground and approximately 53% miles will be underground.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses will include the following: conduit for new underground deployment; messenger strand for aerial deployment; fiber-optic cable; power supplies; vaults and pedestals; fiber-optic transmitters and receivers; Optical Line Terminal (?OLTs?); and all cabling and combiner equipment needed to connect network hubs to the new network.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the Project. Final engineering, design, and construction will commence upon CPUC approval. The initial tasks of design and construction will include project engineering and right of way preparation. This Project includes a combination of aerial and underground infrastructure as noted above. Comcast will, to the extent feasible, leverage its existing facilities near the Project Area. Based on Comcast?s initial assessment, Comcast anticipates it will be complete the Project, which is categorically exempt from CEQA, on a rolling basis within 18 months after CPUC approval is received. This means that Comcast will activate service as areas are completed so many locations will be able to access service earlier than the completion of the Project. Moreover, Comcast anticipates that it will complete 100% of the Project within 18 months after Comcast obtains all necessary permits. Comcast expects the following milestones: 10% of the Project Area to be complete in 4 months, 35% of the Project to be complete in 8 months, 85% of the Project to be complete in 16 months, with 100% of the Project completed in approximately 18 months. Comcast will use commercially reasonable efforts to complete construction within 18 months after CPUC approval. However, delays in the issuance of permits or other factors beyond Comcast?s reasonable control, such as extreme weather conditions, strikes, fires, pandemics and epidemics, embargoes, actions of civil or military law enforcement authorities, acts of God or other acts of legislative, judicial, executive, or administrative authorities may necessitate an extension of the construction completion date. See Attachment 9. Schedule for all Permit Approvals and Project Construction.

Description of the major infrastructure: Comcast Cable Communications Management, LLC (?Comcast?) proposes to construct a fiber network capable of symmetrical service of 1 gigabit or higher speeds (the ?Project?) to the project area identified in section 3 (the ?Project Area?) which will include 1.7 miles of fiber. This Project will not require any additional head ends.  The network will be highly reliable, with low latency, and bandwidth can be scaled well beyond foreseeable needs.      The fiber network would be constructed to connect Comcast?s existing network to additional homes, businesses, and community anchor institutions (?CAIs?) in the Project Area. The newly constructed network will be capable of meeting or exceeding the level of service required by the California Public Utilities Commission?s (?CPUC?) Federal Funding Account (?FFA?) program, with rates and terms consistent with those offered by Comcast in California. Households, businesses, and CAIs in the Project Area would have access to Comcast?s full suite of products and services, which, in addition to broadband service, includes voice, video, and home security services.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the proposed Project. Final engineering, design, and construction for the Project will be conducted if Comcast is awarded the grant. Comcast anticipates that approximately 0% miles of the network will be installed above ground and approximately 100% miles will be underground.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses will include the following: conduit for new underground deployment; messenger strand for aerial deployment; fiber-optic cable; power supplies; vaults and pedestals; fiber-optic transmitters and receivers; Optical Line Terminal (?OLTs?); and all cabling and combiner equipment needed to connect network hubs to the new network.
Estimated project plan with major milest: Comcast has completed the planning and preliminary engineering phase for the Project. Final engineering, design, and construction will commence upon CPUC approval. The initial tasks of design and construction will include project engineering and right of way preparation. This Project includes a combination of aerial and underground infrastructure as noted above. Comcast will, to the extent feasible, leverage its existing facilities near the Project Area. Based on Comcast?s initial assessment, Comcast anticipates it will be complete the Project, which is categorically exempt from CEQA, on a rolling basis within 18 months after CPUC approval is received. This means that Comcast will activate service as areas are completed so many locations will be able to access service earlier than the completion of the Project. Moreover, Comcast anticipates that it will complete 100% of the Project within 18 months after Comcast obtains all necessary permits. Comcast expects the following milestones: 10% of the Project Area to be complete in 4 months, 35% of the Project to be complete in 8 months, 85% of the Project to be complete in 16 months, with 100% of the Project completed in approximately 18 months. Comcast will use commercially reasonable efforts to complete construction within 18 months after CPUC approval. However, delays in the issuance of permits or other factors beyond Comcast?s reasonable control, such as extreme weather conditions, strikes, fires, pandemics and epidemics, embargoes, actions of civil or military law enforcement authorities, acts of God or other acts of legislative, judicial, executive, or administrative authorities may necessitate an extension of the construction completion date. See Attachment 9. Schedule for all Permit Approvals and Project Construction.    The chart below is for illustrative purposes only based on Comcast?s current understanding of similar proposed projects, contains assumptions based on current market dynamics, and is subject to change.     Date(s)             Activity  Month 0            Project Approved / Approval Issued   Months 1-3       Finalized Design and Engineering   Months 3-6       Municipal and Pole Attachment Permits   Months 6-18     Construction and Make-Ready work     Total Estimated Project Completion = 18 Months
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: Comcast will build a fiber network capable of symmetrical service of at least 1 gigabit, serving unserved and underserved communities in the Project Area. This Project will provide service for approximately 251 locations (FFA Map) of which 152 are unserved (FFA Map), 0 are underserved (FCC Map), and 99 are served. Comcast notes there are discrepancies between the FCC Fabric Map and CPUC FFA Map, and Comcast can provide the methodology of providing these numbers upon request. Access to reliable, high-speed broadband services will enable Project Area customers to take advantage of essential online resources. See response to ?A description of the major infrastructure to be deployed? for technologies used.      The preliminary CEQA analysis shows all Project construction will meet one or more categorical CEQA exemptions and does not contain any environmentally sensitive areas.      Comcast plans to install approximately 1.7 total miles of broadband network infrastructure. For aerial installation, Comcast will follow GO 95 pole safety and loading requirements and obtain permits and traffic control for proper placement of aerial equipment. For underground installation, Comcast will follow the permitting jurisdiction?s requirements for underground, including obtaining permits and traffic control for access to manholes/handholes and operating underground equipment. Comcast will reasonably return the site to its original condition to the extent feasible following installation.    Comcast will leverage compatible and available facilities to support the Project, relying on existing utility poles in rights-of-way for aerial installation where attachments are permitted by the pole owner. Comcast will support make ready to condition poles, where feasible or required for attachment, and rely on its existing core network infrastructure (including hubs) to support the Project.    Open access requirements do not apply because Comcast only seeks FFA funds for last mile infrastructure and does not intend to interconnect with the open access statewide Middle Mile Network or any other provider?s infrastructure, but Comcast will keep evaluating middle-mile infrastructure opportunities available to the Project. See Application Section 5. Comcast will connect the Project to its core network in Novato using a newly constructed transport facility detailed in section 5.

Company Applicant Name: Astound Broadband, LLC
Contact Position: VP of Expansion
Description of the major infrastructure: The proposed infrastructure consists of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network to provide high speed broadband services to 100% of the locations in the proposed project area. 33.3 miles of distribution fiber will be deployed using aerial and underground installation methods. Distribution fiber will be terminated at two existing central offices / access nodes or at existing fiber distribution points in the Astound network. All the active network equipment will be hosted in the existing central offices. Therefore, no remote terminals are considered. Construction of middle mile infrastructure is not required as transport will be provided through the existing backhaul at the central offices.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: The length of the proposed fiber infrastructure includes 28 miles (84%) of aerial installation and 5.3 miles (16%) of underground installation for a total of 33.3 miles.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major installation expenses are as follows: Underground fiber installation, which includes conduit boring and fiber pulling into the conduit. Aerial fiber installation, including strand installation, pole replacement, new pole installation, pole make-ready, and aerial fiber placement. Major equipment costs consist of: 2? HDPE Conduit Fiber optic cables Messenger strand and installation materials New poles Underground vaults Optical Line Terminal (OLT) cards and optical modules Splicing closures, splitters and distribution boxes
Estimated project plan with major milest: The project plan for network buildout targets a 15-month timeline for completion after Grant award. This project plan considers that 1) the proposed project is subject to CEQA categorical exemption, 2) there will be close collaboration with the Cities of Atascadero and Paso Robles to streamline permitting, and 3) industry wide delays in the supply chain must be considered. Based on the above, the estimated timeline and milestones are described below: Milestone 1: Permitting [Months 1 to 6] Milestone 2: Materials Procurement & Delivery [Months 3 to 8] Milestone 3: Conduit boring and vault installation [Months 8 to 9] Milestone 4: Pole make ready and strand installation [Months 10 to 11] Milestone 5: Fiber Cable installation [Months 9 to 12] Milestone 6: Fiber Splicing [Months 11 to 12] Milestone 7: Service Drops and Network Equipment Provisioning [Months 12 to 15] Please note that the timeline above is subject to change based on factors that are outside of Astound?s control such as unexpected delays in permitting, supply chain disruptions, extreme weather conditions, and other force majeure considerations.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The proposed broadband project will deploy a FTTP network to provide high-speed, reliable, and affordable services to 532 locations in Atascadero and Paso Robles. Among these locations, 201 are currently unserved, with 102 (51%) situated in low-income areas. The proposed technology for this deployment is the 10-Gigabit-capable symmetric passive optical network (XGS-PON), compliant with ITU-T Recommendation G.9807.1. XGS-PON offers a symmetric line rate of 9.95328 Gbit/s, shared efficiently among multiple users through optical splitters. This configuration ensures symmetrical download/upload speeds of up to 2 Gbps per subscriber. To achieve this, 33.3 miles of fiber infrastructure will be deployed, utilizing both aerial (28 miles) and underground (5.3 miles) installation methods. Astound anticipates categorical exemptions from CEQA based on the following criteria: Class 1 (14 C.C.R. § 15301): This exemption applies to modifications of existing facilities, such as aerial installation on existing poles, including pole replacement. Class 3 (14 C.C.R. § 15303(d)): It pertains to new construction or conversion of small structures, including new pole installations and the proposed 5-mile underground installation. Class 4 (14 C.C.R. § 15304): This category covers minor alterations to land, including the 5 miles of underground installation, mainly employing direct boring in previously disturbed land with appropriate surface restoration. Exceptional Cases: We do not anticipate exceptions to these categorical exemptions (14 C.C.R. § 15300.2) since we have not identified environmentally sensitive areas, scenic highways, or historical resources likely to be impacted by our activities. Final CEQA determinations will follow outside plant engineering and final network design. The project leverages existing facilities, including: Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E) pole infrastructure: Utilized for aerial installation. Central offices / aggregation nodes: Employed to store active network equipment. Existing backhaul connectivity: Utilized to optimize network efficiency. Regarding funding, Astound is not revising the requested area. Moreover, the project does not necessitate Middle Mile facilities, as the project will leverage existing backhaul at Astound's central offices. Consequently, the SB 156 open access requirements for Middle Mile facilities do not apply.

Application: Project Name: Gateway Cities Council of Governments Last Mile Broadband Project
Company Applicant Name: Gateway Cities Council of Governments
Contact Position: Chair, Economic Development Working Group at Gateway Cities Council of Governments
Project Title: Gateway Cities Council of Governments Last Mile Broadband Project
Description of the major infrastructure: The Gateway Cities COG is seeking $ 78,706,391.47 to construct a Fiber-to-the-Home network to connect unserved households and anchor institutions within South Los Angeles. This project will establish 525,783 feet of new fiber optic backbone cable across the region and interconnect with sections of the Statewide Middle Mile Backbone Network (MMBN) while simultaneously providing last-mile connection within the most critically unserved cities, communities, and unincorporated areas. The project will bring scalable broadband speeds to 4,254 unserved locations as determined by CPUC standards. This project contains 205,910 feet of underground fiber, 308,520 feet of aerial fiber, and upsizing of existing fiber of 11,352.. Underground routes will be laid within existing rights of way while all aerial routes will be attached to pre-existing poles. These routes will ensure redundancy and future capacity, and will include new and existing conduit within previously disturbed rights-of-way with a 288-strand fiber cable.  There will be interconnection points with the statewide Middle Mile Broadband Infrastructure (MMBI). Middle mile portions of this project will support the state?s open-access goals, meeting all open-access requirements outlined within SB156 and within the FFA Program Guidelines. Where possible, this network will be built specifically on redundant pathways to state MMBI. In August 2023, the California Department of Technology announced changes to the build-out schedule of the state MMBI (including in Phase I and Phase II). For Middle Mile infrastructure that is not included in Phase I and is essential for serving the most high-need & unserved areas, Gateway Cities COG & Entrust have coordinated with the California Department of Transportation for construction. Our potential ISP partners Onward and Sonic, can provide all end-user connections of this project (pending final agreements). Both ISPs can provide fiber services to the city and residents of the GCCOG. Both ISPs are designated as CLECs operating in California, and have distinguished track records in deploying both aerial and underground fiber infrastructure, serving hundreds of thousands of residents and empowering organizations with lit and dark services. A Letter of Intent detailing project responsibilities and expectations has been initiated with both Onward and Sonic. Sonic and Onward will allow other providers access to the backbone at reasonable rates, to ensure fair and equitable access.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial:
The proposed fiber routes for this project will consist of 308,520 feet of aerial fiber attached on existing poles, and 205,910 of underground fiber.    The aerial portion of this build comes to roughly 33% of the project total. These will be attached to existing poles. Necessary permitting will be obtained once grant funding is made available, prior to construction. 
 The underground portion of this build comes to roughly 33% of the project total. These will be installed through excavation and/or directional boring, with some laterals and service drops utilizing micro trenching. Underground portions of the build will be laid in existing rights of way within the city away from environmentally sensitive locations. As a result, we anticipate a categorical exemption under CEQA, but will undertake a full review once the design engineering is completed.
Major Equipment Expenses:
Overall, the major equipment and setup costs are expected to total $2,356,381 for the project's budget. We are seeking funding through this grant opportunity proposal. Our assumptions for these figures are derived from established components of broadband infrastructure and further crafted by experienced professionals at Magellan. 
 A detailed cost breakdown is as follows: 
 Edge Router: Manages connections between the providers' core network and the internet through dedicated internet access circuits - $160,000 Core Router: Aggregates traffic between optical line terminators (OLT) and distribution hubs - $250,000 Switches: Connects network segments and forms the foundation for network services - $22,500 Access Network: Low-strand count fiber lines ("drops") from the optical splitter to the optical network unit (ONU) at each subscriber location - $600,000 Software: Network equipment functionality and management software, inclusive of periodic updates - $10,000 Security: Hardware and software firewall to counteract malicious connections and safeguard the provider's IP address space - $60,000 Network Management: Encompasses customer provisioning, billing, operations, and network management software - $150,000 Spares: Essential replacement components, such as switches and routers - $25,000 Optical Network Units (ONU): The point of interface between the provider and customer networks - $446,670 NID Enclosure: A protective casing for network equipment, especially ONU, generally affixed to the customer's premises - $63,810 Residential Gateway (RG): A combined router and gateway for disseminating network access within residences or businesses - $223,335 RG Management: Software that extracts data from customer routers or gateways for the provider's network - $6,000 Billing: Software dedicated to setting up, monitoring customer accounts, and service billing - $50,000 Subscriber Provisioning: Systems and software for service activation or deactivation, incorporating installation orders and tech support - $25,000 Fiber Management: Software utilized to trace the precise location and status of fiber and associated assets - $7,500 Data Center and Backup Power: Equipment for each city - $502,866 Do note, the ultimate equipment costs and their detailed breakdowns may vary, pending final design engineering. This will be finalized by the start of Q1 2024.
Estimated project plan with major milest: The Gateway Cities Council of Governments (GCCOG) and ISP partners are committed to delivering unparalleled, affordable high-speed internet to the residents of GCCOG. Our proposal ensures widespread accessibility and bridges the digital divide, aiming for a future where GCCOG?s households can easily participate in teleworking and remote learning. We intend to adhere to the rough outline of timelines and major milestones as described below: FFA grant application: Q4 2023 Design engineering: Q4 2023 - Q1 2024 High level design: Q4 2023 Low level design: Q1 2024 Final design: Q1 2024 CEQA Review: Q4 2024 - We expect an exemption based on the nature of our project and its adhered to previously disturbed land and use of existing Right-of-Way Permitting: Q4 2024 and as required through the remaining phases of the project Construction bid: Q2 2024 Bid award: Q3 2024 Staking/preconstruction: Q4 2024 Backbone construction: Q4 2024-Q3 2025 Splicing and network connection: Q3 2025 Grant-funded service drop installation: Q3 2025 Major Milestones for Access: Sonic Estimated New Subscribers by Year: Year 1: 1029 Year 2: 1415 Year 3: 1716 Year 4: 1951 Year 5: 2080
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The 25 Gateway Cities and adjacent unincorporated areas of LA County are some of the United States? most densely developed and populated areas. With over two million residents and over 70,000 business establishments in the heart of Southern California, as well as the cities themselves and other public agencies, Gateway Cities are a prime market for broadband and related technology. The objective of this broadband plan is to efficiently meet the high-speed internet needs of the Gateway Cities, their residents, and businesses in the area. 
 This broadband plan identifies pockets within the Gateway Council of Governments region (GCCOG) that lack sufficient broadband infrastructure and upgrades needed to ensure its residents can thrive. It targets core network assets for public facilities and in low-income areas to promote digital equity and inclusion. Core network assets are the physical components from which networks are assembled: conduit, fiber cables, poles, towers, etc.  
 We anticipate 4,254 new passings to unserved locations. En route to the unserved locations, we will pass 12,247 other locations, and 20 anchor institutions and public safety facilities that will receive new and improved services. Subscribers to this new network will have access to broadband symmetrical speeds ranging from 50 Mbps/20 Mbps to 10 Gbps/10 Gbps to meet their unique needs and financial position through Onward or Sonic. This network will promote economic development across the city while supporting the essential community services like public safety, housing, education, and civic engagement.   While we anticipate this project to be categorically exempt from CEQA review, a thorough review will be completed by the County?s Planning Department (local CEQA authority) once design engineering is completed. This project will be constructed within existing rights of way, avoiding environmentally sensitive areas. The exact date of this review will be determined post award and prior to construction.  
 We recognize that within such a broad and densely populated region, the network developed as part of this project is one step among many to reach unserved and underserved locations. By focusing on public facilities in low-income communities, we seek to prioritize those in greatest need; however, the intent of our broader Broadband Master Plan is to provide robust broadband connectivity across the region. Infrastructure delivered through this portion of the plan will enable future regional projects.

Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: SECTION 3: ESTIMATED BREAKDOWN OF AERIAL AND UNDERGROUND INSTALLATION     Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low-Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections. 
 Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 
 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical.   3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .    No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements.  
 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project.  
 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. 
 ? 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4.
