Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: ·          Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·         Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical.   3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface. 
 No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design.   2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical.   3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements.   4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project.  5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. ? 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas

Application: Project Name: GSCA Del Norte County Broadband Network
Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Project Title: GSCA Del Norte County Broadband Network
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

Application: Project Name: GSCA Santa Barbara County Broadband Network
Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Project Title: GSCA Santa Barbara County Broadband Network
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: ·          Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.
Major Equipment Expenses:
GOLDEN STATE CONNECT AUTHORITY 
 Section 3: Major Equipment Expenses   Summarize major installation and equipment expenses such as fiber optic cables, hubs, routers, switches, etc.   Major equipment and installation expenses include:                Various strand densities of fiber optics                Conduit (HDPE)                Hut and Cabinet Configuration                Directional Bore 2? to 4? Conduit Pkg                Bore ? Rock Adder                Assorted traffic control material/personnel                Outside Plant Construction (OSP) personnel 
 For a complete list of major installation and equipment expenses with associated cost and useful life, please see Section 10: Proposed Project Expenditures ? Estimated Direct Costs.
Estimated project plan with major milest:
GOLDEN STATE CONNECT AUTHORITY   Section 3: Estimate Project Plan with Major Milestones and Construction Timelines   
 Milestone 1: Complete Low-Level Designs ·          Will complete up to 40 miles of low-level construction ready designs for selected project areas prior to grant award. ·          Remainder of low-level network design to continue concurrent with following Milestones 
 Milestone 2: Secure Necessary Permits ·          CEQA ? will secure prior to grant award. GSCA will be Lead Agency. ·          Execute MOU with local governments securing right-of-way access prior to grant award ·          Local Encroachment Permits ? will secure within 4 months of grant award ·          CalTrans Encroachment Permits ? (if needed) will secure within 5 months of grant award. (Unknow if will be needed due to CA Department of Technology?s failure to release Shape files for State Middle Mile routes, thus don?t know which side of street interconnection will be present.) ·          State Water Resources Control Board ? (if needed) will secure within 5 months of grant award ·          Coast Commission (where required) ? will secure permit within 6 months of grant award 
 Milestone 3: Bid Package Release/Contractor Selection ·          Bid packages will be released prior to grant award and contractors selected ·          Once grant award confirmed, execute agreements with selected firms/individuals 
 Milestone 4: Construction ·          Assume 18-month buildout period, with one-third of build progressing every 6 months 
 Milestone 5: Drive Take-Rate/Customer Sign-Ups ·          Community marketing begins upon grant award and extends through construction period ·          Assume 2/3 of customers over 12 months starting in month 4, as network is lit, with equal monthly increases. Remaining 1/3 of customers over next 6-12 months

The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: ·          Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. ? 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

DescProposedBroadbandPlan: 1. Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) will construct and operate an open access , last mile , municipal , fiber network for communities in rural California.   GSCA?s network will be built and structured to integrate new technology as it is developed in the future, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and allowing for increased capabilities for the network and its customers. The network will be designed to be compatible with wireless connections.   Design/Architecture Technology GSCA?s broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet open access, fiber infrastructure . The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. ? 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: ·          Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location, with about 10% excess capacity for spares, growth, and other needs. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. location. ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection. Ethernet is the most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. All Ethernet devices can be expected to inter-operate and device costs are very low. PtP enables for a simpler design. 2. When the GSCA network is built, residents will have access to speeds up to 10 Gbps symmetrical and businesses will have access to up to 100 Gbps symmetrical. 3. Infrastructure deployment will be done via point-to-point horizontal directional boring. Directional boring is a trenchless method of installing fiber optic cable underground along a predetermined bore path. The directional drilling system allows for the placement of underground cable with minimal disturbance along the ground surface .   No CEQA areas of concern were determined to be present. GSCA presently anticipates that the proposed project areas will be eligible for the Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and/or Class 4 (Minor Alterations in the Condition of Land) categorical exemptions.   GSCA will serve as lead agency for this project for purposes of CEQA and will work closely with local government representatives to ensure that restoration is done in accordance with local requirements. 4. The proposed project areas included in the application have been designed to connect directly to the State Middle Mile project. 5. No middle mile facilities are included in the proposed project areas. 6. Not applicable ? no middle mile facilities included in the proposed project areas.

Company Applicant Name: Golden State Connect Authority
Contact Position: Chief Economic Development Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Golden State Connect Authority (GSCA) broadband network architecture will consist of Point to Point (PtP) Ethernet, open access, fiber infrastructure. Ethernet infrastructure provides dedicated capacity for every home/business; easily upgradable to 10X speed, supports Enterprise call services; supports open access; and, is vendor neutral.   Point to Point (PtP)/Ethernet Design The design consists of a built network with a dedicated fiber strand end-to-end from a fiber hut to each end-user location. The strands will be in cables of varying sizes. The speed is infinite and is determined by the electronics on the end of each fiber span. The minimum installed connection is 1 Gbps, but any location in communities served by GSCA will be capable of 10 Gbps or even 100 Gbps.   PtP/Ethernet is preferred for open access because: ·          Each subscriber has a dedicated fiber connection - not shared with adjacent subscribers or across multiple providers. Delivers more reliable connectivity and better performance - lower latency and lower jitter. Most widely deployed and supported network technology in the world. Ensures a future-proof deployment.   Open Access GSCA will own and operate a signal fiber infrastructure that private service providers utilize to deliver services to their subscribers. Open-access fiber can support various competing providers of Internet, metro-Ethernet, phone, video, security, telemedicine, and other services.   GSCA?s Operational Partner, UTOPIA Fiber, has developed unique processes, tools, and protections to enable providers to co-exist on open access system, thus enabling providers to view their own subscribers, device information, troubleshooting information, etc., without interfering with or having access to that of subscribers of other providers.   Fiber Huts GSCA?s broadband network will utilize walk-in fiber huts instead of cabinets. Fiber huts provide: ·          Better insulation, protection, and security of equipment Better for making connections, repairs, and performing maintenance during inclement weather Protected from vehicular damage or vandalism Support more battery capacity and stand-by generators to run indefinitely in the event of extended power outages Support redundant cooling units for greater reliability Support instant/automatic failover to redundant fiber routes (all huts connected via multiple fiber paths) Provides facility to support smart city applications such as air-quality sensors, weather sensors, etc.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: Golden State Connect Authority network deployment will consist principally of the traditional underground conduit/fiber build design placed entirely within existing disturbed rights-of-way via point to point horizontal directional boring. This is the preferred and best long-term approach to fiber construction from a public agency perspective.   Aerial installation will take place only in those cases where underground installation is not practicable given geological impediments (granite subsurface) or where there is no available easement for utility crossings.   As Low Level Network Design plans are completed for GSCA proposed FFA/Last Mile project areas in the coming months, the locations where undergrounding will not be possible will be definitively identified and communicated. It is anticipated that these cases will be rare.   At present, it is anticipated that 100% of the project (by length) will be underground fiber.

Contact Position: Chief Executive Officer
Description of the major infrastructure: Placement of vaults and handholds such that all addresses in service area can be served with fiber based internet services at the committed service speeds.

Major Equipment Expenses: 1,955,952.32 (Includes $144,950 of Construction Equipment Rental over 18 Months)
Estimated project plan with major milest:
The following schedule is proposed with the assumption that weather is amenable for year round construction (Climate Change has forced us to be less optimistic about projecting future construction schedules and permitting will be a four week timeline. 
 Lastly, the time line project is based on winning the approval for Last Mile funding and the first tranche of that funding. Permitting and Construction Milestones Projection: Receiving Last Mile funding approval  Begin detailed engineering, one month 17 months remaining Prepare and submit to CPUC Environmental Assessment for CEQA exemption-one week Receive Notice to Proceed with Construction-3 weeks, we are now two months in, 16 months remaining We expect the first tranche of Last Mile Funding at this point of the projection Submit engineering plans for encroachment permit from City of Pixley-4 week process, we now have 15 months remaining During the Pixley municipal encroachment permit process we will secure pole agreements with the pole owner to route aerial fiber cables and secure a construction yard near the job rented on a temporary basis-no additional time allocated here as this work will be accommodated during the engineering  Order and receive cable, vaults, telecom equipment during this period Mobilize crew and equipment and begin construction and begin community outreach and partnerships with local governments, community based organizations, library, and school district-4 weeks, 14 months remaining Complete aerial construction, coordination with the community continues, 12 weeks, 11 months remaining We expect the second tranche of Last Mile funding here. Complete underground construction 16 weeks, 9 months remaining Splicing and testing system 12 weeks, 6 months remaining We expect the third tranche of Last Mile Funding at this time period Connect to the State of California Middle Mile system obtain internet, set up IP equipment and or build to temporary backbone infrastructure while waiting for the State of California Middle Mile system to be completed-8 weeks, 4 months remaining Begin to connect to initial customers and continue community marketing and customer connection, 8 weeks, 2 months remaining Continue to market and connect customers to meet customer projected goal of 900 accounts, 12 weeks, construction project is completed marketing continues IP services continue, 8 weeks, time is up?.hooray, success! Final tranche of Last Mile Funding expected at the end of successful construction and final testing.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan:
The Pixley Fiber to the Homes and Businesses Project (PFTTH&B) will use fiber, hubs, GPON technology capable of carrying 100 mp up and downloads for community wide fiber connectivity. GPON stands for Gigabit Passive Optical Networks. A GPON network includes shared network equipment in the central office (OLT) and a dedicated optical unit at each subscriber location (ONU/ONT). In the middle it's all fiber. GPON allows us to consolidate multiple services onto a single fiber transport network if required. 
 All construction will be accomplished within the public utility easement (PUE). As a Competitive Local Exchange Carrier (CLEC) licensed in the State of California (CPUC #U7229C) we are required to file an environmental assessment and expect to receive a ?Notice to Proceed with Construction? from the CPUC after review of our engineering plans and confirmation that we are building within the PUE. By following these requirements, we are CEQA exempt. All underground construction bore pits, pot holes, and vaults/hand-holds will be restored per the municipal encroachment permit requirements of the City of Pixley. Existing utility poles are planned to be used for 95% of the proposed route. Our CLEC license will allow us to enter into a pole access agreement and even join a join pole authority if necessary. Access to the State of California Middle Mile fiber for backhaul is critical to save construction funding. The State of California Middle Mile infrastructure is slated to be built adjacent to Highway 99 in Tulare County where the City of Pixley has been established. Without the State of California Middle Mile access we would have to spend part of the Last Mile funding to build a specific back haul route in order to connect to data centers and bring distributed FTTH&B to each address in Pixley, CA.  
 Additionally, our company intends to continue to serve high speed internet to the community of Pixley and we are thus willing to and fully accept the State?s SB 156 one access requirements for any Middle Mile facilities in the Pixley, CA proposed project area.

Contact Position: Director - Regulatory External and Legislative Affairs
Description of the major infrastructure: AT&T proposes construction of a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) network. AT&T will deploy approximately 26.6 Miles of fiber, in two central offices in Lakeside/San Diego County. Because this is a fiber-based network, there are no remote terminals.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 70% Aerial / 30% Underground.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. No Middle Mile funding is being requested. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing Rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: AT&T will deploy a passive optical fiber network using 10 Gigabit per second Symmetrical Passive Optical Network (XGS-PON) technology with a Fiber-to-the-Premises (FTTP) architecture and upgrade its existing FTTP network, which serves Tiers 1-3 customer locations, to XGS-PON. An XGS-PON network operates at 10 Gbps symmetric and AT&T proposes to offer up to 5 Gbps broadband services to residential consumers. AT&T will deploy XGS-PON/FTTP to both existing and new construction customer locations. Aerial fiber will be placed along existing conduit and utility poles. Underground (buried) fiber will be placed in existing rights-of-way, or within private utility easements, on a limited basis. In areas where trenching is performed, the site will be restored by industry-accepted practices. To the extent possible, AT&T will leverage any existing available facilities in the project area including central office equipment, and other deployed facilities. With AT&T?s FTTP project, the eligible households will be able to choose among AT&T products with speeds up to 5 Gbps download and 5 Gbps upload. Consumers will also be able to elect services at 300/300 Mbps, 500/500 Mbps, 1000/1000 Mbps, 2000/2000 Mbps, or 5000/5000 Mbps and qualifying customers will be eligible for Access from AT&T Internet with speeds of 100/100 Mbps. AT&T believes this last-mile project is categorically exempt from CEQA as the fiber to be deployed that will be strung on existing poles is categorically exempt as Class 1, which applies to modifications to existing facilities. 14 C.C.R. § 15301. Any new areas of aerial fiber or buried fiber is categorically exempt under Class 3, which applies to the construction, installation, or conversion of a limited number of small facilities, structures, or equipment. 14 C.C.R. § 15303(d); see San Francisco Beautiful v. City and County of San Francisco (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1012 (court held that the installation of 726 above ground telecommunications cabinets and associated trenching and fiber throughout San Francisco was exempt under Class 3). All of the work is also within the Class 4 categorical exemption as Minor Alterations to Land. 14 C.C.R. § 15304. AT&T will overbuild its existing facilities in the proposed project area. This includes using any existing poles and/or right-of-way that are available. AT&T will not be requesting funding for middle-mile facilities.

Application: Project Name: San Luis Obispo Affordable Broadband Network
Company Applicant Name: City of San Luis Obispo
Contact Position: Information Technology Manager
Project Title: San Luis Obispo Affordable Broadband Network
Description of the major infrastructure: The project will deploy fiber optic cable to improve backbone fiber optic connections as well as distribution within neighborhoods, and last mile drops.
Estimated Breakdown of Aerial: 68% by length of new aerial and 32% by length of new underground fiber.
Major Equipment Expenses: Major equipment expenses will total $1,112,025. This includes: Edge Routing- $160,000 Core Routing - $250,000 Switching - $22,500 Access Network - $75,000 Spares - $25,000 ONU - $51,450 NID enclosure - $7,350 Residential Gateway - $25,725
Estimated project plan with major milest: The proposed project will build out fiber optic cable throughout areas of the City of San Luis Obispo to provide service to unserved locations. This will include building additional portions of fiber backbone as well as last mile service drops. Assuming a December 2023 grant award, the project should be completed by June 2025, and the network launched in the summer of 2025.   ·       Design and Engineering completed by June 2024. ·       CEQA (exemption) completed by June 2024. ·       Local agency permitting completed by December 2024. ·       Construction bid awarded before December 2024. ·       Construction of backbone, network connections, and service drop installation by June 2025. ·       Project completion June 2025.
DescProposedBroadbandPlan: The proposed project will provide broadband access to unserved sites through the use of fiber optic technology. This will include building additional portions of fiber backbone within the City of San Luis Obispo, as well as service drops to unserved locations. Upload and download speeds of 1 Gbps symmetric will be supported initially, with the ability to serve higher upload and download speeds in the future as needs change. The project should qualify for a Class 1 exemption from the CEQA process, as any new construction follows existing roadways and public right of way, which already contains public utility infrastructure. Additionally, there are no environmentally sensitive areas within the project area. The project will leverage existing public and private conduit and fiber to facilitate rapid expansion into unserved locations. The project does not include building any middle-mile infrastructure.
